# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **chain-growth** and **step-growth** polymerization. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Chain-Growth | Step-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **Active species** | Reactive center at chain end (radical/ion) | No chain end; any two functional groups react |
| **Monomer type** | Unsaturated/cyclic | Bifunctional (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) |
| **By-product** | Usually none | Small molecule (H₂O, HCl) |
| **MW buildup** | Fast, monomer consumed early | Slow, gradual |
| **Example** | Polyethylene from ethylene | Nylon-6,6 from adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine |

*(1 mark per correct contrasting pair, 1 mark for examples)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **Q2.** What are **natural polymers**? Give **two examples** with their monomers and one engineering application each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Natural polymers are macromolecules synthesized by living organisms (plants, animals, microbes) via enzyme-catalyzed polymerization of biological monomers.

**Example 1: Cellulose**
- Monomer: β-D-glucose
- Engineering application: Paper manufacturing, textile fibers

**Example 2: Natural Rubber (Polyisoprene)**
- Monomer: Isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene)
- Engineering application: Vehicle tyres after vulcanization

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Question A (Module 1, 14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of free-radical chain-growth polymerization** in detail, covering **initiation, propagation, and termination** stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Carothers equation** for step-growth polymerization. A polymerization reaction has reached an extent of reaction $p = 0.98$. Calculate the **number-average degree of polymerization** and **number-average molecular weight**, given monomer molecular weight $M_0 = 113$ g/mol. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Free-Radical Polymerization Mechanism (7 Marks)

**Initiation (2 marks):**
The initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes under heat to generate free radicals:

$$\text{(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO)}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^{\bullet}$$

The radical adds to a monomer's double bond, forming a new radical — the **active chain end**:

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CHX} \longrightarrow \text{R-CH}_2\text{-}\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}\text{HX}$$

**Propagation (2 marks):**
The active chain end successively adds monomer units:

$$\text{R-}[\text{CH}_2\text{-CHX}]_n^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CHX} \xrightarrow{k_p} \text{R-}[\text{CH}_2\text{-CHX}]_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

The chain grows rapidly (typically microseconds), with the radical always at the terminal carbon.

**Termination (2 marks):**
Two active chains terminate by:
- **Coupling:** $\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{M}_{n+m}$
- **Disproportionation:** $\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{M}_n\text{(saturated)} + \text{M}_m\text{(unsaturated)}$

**Chain transfer (1 mark):**
The active radical may transfer to solvent/monomer/polymer, but the original chain terminates and a new active species forms.

---

#### Part (b) — Carothers Equation Derivation + Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules, $N$ = number of molecules remaining at time $t$.

Extent of reaction:

$$p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

Number-average degree of polymerization:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

From the first equation: $N = N_0(1 - p)$.

Substituting:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1 - p)} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $p = 0.98$, $M_0 = 113$ g/mol.

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50$$

$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \times M_0 = 50 \times 113 = 5650 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_n = 50, \quad \overline{M}_n = 5650 \text{ g/mol}}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Question B (Module 1, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** Classify **polymers based on their source**. Discuss the structural features, properties, and engineering applications of **natural rubber** and **cellulose**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stoichiometrically imbalanced step-growth polymerization has $r = 0.95$ and $p = 0.90$. Using the **modified Carothers equation**, calculate $\overline{X}_n$ and comment on whether the resulting polymer is suitable for **fiber spinning applications**. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Polymer Classification + Natural Polymers (7 Marks)

**Classification (2 marks):**
Polymers are classified by source as:
- **Natural** — synthesized by living organisms
- **Synthetic** — prepared industrially
- **Semi-synthetic** — chemically modified natural polymers (e.g., cellulose acetate, vulcanized rubber)

**Natural Rubber (2.5 marks):**
- Monomer: Isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene)
- Polymer: cis-1,4-polyisoprene
- Structure: $\text{-(CH}_2\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)=CH-CH}_2\text{)}_n-$
- Property: Amorphous at room temperature, soft and tacky, low tensile strength
- Engineering application: After **vulcanization** (crosslinking with sulfur, 1879 — Charles Goodyear), used in vehicle tyres, conveyor belts, vibration dampers, surgical gloves

**Cellulose (2.5 marks):**
- Monomer: β-D-glucose linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds
- Structure: Linear chains that form strong intra/intermolecular hydrogen bonds
- Property: High crystallinity, insoluble in water, high tensile strength
- Engineering application: Paper, textiles (cotton), cellophane films, cellulose nitrate (early plastics), carboxymethyl cellulose (viscosity modifier in paints)

---

#### Part (b) — Modified Carothers Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $r = 0.95$, $p = 0.90$, $M_0$ assumed = 113 g/mol (or stated in question).

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

**Substitution (3 marks):**

Numerator: $1 + r = 1 + 0.95 = 1.95$

Denominator term: $2rp = 2 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 1.71$

Denominator: $1 + 0.95 - 1.71 = 1.95 - 1.71 = 0.24$

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1.95}{0.24} = 8.125$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):**

$$\overline{X}_n \approx 8.1, \quad \overline{M}_n \approx 8.1 \times 113 \approx 916 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Comment:** This molecular weight is far below the **industrial fiber-grade requirement of $\overline{M}_n > 15,000$ g/mol**. The polymer is therefore an **oligomer** and **not suitable for fiber spinning**. Higher $\overline{X}_n$ can only be achieved by (i) using more equimolar monomer feed ($r \to 1$) and (ii) pushing the extent of reaction closer to unity ($p > 0.999$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $\overline{X}_n$ and $\overline{X}_w$:** Students often write $\overline{X}_n = (1+p)/(1-p)$ for step-growth — this is **WRONG**. $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ is the **number-average**; $(1+p)/(1-p)$ is the **weight-average**.
> 2. **Forgetting initiator efficiency $f$:** In the $R_p$ equation, students write $R_p = k_p \vert \text{M}\vert \sqrt{k_d \vert \text{I}\vert / k_t}$, missing the factor $\sqrt{f}$. This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up free-radical and ionic mechanisms:** Cationic polymerization requires electron-donating substituents (e.g., isobutylene); anionic requires electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., styrene, acrylonitrile). Mismatch = 0 marks.
> 4. **Giving only one example of natural polymer:** KTU expects **at least two** distinct classes (e.g., polysaccharide + protein) — one example from each.
> 5. **Numerical errors in Carothers equation:** Forgetting that $r \leq 1$ by definition; sometimes students take $r = N_B/N_A$ and get the inverted equation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Modes of Polymerization**

- [x] **Two principal modes:** Chain-growth (addition) and Step-growth (condensation).
- [x] **Chain-growth requires:** initiator, active center (radical/cation/anion), and an unsaturated or cyclic monomer.
- [x] **Step-growth requires:** bifunctional monomers, releases small molecule by-product.
- [x] **Carothers equation:** $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ — valid for **equimolar** step-growth.
- [x] **Modified Carothers:** $\overline{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ — for **non-equimolar** feed.
- [x] **Carothers paradox:** To reach DP = 100, need $p = 0.99$; to reach DP = 1000, need $p = 0.999$ — *tiny* differences in conversion cause *huge* differences in MW.
- [x] **Free-radical rate equation:** $R_p = k_p \vert \text{M}\vert \sqrt{f k_d \vert \text{I}\vert / k_t}$.
- [x] **Kinetic chain length:** depends on $k_p / \sqrt{k_d k_t}$ and inversely on $\sqrt{\vert \text{I}\vert}$.
- [x] **Natural polymers:** Cellulose, starch, natural rubber, silk, wool, chitin, collagen, DNA.
- [x] **Synthetic polymer classes:** Thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers, fibers.
- [x] **Thermoplastic** = linear, melts on heating, reprocessable (PE, PP, PVC, PS).
- [x] **Thermoset** = crosslinked network, does not melt, char on heating (Bakelite, Epoxy).
- [x] **Elastomer** = lightly crosslinked, high elastic recovery (Natural rubber, SBR, Silicone).
- [x] **Fiber** = highly oriented crystalline, high tensile strength (Nylon-6,6, PET, Kevlar).
- [x] **Ziegler-Natta catalyst** = TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃ — enables stereoregular coordination polymerization (HDPE, isotactic PP).
- [x] **Living polymerization** = no termination/chain transfer → narrow PDI (≈1.0) — used for block copolymers.
- [x] **KTU memory trigger:** "**C**hain grows at the **C**ap (chain end); **S**tep-growth has **S**everal species reacting at any time."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermoplastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> **Thermoplastics** (also called *thermosoftening plastics*) are a class of polymeric materials composed of **linear or lightly branched macromolecular chains** held together by relatively **weak secondary intermolecular forces** (van der Waals forces, dipole-dipole interactions, and weak hydrogen bonding). Upon the application of heat, these secondary bonds weaken sufficiently, allowing the chains to slide past one another and the material to flow, mold, and reshape. Upon cooling, the secondary bonds reform, restoring rigidity. This **physical transformation is fully reversible** and can be repeated without significant chemical degradation, making thermoplastics inherently **recyclable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The course outcome *CO1* of OECHT612 emphasizes identifying polymer classes and their structure-property relationships. Thermoplastics are the **largest commercially consumed class of polymers**, accounting for nearly **85%** of total global polymer production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine a bowl of **uncooked spaghetti noodles** piled together in a tangled heap:
- The individual noodles represent the long polymer chains.
- The mild friction between noodles (when dry) represents the weak intermolecular forces.
- When you add hot water (heat), the noodles become slippery and slide past each other easily → **the polymer softens and flows**.
- When the water cools, the noodles stick together again, forming a solid mass → **the polymer hardens**.
- You can reheat the same noodles as many times as you want → **the process is reversible**.

A more relatable industrial analogy: **candle wax / butter / chocolate** — these soften when warmed and re-solidify when cooled, behaving exactly like a thermoplastic in macroscopic terms.

## 1.3 Fundamental Structural Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Thermoplastics Behave This Way (The Core "Why"):**
> Because the chains are not covalently cross-linked, the energy required to overcome secondary bonds is relatively low (typically $\Delta H \approx 5\text{–}50 \; \text{kJ/mol}$, much lower than the covalent C–C bond energy of $\approx 350 \; \text{kJ/mol}$). This is why thermal processing occurs at moderate temperatures ($150\text{–}400^\circ C$).

| Feature | Thermoplastic Behavior |
|---------|------------------------|
| Chain topology | Linear or branched (no cross-links) |
| Inter-chain bonding | Weak secondary forces |
| Effect of heat | Softens → flows → re-solidifies |
| Reversibility | **Fully reversible** |
| Solubility | Soluble in selected organic solvents |
| Recyclability | **High** (can be remelted) |
| Mechanical behavior | Generally tough, ductile, fracture-resistant |

## 1.4 General Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** $80\text{–}150^\circ C$ (typical range for amorphous thermoplastics)
- **Melting Temperature ($T_m$):** $100\text{–}350^\circ C$ (for semi-crystalline grades)
- **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT):** $50\text{–}280^\circ C$ under **0.45 MPa** load
- **Density range:** $0.85\text{–}1.55 \; \text{g/cm}^3$
- **Tensile Strength:** $10\text{–}150 \; \text{MPa}$
- **Degree of Polymerization ($DP$):** $10^3 \text{–} 10^5$ repeat units
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Classification of Thermoplastics

Thermoplastics are structurally classified into two major categories based on chain packing behavior:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus Point:**
> Questions on *amorphous vs. semi-crystalline thermoplastics* appear very frequently in KTU exams. Memorize the examples and the property contrast.

### (a) Amorphous Thermoplastics
- Chains are arranged in a **disordered, random coil** configuration.
- No sharp melting point — they exhibit a **glass transition** ($T_g$).
- Optical transparency, isotropic properties, low shrinkage on cooling.
- **Examples:** Polystyrene (PS), Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), Polycarbonate (PC), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC - unplasticized), ABS.

### (b) Semi-Crystalline Thermoplastics
- Chains are partly ordered into **crystalline lamellae** interspersed with amorphous regions.
- Exhibit both a **melting point** ($T_m$) and a **glass transition** ($T_g$), where $T_m > T_g$.
- Higher strength, chemical resistance, and density compared to amorphous analogues.
- **Examples:** Polyethylene (PE - HDPE/LDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyamide (Nylon 6, 66), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), Polyoxymethylene (POM).

## 2.2 Commercial/Engineering Classification

| Category | Examples | Typical Engineering Use |
|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Commodity Thermoplastics** | PE, PP, PS, PVC, PET | Packaging, containers, pipes, household goods |
| **Engineering Thermoplastics** | PA (Nylon), PC, POM, PBT | Gears, bearings, automotive, electrical |
| **High-Performance Thermoplastics** | PEEK, PPS, LCP, PEI, PI | Aerospace, semiconductor, medical implants |

## 2.3 The Two Critical Thermal Transitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transition temperatures $T_g$ and $T_m$ are the **two single most important properties** of a thermoplastic in design and processing. The KTU exam tests these every year.

### Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)
The temperature at which the amorphous regions of a polymer transition from a **rigid, glassy state** to a **rubbery, flexible state**. Below $T_g$, segmental motion is frozen; above $T_g$, chain segments gain mobility.

### Melting Temperature ($T_m$)
The temperature at which the **crystalline regions** of a semi-crystalline polymer lose long-range order and the material becomes a viscous liquid.

### Relationship for Semi-Crystalline Polymers
For most thermoplastics, an empirical relationship holds:
$$T_g \approx \left(\frac{2}{3}\right) T_m \;\; \text{(in Kelvin)}$$

This ratio is a direct consequence of the Bovey–Boyer rule and reflects the cooperative nature of segmental motion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU University Exam – Module 1**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Use |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $T_g \approx \left(\frac{2}{3}\right) T_m$ | $T_g, T_m$ in Kelvin | Estimate missing transition temperature |
| 2 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ (Fox–Flory) | $T_{g,\infty}$ = $T_g$ at infinite $M_n$, $K$ = constant, $M_n$ = number-average MW | Effect of molecular weight on $T_g$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ (Fox equation) | $w_i$ = weight fraction of component $i$ | Predict $T_g$ of polymer blends/copolymers |
| 4 | $\%\text{Crystallinity} = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$ | $\Delta H_f$ = sample heat of fusion, $\Delta H_f^0$ = 100% crystalline heat of fusion (DSC) | Quantify crystallinity from DSC |
| 5 | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | Chain length characterization |
| 6 | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | Molecular weight distribution |
| 7 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Polydispersity Index | Distribution breadth |
| 8 | $\eta_0 = K M_w^{3.4}$ (for $M_w > M_c$) | Zero-shear viscosity, $M_c$ = critical entanglement MW | Melt viscosity vs. molecular weight |
| 9 | $T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \, l}\right)$ (Gibbs–Thomson) | $T_m^0$ = equilibrium $T_m$, $\sigma_e$ = fold surface energy, $l$ = lamellar thickness | Effect of crystallite size on $T_m$ |
| 10 | $\rho = w_c \rho_c + (1 - w_c)\rho_a$ | Crystallinity by density, $w_c$ = crystalline weight fraction | Crystallinity from density |

> **Engineering Utility of the Formula Sheet:**
> - Equations **1–3** are essential for predicting **processing windows** (molding temperatures).
> - Equation **8** is fundamental for **injection molding machine** selection (screw tonnage, clamp force).
> - Equations **4 & 10** are used in **Quality Control laboratories** to certify polymer batches.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Note:**
> - The Coca-Cola bottle is a thermoplastic (PET) — it can be remelted and re-extruded.
> - LEGO bricks are made of **ABS** (an amorphous engineering thermoplastic) chosen for its dimensional stability and impact strength.
> - Automotive bumpers use **polypropylene copolymers** for their toughness and recyclability.
> - Aircraft cabin components increasingly use **PEEK** to replace metal due to its $T_g \approx 143^\circ C$ and excellent flame retardancy.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effect of Molecular Weight on $T_g$ (Fox–Flory Equation)

The Fox–Flory equation is a **classic KTU exam derivation** and must be derived step by step.

### Statement
As the number-average molecular weight $M_n$ decreases, chain ends become a significant fraction of the total volume, creating "free volume" that enhances segmental mobility. Therefore, $T_g$ decreases with decreasing $M_n$.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Define free volume per gram of polymer.**
The total free volume $V_f$ at temperature $T$ is the excess volume beyond the occupied volume. For a polymer, the free volume is contributed by:
- (i) Thermal expansion free volume
- (ii) Chain-end free volume (because chain ends are less constrained)

**Step 2: Express the chain-end free volume contribution.**
Let $\theta$ be the excess free volume per chain end per gram of polymer. For a polymer of number-average molecular weight $M_n$, the number of chain ends per gram is $\dfrac{2 N_A}{M_n}$ (factor of 2 because each chain has two ends).

The chain-end contribution to free volume is:
$$V_{f,\text{end}} = \frac{2 \theta N_A}{M_n}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Doolittle free-volume equation for viscosity.**
The viscosity $\eta$ is related to free volume fraction $f$ by:
$$\eta = A \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

**Step 4: At the glass transition, the free volume fraction $f_g$ is constant (universal value $\approx 0.025$).**
Setting $f = f_g$ at $T = T_g$ for two polymers of different $M_n$ and matching the free volume expressions, we obtain the linear relationship between $1/M_n$ and $T_g$.

**Step 5: Final result (Fox–Flory equation).**
$$\boxed{T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}}$$

where:
- $T_{g,\infty}$ is the asymptotic $T_g$ at infinite molecular weight (chain-end effects vanish).
- $K$ is the **Fox–Flory constant** (typical values: $K \approx 2 \times 10^5 \; \text{K·g/mol}$ for PS).

### Physical Interpretation
- As $M_n \to \infty$, $T_g \to T_{g,\infty}$ (maximum, intrinsic property).
- As $M_n$ decreases, $T_g$ drops linearly with $1/M_n$ — short chains have more ends, more free volume, and lower $T_g$.
- This explains why **plasticizers** (low-MW additives) reduce $T_g$ in flexible PVC.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: $T_g$ of a Miscible Polymer Blend (Fox Equation)

For a miscible blend of two polymers (1) and (2), the Fox equation (1965) gives the blend $T_g$:

**Step 1: Define weight fractions.**
$w_1$ = weight fraction of polymer 1, $w_2 = 1 - w_1$ = weight fraction of polymer 2.

**Step 2: Assume additivity of free volume at $T_g$.**
The total free volume at the blend's $T_g$ is the sum of contributions from each component at the same temperature $T_g$:

**Step 3: Using the inverse proportionality of $T_g$ to free volume:**
$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

**Final Result (Fox Equation):**
$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}}$$

> **Note:** All temperatures are in **Kelvin**.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A blend contains 60 wt% Polystyrene ($T_{g,1} = 373 \; K$) and 40 wt% Polybutadiene ($T_{g,2} = 188 \; K$). Predict the blend $T_g$.

**Solution:**

Using the Fox equation:
$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.60}{373} + \frac{0.40}{188}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 0.001608 + 0.002128 = 0.003736 \; K^{-1}$$

$$T_g = \frac{1}{0.003736} = 267.67 \; K = -5.48^\circ C$$

This is a high-impact polystyrene (HIPS) type system, where the rubbery polybutadiene domains toughen the brittle PS matrix. The single, intermediate $T_g$ confirms miscibility at the segmental level.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Crystallinity from DSC Data

The degree of crystallinity $X_c$ of a semi-crystalline thermoplastic can be computed from a Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) heating curve:

$$X_c (\%) = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$$

**Derivation logic:**
The total heat absorbed in melting the crystalline regions of a sample of mass $m$ is:
$$Q = m \cdot \Delta H_f$$

If the sample were 100% crystalline, the heat would be:
$$Q_{\text{ideal}} = m \cdot \Delta H_f^0$$

The crystallinity is the ratio of the actual heat absorbed to the ideal heat of a perfect crystal:
$$X_c = \frac{Q}{Q_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0}$$

**Reference values of $\Delta H_f^0$ (J/g):**

| Polymer | $\Delta H_f^0$ (J/g) |
|---------|----------------------|
| PE (100% crystalline) | **293** |
| PP (100% isotactic) | **207** |
| PET (100% crystalline) | **140** |
| Nylon 6,6 (100% crystalline) | **196** |
| POM | **326** |

### Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A sample of HDPE shows a melting endotherm with $\Delta H_f = 175 \; J/g$ in a DSC run. Calculate the percent crystallinity.

**Solution:**
$$X_c = \frac{175}{293} \times 100 = 59.7\%$$

This is consistent with typical HDPE crystallinity in the range **50–70%**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effect of Lamellar Thickness on Melting Point (Gibbs–Thomson)

The melting point of a polymer crystal depends on the lamellar thickness $l$ (which itself depends on the crystallization conditions — undercooling, time, annealing):

$$T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \frac{2 \sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot l}\right)$$

**Derivation logic:**
For a crystal of finite size, the surface atoms have higher energy than bulk atoms due to dangling bonds at the fold surface. This excess surface energy $\sigma_e$ (J/m²) lowers the thermodynamic stability of the crystal, reducing $T_m$.

**Engineering significance:**
- Annealing (heating below $T_m^0$) increases $l$, raising $T_m$.
- Rapid quenching produces thin lamellae, lowering the observed $T_m$.
- This is why the "melting range" of a polymer depends on its **thermal history**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Thermoplastics vs. Thermosets

> [!NOTE]
> **Most Repeated KTU Comparison Question.**

| Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosets |
|----------|----------------|------------|
| Chain structure | Linear / branched | 3-D cross-linked network |
| Bonding | Weak secondary forces | Strong covalent cross-links |
| Effect of heat | Softens, melts, reversible | Decomposes, irreversible |
| Recyclability | **Yes (high)** | **No (very low)** |
| Solubility | Soluble in organic solvents | Insoluble, infusible |
| Processing | Injection, extrusion, blow molding | Compression molding, casting, reaction injection |
| Mechanical | Tough, ductile, impact-resistant | Hard, brittle, high modulus |
| Examples | PE, PP, PVC, PS, PA, PC | Phenol-formaldehyde, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine |
| Curing step | **Not required** | **Required (irreversible cross-linking)** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define thermoplastics. Give any four examples.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Thermoplastics are a class of polymeric materials that soften and flow upon heating and harden upon cooling, with the process being fully reversible. The linear or branched chains are held together by weak secondary intermolecular forces.

**Examples (any four):**
1. Polyethylene (PE) — used in plastic bags.
2. Polypropylene (PP) — used in food containers.
3. Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) — used in pipes.
4. Polystyrene (PS) — used in disposable cups.
5. Nylon (PA) — used in gears and textiles.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between amorphous and semi-crystalline thermoplastics with examples.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Amorphous | Semi-Crystalline |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| Chain packing | Disordered, random | Orderly lamellae + amorphous regions |
| Thermal transition | Only $T_g$ | Both $T_m$ and $T_g$ |
| Transparency | Transparent | Opaque/translucent |
| Shrinkage on cooling | Low | Higher (due to crystallization) |
| Chemical resistance | Lower | Higher |
| Examples | PS, PMMA, PC, ABS | PE, PP, PA, PET, POM |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts of **7 marks + 7 marks**. The model answers below are exhaustive and written to **the KTU Board valuation key standard**.

---

### **QUESTION A: 14 Marks** *(Module 1 Internal Choice — Option 1)*
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Set A]**

**Q(a)** Define thermoplastics. Explain the structural features that make thermoplastics processable on heating. Discuss any two engineering applications. *(7 marks)*

**Q(b)** Define the terms $T_g$ (glass transition temperature) and $T_m$ (melting temperature). Explain the molecular motion that occurs at $T_g$. Derive the empirical relationship $T_g \approx \frac{2}{3} T_m$ for a semi-crystalline polymer. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer for Q A(a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Thermoplastics are polymers that can be repeatedly softened upon heating and hardened upon cooling. The softening-hardening cycle is fully reversible, allowing reprocessing and recycling.

**Structural Features (3 marks):**
1. **Linear or lightly branched chain architecture:** There is no covalent cross-linking between chains. This permits chain segments to move past each other under the influence of heat.
2. **Weak secondary intermolecular forces:** The chains are held together by van der Waals forces, dipole-dipole interactions, and weak hydrogen bonding. These have bond energies of $5\text{–}50 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ compared to covalent C–C bond energy of $350 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. Hence a moderate amount of heat ($150\text{–}400^\circ C$) is sufficient to overcome them.
3. **Reversibility:** Upon cooling, the secondary forces reform, restoring rigidity. No chemical change occurs.
4. **Solubility in selective solvents:** Confirms the absence of cross-links.

**Engineering Applications (2 marks):**
1. **PVC pipes in plumbing and drainage:** PVC's corrosion resistance, low cost, and ease of extrusion make it the world's most-used piping material.
2. **PET beverage bottles:** PET is blow-molded into transparent, food-safe, lightweight, and shatter-resistant bottles. Its glass transition ($T_g \approx 70^\circ C$) and melting point ($T_m \approx 260^\circ C$) permit blow molding between these temperatures.

---

### **Model Answer for Q A(b):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **$T_g$ (Glass Transition Temperature):** The temperature at which an amorphous polymer (or the amorphous regions of a semi-crystalline polymer) transitions from a rigid, glassy state to a rubbery, flexible state.
- **$T_m$ (Melting Temperature):** The temperature at which the crystalline regions of a polymer lose long-range order and the material becomes a viscous liquid.

**Molecular Motion at $T_g$ (3 marks):**
Below $T_g$, only vibrational motions of atoms around their equilibrium positions are possible. The chain segments (typically 10–20 backbone atoms) are essentially frozen. As temperature approaches $T_g$, sufficient thermal energy (typically $k_B T \sim 0.1 \; \text{eV}$) becomes available to overcome the secondary intermolecular forces that lock the segments in place. **Cooperative segmental motion** is initiated: groups of 10–20 backbone atoms begin to rotate and translate. The free volume increases abruptly. The polymer is now rubbery above $T_g$.

**Derivation of $T_g \approx \frac{2}{3} T_m$ (2 marks):**

For most semi-crystalline thermoplastics, the entropy of melting $\Delta S_m$ is approximately constant ($\Delta S_m \approx 8\text{–}12 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$ per mole of repeat units). Similarly, the entropy change at the glass transition is $\Delta S_g \approx 0.5\text{–}0.7 \; \Delta S_m$.

By the thermodynamic definition of melting and glass transition, and applying the second law, the Bovey–Boyer empirical rule emerges:
$$T_g \approx \left(\frac{2}{3}\right) T_m \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}$$

For example, for polyethylene: $T_m = 414 \; K$, $T_g \approx 250 \; K$ (closely matches $T_m \times 2/3 = 276 \; K$).

---

### **QUESTION B: 14 Marks** *(Module 1 Internal Choice — Option 2)*
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]**

**Q(a)** Classify thermoplastics based on (i) commercial usage and (ii) crystallinity. List at least two examples in each sub-class and explain one key property of each class. *(7 marks)*

**Q(b)** Define the Fox–Flory equation and derive the expression showing the dependence of $T_g$ on molecular weight. A sample of polystyrene has $M_n = 1.0 \times 10^4 \; g/mol$ and a Fox–Flory constant $K = 1.8 \times 10^5 \; K \cdot g/mol$. If the asymptotic glass transition temperature $T_{g,\infty} = 373 \; K$, calculate the $T_g$ of the sample. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer for Q B(a):**

**Classification Based on Commercial Usage (4 marks):**

| Sub-Class | Examples | Key Property |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **Commodity Thermoplastics** (high volume, low cost) | PE (Polyethylene), PP (Polypropylene), PS (Polystyrene), PVC (Polyvinyl chloride) | Low cost, easy processing, used in packaging and household goods. Density 0.85–1.40 g/cm³. |
| **Engineering Thermoplastics** (high performance) | PA (Nylon), PC (Polycarbonate), POM (Acetal), PET, PBT | Higher strength, stiffness, thermal stability, chemical resistance. Used in automotive, electronics, gears. |
| **High-Performance / Specialty Thermoplastics** | PEEK, PPS, Polyimide (PI), LCP | Continuous service temperature above 200°C, excellent flame retardancy, low creep. Used in aerospace, semiconductor, medical. |

**Classification Based on Crystallinity (3 marks):**

| Sub-Class | Examples | Key Property |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **Amorphous Thermoplastics** | PS, PMMA, PC, ABS, uPVC | Transparent; sharp glass transition; isotropic; good dimensional stability; no melting point. |
| **Semi-Crystalline Thermoplastics** | HDPE, LDPE, PP, Nylon 6, Nylon 66, PET, POM, PTFE | Opaque; exhibit both $T_g$ and $T_m$; higher tensile strength; better chemical resistance; anisotropic shrinkage on cooling. |

---

### **Model Answer for Q B(b):**

**Fox–Flory Equation (2 marks):**
$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}$$

This equation gives the glass transition temperature of a polymer as a function of its number-average molecular weight $M_n$, with $T_{g,\infty}$ being the asymptotic value at infinite molecular weight and $K$ being the Fox–Flory constant (polymer-specific).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** The free volume $V_f$ per gram of polymer includes two contributions — thermal expansion and chain-end "excess free volume." Each chain has 2 chain ends. The number of chains per gram is $N_A / M_n$. Hence chain ends per gram is $2 N_A / M_n$.

**Step 2:** Let $\theta$ be the excess free volume per chain end. The total chain-end free volume is:
$$V_{f,\text{end}} = \frac{2 \theta N_A}{M_n}$$

**Step 3:** According to free-volume theory, the total free volume at the glass transition is a constant (universal value $f_g \approx 0.025$). Equating chain-end contributions for two molecular weights and simplifying, the linear relation between $T_g$ and $1/M_n$ is obtained:
$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $M_n = 1.0 \times 10^4 \; g/mol$, $K = 1.8 \times 10^5 \; K \cdot g/mol$, $T_{g,\infty} = 373 \; K$.

$$T_g = 373 - \frac{1.8 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^4}$$

$$T_g = 373 - 18 = 355 \; K$$

$$T_g = 355 \; K = 81.85^\circ C$$

The low-molecular-weight polystyrene has a $T_g$ approximately **18 K lower** than the high-molecular-weight polymer — confirming the depressant effect of chain ends on the glass transition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Module 1 Thermoplastics Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit Kelvin:** The Fox equation, Bovey–Boyer rule, and most $T_g$-$T_m$ relations require **Kelvin**, not Celsius. Marks are deducted if units are wrong.
> 2. **Writing Celsius and Kelvin interchangeably:** Always convert: $T(K) = T(^\circ C) + 273.15$.
> 3. **Confusing $T_g$ with $T_m$:** State clearly — $T_g$ is for amorphous regions (segmental motion), $T_m$ is for crystalline regions (loss of long-range order).
> 4. **Omitting the assumption** that entropy of melting is approximately constant when deriving the $2/3$ ratio. Examiners want this assumption stated.
> 5. **Wrong example for the polymer type:** e.g., writing "PVC" as semi-crystalline (it is amorphous in unplasticized form). Verify the polymer class.
> 6. **Skipping the structural "why":** A definition alone is 1 mark; explaining the weak intermolecular forces is the **remaining marks**.
> 7. **Numerical computation errors in Fox–Flory:** Watch decimal placement when $K / M_n$ is large.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Revision — KTU Module 1: Thermoplastics**

- **Core Definition:** Linear/branched polymers that soften on heating and re-solidify on cooling, **reversibly**.
- **Key Distinction from Thermosets:** No covalent cross-links → recyclable and remeltable.
- **Two Critical Transitions:** $T_g$ (glass transition — amorphous regions) and $T_m$ (melting — crystalline regions).
- **Bovey–Boyer Rule:** $T_g \approx \frac{2}{3} T_m$ (in Kelvin).
- **Fox–Flory Equation:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K / M_n$ — $T_g$ **increases** with molecular weight and **asymptotes** at high $M_n$.
- **Fox Equation (Blends):** $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ — all $T_g$ values in **Kelvin**.
- **Crystallinity Formula:** $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$.
- **Melt Viscosity Law:** $\eta_0 \propto M_w^{3.4}$ for $M_w > M_c$ — used in mold design.
- **Commercial Classification:** Commodity → Engineering → High-Performance.
- **Structural Classification:** Amorphous (PS, PMMA, PC, ABS) vs. Semi-Crystalline (PE, PP, PA, PET, POM).
- **Examples to Memorize:** PE (packaging), PP (automotive, carpets), PS (cups), PVC (pipes), PMMA (lenses), PC (CDs, helmets), Nylon (gears, textiles), PET (bottles), PTFE (non-stick coatings).
- **Processing Methods:** Injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming, rotational molding, 3D printing (FDM).
- **Gibbs–Thomson:** $T_m$ depends on lamellar thickness — annealing raises $T_m$.
- **Property Trends:**
  - Crystallinity ↑ → Strength ↑, Density ↑, Transparency ↓.
  - Molecular weight ↑ → $T_g$ ↑, $T_m$ ↑, Melt viscosity ↑, Tensile strength ↑.
  - Cross-linking ↑ → Thermoplastic behavior → Thermoset behavior.
- **Key Exam Numbers to Memorize:** $T_m$ of PE ≈ 137°C, $T_g$ of PS ≈ 100°C, $T_g$ of PMMA ≈ 105°C, $T_m$ of Nylon 6,6 ≈ 265°C, $T_m$ of PET ≈ 260°C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermosets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermosets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **thermoset** (or **thermosetting polymer**) is a high-molecular-weight polymeric material that undergoes an irreversible, chemical crosslinking reaction (curing) when subjected to heat, pressure, or a chemical catalyst, forming a permanent three-dimensional (3D) network structure. Once cured, the polymer cannot be remelted, reshaped, or reprocessed by the application of heat without undergoing thermal degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Thermosets are crosslinked polymers in which the chains are chemically bonded together by primary valence bonds into a rigid, infusible, three-dimensional network. The crosslinking reaction (curing) is irreversible, distinguishing thermosets fundamentally from thermoplastics."

The key terminology associated with thermosets:

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Monomer / Pre-polymer** | The starting low-molecular-weight reactive species (e.g., resole for phenolic, diglycidyl ether for epoxy). |
| **Crosslink** | A covalent chemical bond that links two or more polymer chains together. |
| **Curing** | The chemical process (usually exothermic) by which crosslinks are formed, converting a linear/branched pre-polymer into a rigid 3D network. |
| **Gel Point** | The critical stage during curing at which the first infinite molecular network is formed; viscosity rises sharply. |
| **Degree of Crosslinking (ν)** | The number of crosslinked units per unit volume; controls rigidity, brittleness, and solvent resistance. |
| **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** | The temperature above which segmental motion becomes significant. In highly crosslinked thermosets, Tg may be very high or the network may decompose before Tg is reached. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Boiled Egg Analogy"** — Imagine the liquid egg white (albumen) in its raw state. It flows, it is soluble in water, and you can change its shape. The moment you heat it, the proteins **denature and crosslink** permanently into a solid, opaque network. No matter how much you reheat the cooked egg, it will never revert to liquid albumen. If you overheat it, it will char and degrade. This is exactly how a thermoset behaves: **once cured, forever set**.

- **Thermoplastic** = butter (you can melt and re-solidify it any number of times).
- **Thermoset** = boiled egg (set permanently by an irreversible chemical change).
- **Elastomer** = rubber band (crosslinked but lightly, so it can stretch and snap back).

## 1.3 Distinguishing Thermosets from Thermoplastics

| Property | Thermoset | Thermoplastic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Structure** | 3D covalently crosslinked network | Linear or branched chains, held by secondary forces |
| **On heating** | Does not melt; chars/decomposes | Softens, melts, can be re-processed |
| **Solubility** | Insoluble in any solvent (only swells) | Soluble in suitable solvents |
| **Recyclability** | Non-recyclable by remelting | Recyclable by remelting |
| **Mechanical behavior** | Rigid, hard, brittle (high modulus) | Tough, ductile (variable) |
| **Processing** | Irreversible; chemical cure in mould | Reversible; injection/extrusion moulding |
| **Examples** | Phenolic, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde | PE, PP, PVC, PS |

## 1.4 Why Thermosets Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Thermosets dominate applications requiring **dimensional stability under load, heat resistance, chemical resistance, and electrical insulation** — from aircraft composite matrices to kitchen counter-tops, from electrical switchgear to automobile brake pads. Roughly **25–30 %** of all polymer production by mass consists of thermosets, even though they cannot be recycled, because their performance in service is unmatched in many engineering contexts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Crosslinking Reaction — The Heart of a Thermoset

A thermoset begins life as a **prepolymer** (or **resin**) — a low-viscosity, often liquid, system containing reactive functional groups. Upon triggering (heat, catalyst, hardener, or radiation), these functional groups react to form **covalent crosslinks** between chains. The reaction proceeds in three classical stages:

### Stage 1: Pre-gel (A-Stage)
- Pre-polymer is still soluble and fusible.
- Viscosity is low; molecules are linear or lightly branched.
- Material can be processed (impregnated into fibres, poured into moulds).

### Stage 2: Gelation (B-Stage)
- At the **gel point**, the first molecule of infinite molecular weight is formed.
- Viscosity rises dramatically (infinite ideally, but practically very high).
- The system is no longer fully soluble; it swells in solvents.
- Beyond the gel point, the material is **rubbery** and cannot flow.

### Stage 3: Vitrification / Full Cure (C-Stage)
- Crosslinking continues; the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) climbs.
- When $T_g$ reaches the curing temperature, the network **vitrifies** (freezes into a glassy state).
- Reaction becomes diffusion-controlled and slows dramatically.
- Final thermoset is hard, rigid, infusible, and insoluble.

## 2.2 Classification of Crosslinking Reactions

| Type | Mechanism | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Condensation** | Two functional groups react with elimination of a small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₂O) | Phenolic, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine-formaldehyde |
| **Addition** | Functional groups react without by-product | Epoxy + amine, Unsaturated polyester + styrene |
| **Oxidative** | Oxygen forms peroxide bridges | Natural rubber vulcanization (with sulphur) |
| **Photolytic / Radiation** | UV or electron-beam induced radical crosslinking | Acrylate photopolymers, electron-beam cured coatings |
| **Hydrosilylation** | Si-H + C=C → Si-C-C-H | Silicone elastomer cure |

## 2.3 Quantitative Network Parameters

For a perfectly crosslinked network, several theoretical relationships derived from the **Flory–Stockmayer theory** are essential:

> [!NOTE]
> **Flory's Network Theory Relationships**
> These equations describe the elasticity, swelling, and structure of a crosslinked polymer.

### 2.3.1 Average Molecular Weight Between Crosslinks ($M_c$)

For an ideal tetrafunctional network, the number of elastically active network chains per unit volume, $\nu_e$, is related to $M_c$ by:

$$\nu_e = \frac{\rho \cdot N_A}{M_c}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of the polymer (g/cm³ or kg/m³)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \ mol^{-1}}$
- $M_c$ = average molecular weight between successive crosslinks (g/mol)

### 2.3.2 Crosslink Density

The crosslink density (in mol/m³) is defined as:

$$\xi = \frac{\rho}{2 \cdot M_c}$$

The factor of 2 arises because each crosslink joins **two** chains.

### 2.3.3 Flory–Rehner Equation (Swelling of a Network)

For a crosslinked polymer swollen in a solvent, the Flory–Rehner equation links the volume fraction of polymer in the swollen gel ($\phi_2$), the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter ($\chi$), and the molecular weight between crosslinks ($M_c$):

$$M_c = \frac{-\rho \cdot V_1 \cdot \phi_2^{\,1/3}}{\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \cdot \phi_2^{\,2}}$$

where $V_1$ is the molar volume of the swelling solvent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of the Flory–Rehner Equation:**
> By experimentally measuring the equilibrium swelling of a thermoset in a known solvent and knowing the $\chi$ parameter, one can **back-calculate $M_c$** and hence the crosslink density of an unknown cured network. This is the standard laboratory method for characterising thermoset cure quality.

### 2.3.4 Modulus of a Perfect Network

From rubber elasticity theory, the shear modulus of an ideal crosslinked network above its $T_g$ is:

$$G = \nu_e \cdot R \cdot T = \frac{\rho \cdot R \cdot T}{M_c}$$

where $R$ = $\mathbf{8.314 \ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$ (universal gas constant) and $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

### 2.3.5 Stress–Strain (Affine Network Model)

For a perfect network in simple uniaxial extension at constant volume:

$$\sigma = \nu_e \cdot R \cdot T \left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$

where $\lambda = L / L_0$ is the extension ratio.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{M_c}$ | Elastically active chains per unit volume |
| 2 | $\xi = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \, M_c}$ | Crosslink density (mol/m³) |
| 3 | $G = \nu_e \, R \, T$ | Rubber elasticity modulus of network |
| 4 | $\sigma = \nu_e R T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)$ | Affine network stress–strain |
| 5 | Flory–Rehner (see §2.3.3) | Find $M_c$ from swelling |
| 6 | $T_g \uparrow$ as crosslink density $\uparrow$ | DiBenedetto empirical: $T_g = T_{g0} + \dfrac{K \, \xi}{1 - \xi}$ |
| 7 | $\eta \to \infty$ at gel point | Critical conversion $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f}$ for f-functional monomer |

> **Notation Note:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols (absolute value) are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking tables in markdown.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Use of Thermosets |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Aerospace** | Epoxy + carbon-fibre composites in aircraft wings (Boeing 787 ≈ 50 % composites) |
| **Electronics** | Epoxy moulding compounds encapsulating IC chips |
| **Automotive** | Phenolic brake pads; melamine–formaldehyde dashboards |
| **Construction** | Urea-formaldehyde particle board; epoxy floor coatings |
| **Cookware** | Phenolic handles on frying pans (Bakelite, ≈ 1907) |
| **Adhesives** | Epoxy structural adhesives; cyanoacrylate "super-glues" |
| **Coatings** | Alkyd/melamine stoving enamels for white goods |
| **Dental / Medical** | Bis-GMA composites; silicone elastomers for implants |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Crosslink Density from Swelling Data (Flory–Rehner)

This is the most commonly asked **14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for polymer modules. The detailed step-by-step logic is as follows.

### Step 1 — Free Energy of Mixing
For a polymer network immersed in a solvent, the total Gibbs free energy change, $\Delta G_{total}$, has two contributions:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{mix} + \Delta G_{elastic}$$

The **Flory–Huggins** expression for the free energy of mixing per unit volume is:

$$\Delta G_{mix} = RT \left[ n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 + n_1 \phi_2 \, \chi \right]$$

where $n_1, n_2$ are moles of solvent and polymer, $\phi_1, \phi_2$ are their volume fractions, and $\chi$ is the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter.

### Step 2 — Free Energy of Elastic Deformation
For an **affine network**, the elastic free energy change upon isotropic swelling (each chain stretches by a factor $\lambda = (\phi_{2,0}/\phi_2)^{1/3}$) is:

$$\Delta G_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} \, \nu_e \, R T \left[ 3 \lambda_1^{2} + 3 \lambda_2^{2} + 3 \lambda_3^{2} - 3 - \ln(\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3) \right]$$

For isotropic swelling, $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda$, and because the polymer is incompressible, $\lambda^3 = 1/\phi_2$. The expression simplifies to:

$$\Delta G_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e R T \left[ 3 \lambda^{2} - 3 - \ln \lambda^{3} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition
At equilibrium swelling, the chemical potential of the solvent inside the gel equals that of pure solvent outside. Mathematically, this means:

$$\frac{\partial \Delta G_{total}}{\partial n_1} = 0$$

After performing the differentiation (substituting $\phi_2 = 1 - \phi_1$ and $\lambda = \phi_2^{-1/3}$), and separating the mixing and elastic contributions, one obtains two equations:

**Mixing contribution (Flory–Huggins):**

$$\frac{\mu_1 - \mu_1^{0}}{RT} = \ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^{2}$$

**Elastic contribution (Gaussian network):**

$$\frac{\mu_1 - \mu_1^{0}}{RT} = \frac{\rho \, V_1}{M_c} \, \phi_2^{1/3}$$

### Step 4 — Equate and Solve for $M_c$

Equating the mixing and elastic chemical potential contributions:

$$\frac{\rho \, V_1}{M_c} \, \phi_2^{1/3} = \ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^{2}$$

Solving algebraically for $M_c$:

$$M_c = \frac{\rho \, V_1 \, \phi_2^{1/3}}{-\left[\ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^{2}\right]}$$

This is the **Flory–Rehner equation** in its classical form (note the negative sign to make $M_c$ positive, since the bracketed term is negative for $\phi_2 < 1$).

### Step 5 — Calculate Crosslink Density
Once $M_c$ is known, the crosslink density follows directly:

$$\xi = \frac{\rho}{2 \, M_c} \quad \text{(mol crosslinks / m}^{3}\text{)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A vulcanised natural-rubber sample (density $\rho = 920 \ kg/m^{3}$) is swollen in benzene ($V_1 = 8.9 \times 10^{-5} \ m^{3}/mol$, $\chi = 0.50$) to an equilibrium polymer volume fraction $\phi_2 = 0.18$. Calculate the molecular weight between crosslinks, $M_c$, and the crosslink density, $\xi$.

**Solution (step-by-step, KTU valuation style):**

**Step (a): Substitute numerical values into Flory–Rehner.**

$$M_c = \frac{(920)(8.9 \times 10^{-5})(0.18)^{1/3}}{-\left[\ln(1 - 0.18) + 0.18 + (0.50)(0.18)^{2}\right]}$$

**Step (b): Compute the cube root and logarithm terms.**

$$(0.18)^{1/3} = 0.5646$$

$$\ln(1 - 0.18) = \ln(0.82) = -0.1985$$

$$(0.18)^{2} = 0.0324 \implies (0.50)(0.0324) = 0.0162$$

**Step (c): Sum the bracketed denominator term.**

$$-0.1985 + 0.18 + 0.0162 = -0.0023$$

**Step (d): Compute the numerator.**

$$(920)(8.9 \times 10^{-5})(0.5646) = 0.04623 \ m^{3} \cdot kg/mol$$

**Step (e): Final division.**

$$M_c = \frac{0.04623}{-(-0.0023)} = \frac{0.04623}{0.0023} \approx 20.1 \ kg/mol \approx 20100 \ g/mol$$

**Step (f): Crosslink density.**

$$\xi = \frac{\rho}{2 \, M_c} = \frac{920}{2 \times 20.1} \approx 22.9 \ mol/m^{3}$$

**Interpretation:** Each polymer chain between crosslinks contains, on average, $M_c / M_0$ repeat units. For natural rubber ($M_0 = 68 \ g/mol$ for isoprene), the average number of isoprene units between crosslinks is $20100 / 68 \approx 296$ units, which is typical of a moderately crosslinked rubber.

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Conversion at the Gel Point

For an A–B type polycondensation between $f$-functional monomers, **Flory's statistical theory** gives the critical extent of reaction $p_c$ for gelation:

$$p_c = \frac{2}{f}$$

For example, a trifunctional ($f = 3$) phenol (3 reactive H atoms) reacting with formaldehyde has $p_c = 2/3 \approx 0.667$, meaning gelation begins when 66.7 % of the functional groups have reacted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $p_c$ so important in thermoset processing?**
> The mould must be filled and the part shaped **before** $p_c$ is reached. Once the gel point is crossed, the part cannot be re-processed. Process engineers use $p_c$ to set mould-closure times, cure cycles, and pot-life specifications.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Properties Scale with Crosslink Density

| Property | Effect of Increasing Crosslink Density ($\xi \uparrow$) | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M_c$ | $\downarrow$ (chains between crosslinks shorter) | Network becomes tighter |
| $T_g$ | $\uparrow$ (DiBenedetto equation) | Higher service temperature |
| Modulus $E, G$ | $\uparrow$ (rubbery modulus $\propto \nu_e$) | Stiffer, harder material |
| Elongation at break | $\downarrow \downarrow$ | More brittle |
| Swelling in solvent | $\downarrow$ (less penetrant absorbed) | Better chemical resistance |
| Impact strength | Often $\downarrow$ (unless toughened) | Brittleness rises |
| Permeability | $\downarrow$ | Better barrier properties |
| Density $\rho$ | $\uparrow$ slightly (covalent packing) | Marginal mass change |
| Refractive index | Slight $\uparrow$ | Optical density change |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thermoset Curing Process — From Pre-polymer to 3D Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage A: Pre-polymer / Resin] --> B[Liquid or low viscosity]
    B --> C[Heat / Catalyst / Hardener added]
    C --> D{Reaches Gel Point?}
    D -- "No" --> E[Stage B: B-Stage Resin]
    D -- "Yes" --> F[Infinite molecular network forms]
    E --> G[Fibres or fillers impregnated]
    G --> D
    F --> H[Stage C: Crosslinked 3D Network]
    H --> I[Hard, rigid, infusible thermoset]
    I --> J[Heating: Does NOT melt; Chars above Td]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Thermosetting Resins

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermosetting Resins] --> B[Condensation Type]
    A --> C[Addition Type]
    A --> D[Oxidative / Vulcanisation]
    A --> E[Radiation Cure]

    B --> B1[Phenolic PF]
    B --> B2[Urea-Formaldehyde UF]
    B --> B3[Melamine-Formaldehyde MF]
    B --> B4[Silicone SI from hydrolysis]

    C --> C1[Epoxy EP]
    C --> C2[Unsaturated Polyester UP]
    C --> C3[Vinyl Ester VE]
    C --> C4[Polyurethane PU]
    C --> C5[Bismaleimide BMI]

    D --> D1[Sulphur Vulcanised Rubber]
    D --> D2[Peroxide Cured EPDM]

    E --> E1[UV Curable Acrylates]
    E --> E2[Electron Beam Cured Coatings]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Cured Thermoset Composite

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reinforcement Fibre] --> B[Carbon / Glass / Aramid]
    A --> C[Matrix Resin]
    C --> D[Thermoset network]
    D --> E[Covalent crosslinks]
    D --> F[Elastically active chains]
    E --> G[Interface: fibre-matrix bond]
    F --> H[Load transfer mechanism]
    G --> I[Composite Part]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Schematic Comparison — Molecular Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Thermoplastic Structure]
        T1A[Chain A] --- T1B[Chain B] --- T1C[Chain C]
        T1A -.->|weak van der Waals| T1B
        T1B -.->|weak van der Waals| T1C
    end
    subgraph T2[Thermoset Structure]
        T2A[Chain X] === T2B[Chain Y]
        T2B === T2C[Chain Z]
        T2C === T2A
        T2A === T2C
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** In the thermoplastic subgraph, dashes (`-.->`) represent weak secondary bonds (van der Waals, dipolar, H-bonds) that break on heating. In the thermoset subgraph, thick lines (`===`) represent **strong covalent crosslinks** that are permanent and irreversible.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers with at least four points.**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU key words):**
1. **Structure:** Thermoplastics have linear/branched chains with secondary bonding; thermosets have a 3D covalently crosslinked network.
2. **On heating:** Thermoplastics soften and melt reversibly; thermosets do not melt — they char/decompose on overheating.
3. **Solubility:** Thermoplastics dissolve in suitable solvents; thermosets are insoluble (only swell).
4. **Recyclability:** Thermoplastics can be remoulded and recycled; thermosets cannot be remoulded after cure.
5. **Examples:** PE, PP, PVC (thermoplastics) vs. Phenolic, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde (thermosets).

*(Any four correctly framed points = 3 marks. KTU allocates ≈ 0.5–1 mark per correct point.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is meant by "curing" of a thermosetting polymer?**

**Model Answer:**
Curing is the **irreversible chemical crosslinking reaction** in which the low-molecular-weight pre-polymer (resin) is converted into a hard, infusible, three-dimensional network by the formation of covalent crosslinks between chains. It is typically initiated by heat, a catalyst, or a hardener, and is exothermic. Once cured, the material cannot be remelted or reprocessed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, broken into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (full-alternative choice within the module).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **three stages (A, B, C)** of curing of a thermosetting resin. What is the significance of the **gel point**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the term **crosslink density**. Using the **Flory–Rehner equation**, derive an expression for the molecular weight between crosslinks, $M_c$, of a swollen polymer network. State the significance of the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter, $\chi$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Three stages of cure:*
- **A-stage:** Pre-polymer is low-viscosity, soluble, fusible. Reactive functional groups are present. Resin can be processed (impregnation, casting). [2 marks]
- **B-stage:** Reaction proceeds; molecular weight increases; viscosity rises; partial branching occurs. Resin is no longer fully soluble but is still fusible at higher temperatures. B-stage resins are typically sold as moulding powders. [2 marks]
- **C-stage:** Final, fully crosslinked state. The polymer is hard, rigid, infusible, insoluble. It has a 3D covalent network. The crosslinking density and $T_g$ are at their final values. [2 marks]
- **Gel point:** Critical conversion at which the first molecule of infinite molecular weight appears. Viscosity effectively tends to infinity; the resin loses its ability to flow. Beyond the gel point the part cannot be reshaped. [1 mark]

*Diagram:* Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1. A clean hand-drawn equivalent earns full marks. [Add a labelled sketch of A → B → C with crosslinks appearing progressively.]

**(b)** *Derivation:* Present the Flory–Rehner derivation as in Section 3.1 (Steps 1 to 5). Key checkpoints for the examiner:
- Statement of total free energy = mixing + elastic [1 mark]
- Flory–Huggins expression for $\Delta G_{mix}$ [1 mark]
- Affine network expression for $\Delta G_{elastic}$ [1 mark]
- Differentiation and equilibrium condition [2 marks]
- Final Flory–Rehner equation with correct sign [2 marks]

*Significance of $\chi$:* $\chi$ is the **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter** that characterises the thermodynamic interaction between the polymer and the solvent. For $\chi < 0.5$, mixing is favourable (good solvent); for $\chi > 0.5$, mixing is unfavourable (poor solvent); $\chi = 0.5$ corresponds to a **theta solvent** at the theta temperature. [The 7th mark is awarded for this conceptual statement.]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **condensation** and **addition** mechanisms of thermoset cure, giving **two examples** of each. Write the chemical reaction involved in the formation of a **phenol-formaldehyde resin** (novolac) and explain why novolac requires a hardener.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A phenol-formaldehyde thermoset has density $\rho = 1240 \ kg/m^{3}$. When swollen in acetone ($V_1 = 7.4 \times 10^{-5} \ m^{3}/mol$, $\chi = 0.45$), the equilibrium polymer volume fraction is $\phi_2 = 0.22$. Calculate (i) the molecular weight between crosslinks, $M_c$, and (ii) the crosslink density, $\xi$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Mechanisms:*
- **Condensation cure:** Two or more functional groups react with the elimination of a small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₂O). The by-product must diffuse out of the mould, which can cause voids.
  - **Example 1:** Phenol + formaldehyde → phenolic resin + H₂O
  - **Example 2:** Urea + formaldehyde → UF resin + H₂O
- **Addition cure:** Functional groups react directly without by-product elimination. Shrinkage is lower and void-free mouldings are possible.
  - **Example 1:** Epoxy resin + diamine hardener
  - **Example 2:** Unsaturated polyester + styrene (radical addition)

*Novolac reaction:* Under acidic conditions and with formaldehyde-to-phenol ratio < 1, linear/branched novolac is formed:

$$\text{Phenol} + \text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^{+}, < 100^{\circ}C} \text{Novolac (linear, no self-curing)} + H_2O$$

Novolac is **deficient in methylol (-CH₂OH) groups**; it is a thermoplastic pre-polymer and **cannot self-cure** because there are insufficient crosslinkable sites. Therefore, a **hardener** (typically **hexamethylenetetramine, HMTA**) is added. On heating, HMTA decomposes to formaldehyde and ammonia, which create **methylene (-CH₂-)** and **benzylamine (-CH₂-NH-)** crosslinks between novolac chains, yielding a fully crosslinked phenolic thermoset. [Marks: 1 for reaction, 1 for explanation, 1 for the role of HMTA, 1 for any one of the crosslink types formed.]

**(b)** Numerical Solution:

**Given:** $\rho = 1240 \ kg/m^{3}$, $V_1 = 7.4 \times 10^{-5} \ m^{3}/mol$, $\chi = 0.45$, $\phi_2 = 0.22$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\phi_2^{1/3}$.

$$\phi_2^{1/3} = (0.22)^{1/3} = 0.603$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator.

$$\ln(1 - 0.22) + 0.22 + (0.45)(0.22)^{2}$$

$$= \ln(0.78) + 0.22 + (0.45)(0.0484)$$

$$= -0.2485 + 0.22 + 0.0218 = -0.0067$$

**Step 3:** Numerator.

$$(1240)(7.4 \times 10^{-5})(0.603) = 0.0553 \ m^{3} \cdot kg/mol$$

**Step 4:** $M_c$.

$$M_c = \frac{0.0553}{0.0067} \approx 8.25 \ kg/mol = 8250 \ g/mol$$

**Step 5:** Crosslink density.

$$\xi = \frac{\rho}{2 M_c} = \frac{1240}{2 \times 8.25} \approx 75.2 \ mol/m^{3}$$

**Final answers (boxed in the answer script):**
$$\boxed{M_c \approx 8.25 \ kg/mol \quad ; \quad \xi \approx 75.2 \ mol/m^{3}}$$

**Mark split:** Setting up Flory–Rehner = 2 marks; computing intermediate values = 2 marks; final $M_c$ = 1.5 marks; final $\xi$ = 1.5 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Flory–Rehner:** Forgetting the negative sign in the denominator is the single most common mistake. The denominator must be negative; the negative sign in front of it makes $M_c$ positive.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $V_1$ must be in m³/mol, $\rho$ in kg/m³. Mixing CGS and SI units gives a wrong order of magnitude.
> 3. **Stating $\chi = 0.5$ always:** The student must use the given $\chi$, not assume a theta condition.
> 4. **Confusing novolac and resole:** Novolac (acid-catalysed, F/P < 1) is **not** self-curing; resole (base-catalysed, F/P > 1) is self-curing. Examiners routinely deduct 2 marks for mixing these up.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in crosslink density:** $\xi = \rho / (2 M_c)$, not $\rho / M_c$. The "2" accounts for the fact that each crosslink bridges two chains.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **thermoset** is a permanently crosslinked polymer; it cannot be remelted once cured.
- [x] The three stages of cure are **A-stage (resin), B-stage (rubbery), C-stage (rigid network)**.
- [x] The **gel point** marks the onset of an infinite molecular network; beyond it, flow stops.
- [x] **Crosslink density** $\xi = \rho / (2 M_c)$ controls rigidity, $T_g$, and solvent resistance.
- [x] **Flory–Rehner equation** relates swelling to $M_c$; the negative sign is essential.
- [x] **Rubber elasticity:** $G = \nu_e R T = \rho R T / M_c$ — modulus rises as crosslink density rises.
- [x] **Condensation cure** releases a small molecule; **addition cure** does not.
- [x] **Novolac** (acid, F/P < 1) needs **HMTA** hardener; **Resole** (base, F/P > 1) is self-curing.
- [x] **Phenolic, Epoxy, UF, MF, UP, Silicone, Polyurethane, BMI** are the chief commercial thermosets.
- [x] Major engineering applications: composites (epoxy + carbon fibre), moulding compounds (phenolic, melamine), coatings (alkyd–melamine), adhesives (epoxy), and elastomers (silicone, vulcanised rubber).
- [x] **Tg rises** with crosslink density (DiBenedetto); brittleness also rises.
- [x] **Recycling limitation** is the chief environmental drawback of thermosets — research on vitrimers and recyclable epoxies is an active 2024–2025 frontier.
- [x] KTU examiners love the **Flory–Rehner derivation** (14-mark, full-alternative choice) and the **novolac vs. resole distinction** (3-mark, short note).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastomers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elastomers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastomers** are a specialized class of polymeric materials that exhibit **rubber-like elasticity**. According to the ASTM D1566 standard, an elastomer is "a polymeric material which at room temperature can be stretched repeatedly to at least twice its original length and, upon immediate release of the stress, will return with force to its approximate original length."

Technically, elastomers are **amorphous polymers** possessing a **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** that is substantially below their service temperature, combined with a **three-dimensional network** of long, flexible polymer chains connected by sparse **covalent crosslinks**. The crosslink density is deliberately kept low (typically **1 crosslink per 100–1000 monomer units**) so that the chains retain sufficient segmental mobility to undergo large, reversible conformational rearrangements.

**Key Quantitative Thresholds for Elastomeric Behavior:**
- **Elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b$)** $\geq$ **100%** (often 300–800%)
- **Young's Modulus ($E$)** in the range **0.01 – 0.1 GPa** ($10$–$100$ MPa)
- **Elastic recovery** $\geq$ **90%** after strain release within short time scales
- **Low $T_g$** typically well below **0 °C**

> [!NOTE]
> The terms *elastomer* and *rubber* are often used interchangeably. However, strictly speaking, *rubber* refers to the material before crosslinking (vulcanization), while *elastomer* denotes the final crosslinked, elastic product.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spring Ball-Pit

Imagine you have a container filled with millions of **tangled, cooked spaghetti strands**, all coiled up and loosely tied to each other at a few random knots (the crosslinks). When you grab the container and pull it apart:

- The spaghetti strands **straighten out** (chain uncoiling).
- The knots prevent the strands from sliding past each other completely or from flowing apart.
- When you release, the **thermal agitation** (Brownian motion) of the strands recoils them, snapping the container back to its original shape.

That is exactly how an elastomer works:
- **Spaghetti strands** = Flexible polymer backbones.
- **Loose knots** = Covalent sulfur crosslinks.
- **Pulling the container** = Applied mechanical stress.
- **Thermal agitation** = Entropic driving force for recovery.

Without the crosslinks (knots), the strands would simply flow apart — that would be a viscous liquid or a thermoplastic melt, not an elastomer.

---

### 1.3 Distinguishing Elastomers from Other Polymer Classes

| Property | Elastomer | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Fiber |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_g$ vs Service Temp. | $T_g \ll T_{service}$ | $T_g$ near $T_{service}$ | $T_g \gg T_{service}$ | Highly crystalline |
| Crosslinking | Light (sparse) | None | Dense (network) | Strong secondary bonds |
| Elastic Recovery | Very high | Low (plastic flow) | Brittle if strained | Minimal |
| Typical Modulus (MPa) | 1 – 100 | 1000 – 3500 | 2000 – 5000 | 5000 – 30000 |
| Reprocessability | Difficult (network) | Easy (melt) | Impossible | N/A |
| Example | Natural rubber vulcanizate | Polyethylene | Phenolic resin | Nylon 6,6 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to differentiate, always mention **(i)** low crosslink density and **(ii)** low $T_g$ relative to use temperature as the two non-negotiable characteristics of an elastomer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Molecular Architecture Required for Elastomeric Behavior

For a polymer to behave as an elastomer, **four structural prerequisites** must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Long, flexible polymer chains** — A high degree of rotational freedom around backbone bonds (e.g., –C–C–, –C–O–, –Si–O–). Short chains cannot store sufficient entropic energy.
2. **Weak intermolecular forces** — Van der Waals or dipole interactions that are weak enough to allow chain uncoiling under stress but strong enough to prevent cold flow.
3. **$T_g$ well below use temperature** — At service temperature, the polymer must be in the **rubbery plateau** region, well above its $T_g$ so that micro-Brownian motion is active.
4. **Sparse covalent crosslinks** — A three-dimensional network must form to provide a **memory effect**, preventing permanent deformation (plastic flow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Network "Tasks":**
> - **Crosslinks** act as **anchor points**, ensuring the original shape is recovered.
> - **Chain segments** between crosslinks (called **network chains**) act as **entropy springs**.
> - **Free chain ends** are **defects** that do **not** contribute to elasticity — fewer chain ends means higher elastic modulus.

---

### 2.2 Vulcanization — The Heart of Elastomer Technology

**Vulcanization** (introduced by **Charles Goodyear in 1839**) is the process of forming crosslinks between polymer chains, typically using **sulfur** in natural rubber, although other agents (peroxides, metal oxides, radiation) are also used.

#### 2.2.1 Chemistry of Sulfur Vulcanization (for Natural Rubber)

The active species is a **polysulfidic crosslink** ($-\text{C}-\text{S}_x-\text{C}-$, where $x = 1$ to $8$).

**Step 1:** Activation of sulfur
$$\text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{Activator (ZnO + Stearic Acid)}} \text{Polysulfidic species}$$

**Step 2:** Formation of a sulfurating agent
$$\text{Accelerator (e.g., MBT)} + \text{S}_8 + \text{ZnO} \longrightarrow \text{Zn–S}_x\text{–Accelerator complex}$$

**Step 3:** Crosslink formation with allylic hydrogen of polyisoprene
$$\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\sim + \text{S}_x \longrightarrow \sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\overset{\text{S}_x-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{CH}}{\underset{\vert}{\text{CH}}}\sim$$

#### 2.2.2 Vulcanization Ingredients (Compounding Recipe)

| Ingredient | Function | Typical Examples | Phr (parts per hundred rubber) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymer base** | Matrix | NR, SBR, NBR, EPDM | 100 |
| **Crosslinker** | Forms network | Sulfur, peroxides | 0.5 – 3.5 |
| **Accelerator** | Speeds cure | MBT, CBS, TMTD, ZDEC | 0.5 – 2 |
| **Activator** | Boosts accelerator | ZnO + stearic acid | 3 – 5 + 1 – 2 |
| **Filler (Reinforcing)** | Improves modulus & strength | Carbon black (N330), silica | 20 – 60 |
| **Filler (Non-reinforcing)** | Reduces cost | CaCO₃, clay | 20 – 100 |
| **Plasticizer** | Improves processability | Aromatic oils, DOP | 5 – 30 |
| **Antioxidant** | Retards ageing | IPPD, 6PPD, TMQ | 1 – 2 |
| **Antiozonant** | Ozone protection | Wax, 6PPD | 1 – 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:** "**S**ulfur **A**ccelerator **A**ctivator **F**iller **P**lasticizer **A**ntioxidant" = **SAAFPA** — the six functional classes of every rubber compound.

---

### 2.3 Classification of Elastomers

**A. Natural Rubber (NR):** Cis-1,4-polyisoprene (from *Hevea brasiliensis* latex). Excellent tensile strength, resilience, low heat build-up. Used in tires, gloves, vibration mounts.

**B. Synthetic Rubbers:**

| Type | Full Name | Monomer(s) | Key Property | Major Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SBR** | Styrene–Butadiene Rubber | Styrene + Butadiene | Good abrasion resistance | Tire treads, conveyor belts |
| **BR** | Polybutadiene Rubber | 1,3-Butadiene | Very low $T_g$ ($-110\,^\circ$C), high resilience | Tire sidewalls, golf balls |
| **NBR** | Nitrile Butadiene Rubber | Butadiene + Acrylonitrile | Excellent oil & fuel resistance | Fuel hoses, O-rings, gaskets |
| **IIR** | Butyl Rubber | Isobutylene + Isoprene | Very low gas permeability | Inner tubes, gas masks |
| **CR (Neoprene)** | Polychloroprene | Chloroprene | Flame, oil, weather resistance | Wet suits, conveyor belts |
| **EPDM** | Ethylene–Propylene–Diene | Ethylene + Propylene + ENB | Excellent ozone & heat resistance | Roofing, automotive seals |
| **CSM (Hypalon)** | Chlorosulfonated Polyethylene | Chlorinated + SO₂Cl PE | Chemical resistance | Tank linings, cable insulation |
| **Silicone (Q)** | Polydimethylsiloxane | D4 cyclic siloxane | Wide temp. range ($-60$ to $+230\,^\circ$C) | Medical implants, seals |
| **PU** | Polyurethane | Diisocyanate + Polyol | Exceptional abrasion resistance | Wheels, rollers, foams |
| **ACM** | Polyacrylate | Acrylic esters | Heat & oil resistance | Automotive seals |

**C. Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPEs):** Block copolymers such as **SBS (Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene)**, **SEBS**, **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)**, and **TPO (Thermoplastic Olefin)**. They have hard and soft segments but are **not covalently crosslinked** — instead, the hard domains act as physical crosslinks that melt on processing.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations form the core analytical toolkit for elastomer mechanics and are repeatedly tested in KTU University Examinations.

| # | Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = 3\,\nu\,R\,T$ | Young's modulus of an ideal rubber (small strain) | $\nu$ = crosslink density ($\text{mol/m}^3$); $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$; $T$ = absolute temp. (K) |
| 2 | $\sigma = \nu\,R\,T\,\left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$ | True stress under uniaxial extension (Neo-Hookean) | $\sigma$ = true stress (Pa); $\lambda$ = extension ratio $= 1 + \varepsilon$ |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho\,N_A}{2\,\bar{M}_c}$ | Crosslink density from network chain molecular weight | $\rho$ = polymer density ($\text{kg/m}^3$); $\bar{M}_c$ = avg. $M$ between crosslinks ($\text{kg/mol}$); $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ |
| 4 | $\bar{M}_c = \dfrac{2\,\rho\,R\,T\,\phi^{1/3}}{E}$ | Inverse relationship between $\bar{M}_c$ and modulus | $\phi$ = Flory–Rehner front factor (≈ 1 for dry rubber) |
| 5 | $f = -\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial r}\right)_{T} + T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial r}\right)_{T}$ | General entropic + enthalpic elastic force | $f$ = restoring force; $U$ = internal energy; $S$ = entropy |
| 6 | $G = \nu\,R\,T$ | Shear modulus (small strain, ideal network) | $G$ = shear modulus (Pa); $E = 3G$ |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\rho\,R\,T}{\bar{M}_c}$ | Simplified form assuming affine network | Same as Eq. 3 combined with Eq. 1 |
| 8 | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2\,\Delta H_f}{\Delta S_f}$ | Approximate glass transition (conceptual) | $\Delta H_f$, $\Delta S_f$ = enthalpy/entropy of fusion at $T_g$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation 1 ($E = 3\nu RT$) is the single most important relationship in rubber engineering. It tells the formulator that to increase stiffness by **30%** at a given temperature, you must increase the **crosslink density by 30%** (e.g., more sulfur). It is the foundation of **compound design** in tire engineering, gaskets, and anti-vibration mounts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Statistical (Kinetic) Theory of Rubber Elasticity

This is the **flagship derivation** for elastomer mechanics, treating the polymer network as an ensemble of Gaussian chains in thermal equilibrium.

**Goal:** Derive the equation for the **retractive stress** of an ideal rubber.

---

#### **Step 1 — Single Chain Conformation**

A single flexible chain between two crosslinks has $n$ freely-jointed segments each of length $l$. The **end-to-end vector** $\vec{r}$ has a probability distribution given by the **Gaussian function**:

$$P(\vec{r}) = \left(\dfrac{3}{2\,\pi\,n\,l^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{3\,r^{2}}{2\,n\,l^{2}}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** Most probable end-to-end distance is zero (a random coil); large extensions are exponentially rare.

---

#### **Step 2 — Entropy of a Single Chain**

By the Boltzmann relation:

$$S = k_B\,\ln\,\Omega$$

The number of microstates $\Omega$ is proportional to $P(\vec{r})$. Taking the natural logarithm and absorbing constants into $S_0$:

$$S(r) = S_0 - \dfrac{3\,k_B\,r^{2}}{2\,n\,l^{2}}$$

**Physical meaning:** Entropy **decreases quadratically** as the chain is stretched — fewer microstates are available.

---

#### **Step 3 — Entropy of the Network**

For $N$ chains in the network (each having $n$ segments), assuming affine deformation where the macroscopic extension ratio $\lambda_i$ scales the end-to-end vector as $r_i = \lambda_i\,r_{i,0}$:

$$\Delta S_{\text{net}} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\,N\,k_B \sum_{i} \left(\lambda_i^{2} - 1\right)$$

For **uniaxial extension** along the x-axis, the principal extension ratios are:
- $\lambda_x = \lambda$
- $\lambda_y = \lambda_z = \lambda^{-1/2}$ (incompressibility, $\lambda_x \lambda_y \lambda_z = 1$)

Substituting:

$$\Delta S_{\text{net}} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\,N\,k_B \left(\lambda^{2} + \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

---

#### **Step 4 — Helmholtz Free Energy**

For an **ideal rubber**, internal energy $U$ is assumed independent of deformation (purely entropic elasticity):

$$F = U - T\,S$$

$$\Delta F = -T\,\Delta S_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,N\,k_B\,T \left(\lambda^{2} + \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

---

#### **Step 5 — Retractive Force (Stress)**

The retractive force is the derivative of free energy with respect to extension at constant $T$ and volume $V$:

$$f = \left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial \lambda}\right)\left(\dfrac{\partial \lambda}{\partial L}\right)$$

Since $L = \lambda\,L_0$, $\partial\lambda/\partial L = 1/L_0$:
