$$\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,N\,k_B\,T \left(2\lambda - \dfrac{2}{\lambda^{2}}\right) = N\,k_B\,T \left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$f = \dfrac{N\,k_B\,T}{L_0}\left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$

---

#### **Step 6 — Convert to True Stress (Engineering Form)**

Engineering stress $f/A_0$ where $A_0$ is the original cross-section. The **true stress** $\sigma = f/A$ where $A = A_0/\lambda$. Substituting and using $N/V = \nu\,N_A$ and $k_B N_A = R$:

$$\sigma = \nu\,R\,T \left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$

For **small strains**, $\lambda \approx 1 + \varepsilon$, and using Taylor expansion $\lambda^{2} \approx 1 + 2\varepsilon$, $\lambda^{-2} \approx 1 - 2\varepsilon + 3\varepsilon^{2}$:

$$\sigma \approx \nu\,R\,T\,(3\varepsilon) = 3\,\nu\,R\,T\,\varepsilon$$

Since $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\,E = 3\,\nu\,R\,T\,} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \boxed{\,G = \nu\,R\,T\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Crosslink Density from Swelling Data (Flory–Rehner)

When an elastomer is immersed in a good solvent, it swells. The equilibrium swelling ratio $Q$ (volume of swollen / volume of dry) relates to $\bar{M}_c$ via:

$$\chi_1 = \text{Flory–Huggins interaction parameter}$$

$$\dfrac{\chi_1}{V_1} = -\dfrac{\nu}{V_0}\!\left[\ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi_1\,\phi_2^{2}\right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{\phi_2^{2/3}}$$

Where $\phi_2 = 1/Q$ is the polymer volume fraction at equilibrium. Solving for $\bar{M}_c$:

$$\bar{M}_c = \dfrac{-\rho\,V_1\,\phi_2^{1/3}}{\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi_1\,\phi_2^{2}}$$

with $V_1$ = molar volume of solvent, $\rho$ = polymer density.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

> **Problem:** A natural rubber vulcanizate has a density $\rho = 910\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and an average molecular weight between crosslinks $\bar{M}_c = 5000\,\text{g/mol} = 5\,\text{kg/mol}$. Calculate **(a)** the crosslink density $\nu$, **(b)** the Young's modulus $E$ at $T = 298\,\text{K}$, and **(c)** the predicted true stress at $\lambda = 2.5$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Crosslink density:**

$$\nu = \dfrac{\rho\,N_A}{2\,\bar{M}_c} = \dfrac{910 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 5}$$

$$\nu = \dfrac{5.480 \times 10^{26}}{10} = 5.480 \times 10^{25}\,\text{crosslinks/m}^{3}$$

Converting to $\text{mol/m}^3$ by dividing by $N_A$:

$$\nu = \dfrac{5.480 \times 10^{25}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 90.99\,\text{mol/m}^{3} \approx 91\,\text{mol/m}^{3}$$

**(b) Young's modulus:**

$$E = 3\,\nu\,R\,T = 3 \times 90.99 \times 8.314 \times 298$$

$$E = 3 \times 90.99 \times 2477.6 \approx 6.763 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} \approx 0.676\,\text{MPa}$$

**(c) True stress at $\lambda = 2.5$:**

$$\sigma = \nu\,R\,T \left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right) = 90.99 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \left(2.5 - \dfrac{1}{6.25}\right)$$

$$\sigma = 2.254 \times 10^{5} \times (2.5 - 0.16) = 2.254 \times 10^{5} \times 2.34$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 5.275 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} \approx 0.528\,\text{MPa}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Thermoset Rubber vs Thermoplastic Elastomer

| Criterion | Vulcanized Rubber (e.g., NR + S) | Thermoplastic Elastomer (e.g., SBS) |
|---|---|---|
| Crosslink type | **Covalent** (C–S$_x$–C) | **Physical** (glass domain anchors) |
| Reversibility of crosslink | Irreversible (chemical bond) | Reversible on heating above $T_m$ of hard block |
| Process route | Compression/injection molding + cure | Melt extrusion, injection, blow molding |
| Recyclability | Poor (ground into crumb) | Excellent (re-meltable) |
| Service temp. ceiling | ~120 °C (NR) | ~80 °C (SBS), higher for TPU |
| Compression set (25%, 70°C, 24h) | 15 – 30% | 30 – 60% |
| Tensile strength | 20 – 30 MPa (with carbon black) | 5 – 25 MPa |
| Cost per kg | Lower for SBR/NR | Higher (specialty) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following diagrams are constructed in **Mermaid syntax** with KTU-compliant node identifiers (purely alphanumeric, prefixed with letters) and plain uppercase labels — strictly no markdown bold or HTML inside the labels.

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification Hierarchy of Elastomers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[ELASTOMERS] --> B1[NATURAL RUBBER]
    A0 --> B2[SYNTHETIC RUBBER]
    A0 --> B3[THERMOPLASTIC ELASTOMER]
    B1 --> C1A[POLYISOPRENE NR]
    B1 --> C1B[POLYISOPRENE GUTTA PERCHA]
    B2 --> D1[GENERAL PURPOSE]
    B2 --> D2[OIL RESISTANT]
    B2 --> D3[HEAT AND WEATHER RESISTANT]
    B2 --> D4[SPECIALTY]
    D1 --> E1A[SBR STYRENE BUTADIENE]
    D1 --> E1B[BR POLYBUTADIENE]
    D1 --> E1C[IIR BUTYL RUBBER]
    D1 --> E1D[EPDM]
    D2 --> E2A[NBR NITRILE RUBBER]
    D2 --> E2B[CR NEOPRENE]
    D2 --> E2C[ACM POLYACRYLATE]
    D3 --> E3A[EPDM]
    D3 --> E3B[CSM HYPALON]
    D3 --> E3C[SILICONE Q]
    D3 --> E3D[FLUOROELASTOMER FKM]
    D4 --> E4A[PU POLYURETHANE]
    D4 --> E4B[CHLORINATED PE]
    B3 --> F1A[SBS STYRENIC BLOCK]
    B3 --> F1B[TPU THERMOPLASTIC URETHANE]
    B3 --> F1C[TPO OLEFINIC]
    B3 --> F1D[COPA COPOLYAMIDE]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Flow of Vulcanization Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[RAW POLYMER NR SBR NBR] --> Q1[MASTICATION BREAKDOWN MW]
    Q1 --> R1[MASTERBATCH MIXING ADD FILLERS PLASTICIZER]
    R1 --> S1[ADD CURATIVES SULFUR ACCELERATOR ZNO]
    S1 --> T1[SHAPING EXTRUSION CALENDERING MOLDING]
    T1 --> U1[VULCANIZATION 140 TO 180 DEG C 5 TO 30 MIN]
    U1 --> V1[POST CURE TRIM INSPECTION]
    V1 --> W1[FINAL ELASTOMER PRODUCT]

    X1[QUALITY CONTROL TESTS] -.-> U1
    X2[REJECT SCRAP RECYCLE] -.-> R1
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Network Architecture: Cured vs Uncured Rubber

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph S1[UNCURED RUBBER]
        UC1[POLYMER CHAIN A] --> UC2[POLYMER CHAIN B]
        UC2 -.->|WEAK VAN DER WAALS| UC3[POLYMER CHAIN C]
        UC3 -.->|NO PERMANENT BOND| UC4[POLYMER CHAIN D]
        UC4 --> UC5[POLYMER CHAIN E]
    end
    subgraph S2[VULCANIZED RUBBER]
        VC1[POLYMER CHAIN A] ==>|SULFUR CROSSLINK| VC2[POLYMER CHAIN B]
        VC2 ==>|SULFUR CROSSLINK| VC3[POLYMER CHAIN C]
        VC3 ==>|SULFUR CROSSLINK| VC4[POLYMER CHAIN D]
        VC4 ==>|SULFUR CROSSLINK| VC5[POLYMER CHAIN E]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Stress–Strain Behavior Comparative

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[APPLIED STRAIN EPSILON] --> B1[ELASTOMER]
    A0 --> B2[THERMOPLASTIC]
    A0 --> B3[THERMOSET]
    A0 --> B4[ELASTIC FIBER]
    B1 --> C1[LOW MODULUS HIGH ELONGATION ELASTIC RECOVERY OVER 90 PERCENT]
    B2 --> C2[HIGH MODULUS YIELD AT 5 TO 50 PERCENT PLASTIC DEFORMATION]
    B3 --> C3[HIGH MODULUS BRITTLE FRACTURE BELOW 5 PERCENT]
    B4 --> C4[VERY HIGH MODULUS LOW ELONGATION HIGH STRENGTH]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "elastomer". List any four characteristic properties that distinguish elastomers from plastics.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** An elastomer is a polymeric material that can be stretched repeatedly to at least twice its original length at room temperature and, upon release of the applied stress, returns immediately to its approximate original length (ASTM D1566).
>
> **Four characteristic properties (½ mark each):**
> 1. **High elastic recovery** ($\geq$ 90%) upon removal of stress.
> 2. **Very high elongation at break** (typically 100 – 800%).
> 3. **Low Young's modulus** (0.01 – 0.1 GPa) compared to plastics.
> 4. **Low glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** well below service temperature, ensuring rubbery behavior.
> 5. *(Optional bonus property)* Presence of a sparse **covalent crosslinked network** for shape memory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is vulcanization? Mention the role of (a) sulfur, (b) accelerators, and (c) zinc oxide in the vulcanization of natural rubber.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Vulcanization (1 mark):** Vulcanization is the chemical process of forming crosslinks between polymer chains, converting soft, tacky raw rubber into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable material. It was discovered by Charles Goodyear in 1839.
>
> **Roles (2 marks):**
> - **(a) Sulfur (0.7 mark):** Acts as the **primary crosslinking agent**. It forms polysulfidic bridges ($-\text{C}-\text{S}_x-\text{C}-$) between adjacent polymer chains at the allylic positions of polyisoprene, typically used in the range 0.5 – 3.5 phr.
> - **(b) Accelerators (0.7 mark):** Organic compounds such as **MBT, CBS, TMTD** that dramatically increase the rate of vulcanization (reducing cure time from hours to minutes) and improve crosslink efficiency by enabling use of less sulfur.
> - **(c) Zinc oxide / ZnO (0.6 mark):** Acts as an **activator** in combination with stearic acid. It forms a Zn–accelerator complex that activates sulfur, enabling faster and more complete crosslink formation at lower temperatures.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (Module Internal Choice):** *"Answer any ONE FULL question from this module. Each full question carries 14 marks and contains two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each."* Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Natural Rubber & Vulcanization Chemistry**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **latex processing and mastication of natural rubber**. Explain why mastication is necessary and how it alters the molecular weight distribution. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a **balanced chemical equation**, explain the **sulfur vulcanization of natural rubber**, including the role of accelerators and activators. Discuss the effect of **crosslink density on tensile strength, modulus, and elongation at break**. *(7 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Latex Processing & Mastication (7 marks):**
>
> - **Latex collection (1.5 marks):** Natural rubber latex is the milky sap of the *Hevea brasiliensis* tree, an aqueous colloidal dispersion (~30–40% cis-1,4-polyisoprene) stabilized by proteins and fatty acids. The latex is **tapped**, treated with **ammonia** (preservative), and shipped to processing plants.
> - **Coagulation (1.5 marks):** Acetic or formic acid is added to the latex to lower its pH and **coagulate** the rubber particles into a soft, white mass. This is then washed, sheeted, and **air-dried** into **ribbed smoked sheets (RSS)** or **crepe rubber**.
> - **Mastication (2.5 marks):** Raw NR has a very high molecular weight ($\bar{M}_w \approx 10^{6}\,\text{g/mol}$), making it tough and difficult to process. **Mastication** is the mechanical shearing of raw rubber on a **two-roll mill** or **internal mixer (Banbury)** at temperatures typically **30 – 80 °C**, in the presence of **peptizers** (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol, zinc 2-benzamidothiophenate).
> - **Purpose & effect (1.5 marks):** Mastication **breaks down the long chains** via mechano-oxidative scission, lowering $\bar{M}_w$ to $\sim 2 \times 10^{5}$ and narrowing the **molecular weight distribution** (MWD). This improves **processability** (lower viscosity, easier mixing of fillers and curatives) without destroying the chain's ability to be re-crosslinked later.
>
> **(b) Sulfur Vulcanization Chemistry (7 marks):**
>
> - **Reaction (2.5 marks):** Sulfur ($\text{S}_8$) is activated by ZnO + stearic acid + accelerator to form a reactive polysulfidic species that attacks the **allylic C–H bond** of the polyisoprene repeat unit:
>
> $$\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\sim + \text{S}_x \longrightarrow \sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\overset{\text{S}_x-\sim}{\underset{\vert}{\text{CH}}}\sim$$
>
> Two such radicals on adjacent chains combine to form a **polysulfidic crosslink** ($-\text{C}-\text{S}_x-\text{C}-$, where $x$ ranges from 1 to 8). Most efficient networks contain **mono- and di-sulfidic** crosslinks ($x = 1, 2$).
>
> - **Role of accelerator & activator (2 marks):** **Accelerators** like MBT (2-mercaptobenzothiazole) or CBS (N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide) form complexes with ZnO + stearic acid that activate sulfur, allowing **efficient cure at 140 – 160 °C in 5 – 30 minutes** instead of hours.
> - **Effect of crosslink density (2.5 marks):**
>
> | Property | Crosslink Density ↑ (more S) |
> |---|---|
> | Young's Modulus ($E$) | **Increases** (per $E = 3\nu RT$) |
> | Tensile Strength | **Increases to a maximum**, then **decreases** (over-cure becomes brittle) |
> | Elongation at Break | **Decreases** monotonically (chains become more constrained) |
> | Hardness / Resilience | **Increases**, then falls at over-cure |
> | Hysteresis / Heat Build-up | **Increases** (energy dissipation) |
>
> An **optimum crosslink density** exists at ~0.5 – 2 phr sulfur for maximum mechanical performance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Synthetic Elastomers & Statistical Theory of Rubber Elasticity**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare the following synthetic rubbers with respect to their **monomer composition, key property, and one major industrial application**: (i) SBR, (ii) NBR, (iii) IIR, (iv) EPDM, (v) Silicone. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the equation for the **Young's modulus of an ideal rubber** using the **statistical (kinetic) theory of rubber elasticity**. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**
>
> | Elastomer | Monomers | Key Property | Major Application |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **SBR** (Styrene–Butadiene Rubber) | Styrene (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂) + 1,3-Butadiene | Excellent **abrasion resistance**; $T_g \approx -50\,^\circ$C | **Passenger car tire treads** |
> | **NBR** (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber) | Butadiene + Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN) | Outstanding **oil & fuel resistance** (proportional to ACN content) | **Fuel hoses, O-rings, gaskets** in automobiles |
> | **IIR** (Butyl Rubber) | Isobutylene + small amount of isoprene (≈3%) | Extremely **low gas permeability**; $T_g \approx -67\,^\circ$C | **Inner tubes** of tires, pharmaceutical closures, gas mask seals |
> | **EPDM** (Ethylene–Propylene–Diene Monomer) | Ethylene + Propylene + non-conjugated diene (e.g., ENB) | Excellent **ozone, UV, and heat resistance**; can be peroxide-cured | **Automotive weather-strips, roofing membranes, radiator hoses** |
> | **Silicone (Q)** | Dichlorodimethylsilane → polydimethylsiloxane via hydrolysis | Wide service temp. **$-60$ to $+230\,^\circ$C**; biocompatible; excellent dielectric | **Medical implants, food-grade seals, high-temperature gaskets** |
>
> **(b) Derivation (7 marks):** *(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes — Step 1 to Step 6.)*
>
> **Key assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. Each network chain behaves as a **Gaussian chain** (valid for $\lambda \leq 1.5$).
> 2. The network deforms **affinely** — junction points move proportionally with the macroscopic strain.
> 3. The polymer is **incompressible** ($\lambda_x \lambda_y \lambda_z = 1$).
> 4. The internal energy $U$ is **independent of deformation** — elasticity is purely **entropic**.
>
> **Final Result (highlight):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,E = 3\,\nu\,R\,T\,} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \boxed{\,\sigma = \nu\,R\,T\left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (High-Frequency KTU Valuation Errors):**
>
> 1. **Wrong crosslink density equation (–3 marks):** Some students write $\nu = \rho N_A / \bar{M}_c$ (missing the factor of 2). The correct form for an *ideal tetrafunctional* network is $\nu = \rho N_A / (2\bar{M}_c)$, because each crosslink is shared between **two** chains.
> 2. **Confusing $T_g$ with $T_m$ for elastomers (–2 marks):** Elastomers are largely **amorphous** (no sharp melting point). They have a $T_g$ well below service temperature. Writing about melting behavior is a mark-loser.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong isomeric form of polyisoprene (–2 marks):** **Natural rubber is *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene** (rubbery); **gutta-percha is *trans*-1,4-polyisoprene** (hard, crystalline). Always specify the isomer.
> 4. **Skipping assumptions in kinetic theory derivation (–2 marks):** A derivation without the four standard assumptions (Gaussian, affine, incompressible, pure entropic) is treated as incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 5. **Using "rubber" and "elastomer" interchangeably without justification (–1 mark):** Technically *rubber* = pre-cure, *elastomer* = post-cure crosslinked product.
> 6. **Mixing up $E = 3\nu RT$ vs $G = \nu RT$ (–2 marks):** $E$ has the factor of 3 (uniaxial extension); $G$ does not (pure shear).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before Walking Into the Exam Hall.**

- ✅ **Elastomer =** polymer with $T_g \ll T_{\text{service}}$ **AND** sparse covalent crosslinks **AND** high elastic recovery ($\geq$ 90%).
- ✅ **NR is *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene** from *Hevea brasiliensis*; **gutta-percha is the *trans* isomer**.
- ✅ **Vulcanization** = crosslinking with sulfur (Goodyear, 1839). Crosslink type: polysulfide ($-\text{C}-\text{S}_x-\text{C}-$).
- ✅ **Vulcanization ingredients = SAAFPA:** **S**ulfur, **A**ccelerator, **A**ctivator, **F**iller, **P**lasticizer, **A**ntioxidant.
- ✅ **ZnO + Stearic Acid** = activator system. **MBT/CBS/TMTD** = accelerators.
- ✅ **Mastication** mechanically reduces $\bar{M}_w$ of NR from ~$10^6$ to ~$2 \times 10^5$ g/mol for processability.
- ✅ **Carbon black** = reinforcing filler; **CaCO₃/clay** = non-reinforcing (extender) filler.
- ✅ **SBR** = tire treads; **NBR** = oil seals; **IIR** = inner tubes; **EPDM** = weather strips; **Silicone** = high-temp seals; **CR (Neoprene)** = wet suits & belts.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $E = 3\nu RT$ (Young's modulus); $G = \nu RT$ (shear modulus).
- ✅ **Master stress equation (Neo-Hookean):** $\sigma = \nu RT\left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)$.
- ✅ **Crosslink density:** $\nu = \dfrac{\rho\,N_A}{2\,\bar{M}_c}$.
- ✅ **Increasing crosslink density** → ↑ modulus, ↑ hardness, ↓ elongation, tensile strength peaks then drops.
- ✅ **Kinetic theory assumptions:** Gaussian chains, affine deformation, incompressibility, purely entropic elasticity.
- ✅ **TPEs (e.g., SBS)** are **physically** crosslinked (hard domains) and **thermally reversible**; thermoset rubbers are **chemically** crosslinked (irreversible).
- ✅ **Compression set** is a key durability parameter — lower is better.
- ✅ **Service temperatures:** NR ≈ 80–100 °C; EPDM ≈ 150 °C; Silicone ≈ 230 °C; FKM ≈ 250 °C.
- ✅ **Tire sidewalls** use BR (polybutadiene) for fatigue resistance; **tire treads** use SBR + BR blend for abrasion + resilience.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Polymerization: Addition, Condensation, and Copolymerisation - Different Types of Copolymers and Their Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Polymerization: Addition, Condensation, and Copolymerisation - Different Types of Copolymers and Their Characteristics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Polymerization — The Foundational Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymerization** is a chemical process in which small molecules called **monomers** (from Greek *meros* = part) undergo successive chemical reactions to form long-chain macromolecules known as **polymers** (from Greek *poly* = many). The repeat unit in the polymer backbone is called a **mer**. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Train and the Coaches" Imagine a **monomer** as a single railway coach. Polymerization is like coupling thousands of these coaches one after another to form an entire long train — the **polymer**. The coupling mechanism (how the coaches snap together) and the type of coaches (identical vs. mixed) determine the kind of train you get. --- ## 1.2 Classification of Polymerization Reactions Polymerization is broadly classified into two mechanistic categories and one combined strategy: ### A. Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Addition polymerization* is a chain reaction in which monomers with unsaturated groups (typically C=C) add to a growing chain one unit at a time via a reactive centre (radical, ion, or metal complex) without the loss of any small molecule. The molecular formula of the repeating unit is **identical** to that of the monomer. - **Mechanism Initiation:** A radical or ionic initiator (R•) attacks the π-bond of the monomer. - **Propagation:** The active centre transfers to the new chain-end, which then attacks another monomer. - **Termination:** Two active chains combine or disproportionate, ending growth. - **Example:** Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) → Polyethylene (-CH₂-CH₂-)ₙ > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Addition polymerization is *also known as* **chain-growth polymerization**. Examples include free-radical, cationic, anionic, and coordination (Ziegler–Natta) polymerizations. ### B. Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Condensation polymerization* is a stepwise reaction between bifunctional or polyfunctional monomers in which each bond-forming step eliminates a small molecule (commonly **H₂O**, **HCl**, **CH₃OH**, or **NaCl**). The repeat-unit molecular formula is **NOT** the same as either monomer. - **Mechanism Initiation:** Any two functional groups (e.g., -COOH and -OH) can react — *no initiator required*. - **Propagation:** All species (monomer, dimer, trimer, oligomer) coexist and react randomly. - **Termination:** Occurs only when no more complementary functional groups are available. - **Example:** Hexamethylenediamine + Adipic acid → Nylon-6,6 + n H₂O > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Condensation polymerization is *also known as* **step-growth polymerization**. Examples include polyesterification, polyamidation, and phenol-formaldehyde resin formation. ### C. Copolymerization > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Copolymerization* is the polymerization of a mixture of two or more different monomers (M₁, M₂, …) to form a macromolecule whose backbone contains more than one type of repeat unit. The product is called a **copolymer**. - **Why copolymerize?** To combine the desirable properties of different homopolymers into a single material (e.g., toughness of polybutadiene with rigidity of polystyrene → **high-impact polystyrene, HIPS**). - **Example:** Styrene + Butadiene → SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) used in tyres. > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** Copolymerization is **not a separate mechanism** — it can proceed via *either* addition or condensation pathway depending on the monomers. The distinction is in the **number of distinct monomers** used, not the mechanism. --- ## 1.3 Comparison Snapshot (Memorize This Table) | Feature | Addition (Chain-Growth) | Condensation (Step-Growth) | |---|---|---| | Initiator required? | Yes (radical/ionic) | No | | Monomer type | Unsaturated (C=C, C≡C) | Bifunctional/polyfunctional | | Small molecule released? | **No** | **Yes** (H₂O, HCl, etc.) | | Repeat unit = monomer? | Yes | No | | Growth pattern | Chain-end reactive centre | Random step-wise | | Molecular weight rises | Steadily, sudden | Slowly, very high conversion needed | | Examples | PE, PP, PVC, PS | Nylon, PET, Bakelite | --- ## 1.4 Different Types of Copolymers and Their Characteristics Copolymers are classified based on the **arrangement** of the two monomer units (let's call them A and B) along the polymer backbone: ### 1.4.1 Alternating Copolymer - **Sequence:** …A–B–A–B–A–B… - **Characteristics:** - Perfectly regular alternation. - Requires strong electron-donor/electron-acceptor pair (e.g., styrene + maleic anhydride). - Properties are uniform and predictable. - Often more crystalline than random copolymers. ### 1.4.2 Random (Statistical) Copolymer - **Sequence:** …A–A–B–A–B–B–A–A–B… - **Characteristics:** - Monomer units follow a statistical distribution governed by **reactivity ratios** (r₁, r₂). - Most common type industrially. - Properties are an average of the two homopolymers. - Example: **Saran** (vinyl chloride + vinylidene chloride). ### 1.4.3 Block Copolymer - **Sequence:** …AAAA–BBBB–AAAA–BBBB… - **Characteristics:** - Long continuous runs (blocks) of one monomer joined to long runs of another. - Often exhibit **microphase separation** → self-assembly into nanostructures (spheres, cylinders, lamellae). - Example: **SBS rubber** (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene) — used in shoe soles and asphalt modification. - Synthesized via **living polymerization** or controlled radical techniques (RAFT, ATRP). ### 1.4.4 Graft Copolymer - **Sequence:** Backbone of monomer A with branches (grafts) of monomer B. - **Visualization:** Like a "tree" — main trunk = A; branches = B. - **Characteristics:** - Combines properties of backbone and graft. - Example: **HIPS** (High-Impact Polystyrene) — PS backbone with polybutadiene grafts. - Synthesized by grafting B onto pre-formed A backbone (e.g., via peroxide initiation). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook — "ARBG"**: A → **A**lternating, **R**andom, **B**lock, **G**raft. This acronym is highly useful for short-answer questions worth 3 marks. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Mechanism of Addition Polymerization The free-radical chain-growth mechanism proceeds in **three (sometimes four) distinct stages**: ### Step 1: Initiation A free-radical initiator (commonly benzoyl peroxide, BPO, or azobisisobutyronitrile, AIBN) decomposes under heat/light to produce primary radicals (R•). The radical then adds to a monomer's double bond, generating a chain-initiating radical. $$I \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 R^{\bullet}$$ $$R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CHX \rightarrow R-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX$$ ### Step 2: Propagation The active chain-end radical repeatedly adds monomer units; each addition regenerates the radical at the new chain-end. $$R-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX + CH_2=CHX \rightarrow R-CH_2-CHX-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX$$ ### Step 3: Termination Two growing chain radicals combine (coupling) or disproportionate. **Coupling:** $$R-(CH_2-CHX)_n-^{\bullet}CHX + R-(CH_2-CHX)_m-^{\bullet}CHX \rightarrow R-(CH_2-CHX)_n-CHX-CHX-(CH_2-CHX)_m-R$$ **Disproportionation:** $$R-(CH_2-CHX)_n-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX + R-(CH_2-CHX)_m-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX \rightarrow R-(CH_2-CHX)_n-CH=CHX + R-(CH_2-CHX)_m-CH_2-CH_2X$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The **kinetic chain length (ν)** is the average number of monomers consumed per radical. Higher ν → higher molecular weight. Termination by coupling produces chains with degree of polymerization ≈ 2ν; termination by disproportionation ≈ ν. ### Step 4 (Optional): Chain Transfer The active radical abstracts an atom (often H) from solvent, initiator, or polymer, transferring the activity. This limits molecular weight and creates branched structures. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism of Condensation Polymerization ### Step 1: Monomer Collision & Reaction Any two species with complementary functional groups (e.g., -COOH + -OH) can react. No initiator needed. Monomers can be: - **A–B type** (e.g., amino acid, hydroxy acid) - **A–A + B–B type** (e.g., diacid + diol, diamine + diacid) ### Step 2: Stepwise Growth At any instant, all species — monomer, dimer, trimer, …, n-mer — are present and reactive. Each step releases a small molecule (H₂O, HCl, etc.). $$HOOC-R-COOH + HO-R'-OH \rightarrow HOOC-R-COO-R'-OH + H_2O$$ ### Step 3: High-Conversion Requirement > [!TIP] > **Carothers' Insight (1936):** To obtain high molecular weight in step-growth polymerization, **conversion must exceed 98–99%**. This is because number-average degree of polymerization $\bar{X}_n$ is inversely related to the fraction of unreacted functional groups *p*. --- ## 2.3 Copolymerization Kinetics — The Mayo-Lewis Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **central equation** of copolymerization and is **high-yield** for KTU problems. KTU examiners frequently ask derivations and applications of this equation. The **instantaneous copolymer composition** is governed by four propagation reactions involving two monomers M₁ and M₂ with reactivity ratios r₁ and r₂. $$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \quad ; \quad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$$ **Mayo-Lewis (Copolymer Composition) Equation:** $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$$ **Mole fraction of M₁ in copolymer:** $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$ where $f_1$ and $f_2$ are mole fractions of M₁ and M₂ in the feed, and $F_1$, $F_2$ are mole fractions in the copolymer. ### Special Cases (Must Memorize) | Condition | r₁ × r₂ | Copolymer Type | |---|---|---| | r₁ = r₂ = 0 | 0 | Perfectly **alternating** | | r₁ = r₂ = 1 | 1 | **Random** (ideal) | | r₁ > 1, r₂ < 1 | varies | Tends to **block** with some alternation | | r₁ = r₂ → large | large | Tends to **homopolymer** mixture (two separate polymers) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Don't confuse *reactivity ratios* (r₁, r₂) with *reactivity* itself. High r₁ means the radical ending in M₁ prefers M₁ over M₂. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!NOTE] > The following table is your **exam-day quick reference**. Memorize the variables, units, and physical meaning of each equation. | # | Equation Name | Formula | Variable Key | Units | Valid For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number-average DP (Step-growth, equal stoichiometry) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | p = extent of reaction (conversion) | dimensionless | Condensation only | | 2 | Number-average DP (Unequal stoichiometry, ratio r) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | r = N_A/N_B ≤ 1 | dimensionless | Condensation with imbalance | | 3 | Carothers' gel point | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | $\bar{f}$ = avg functionality | dimensionless | Branched step-growth | | 4 | Mayo-Lewis composition | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1](r_1[M_1]+[M_2])}{[M_2]([M_1]+r_2[M_2])}$ | r₁, r₂ = reactivity ratios | dimensionless | All copolymerizations | | 5 | Instantaneous M₁ in copolymer | $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | f = feed mole fraction | dimensionless | Free-radical copolymerization | | 6 | Alfrey-Price Q-e scheme | $r_1 = \dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp[-e_1(e_1-e_2)]$ | Q = reactivity, e = polarity | dimensionless | Predicting unknown reactivity ratios | | 7 | Kinetic chain length (radical) | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | R_p, R_t = rates | — | Free-radical polymerization | | 8 | Number-average MW | $\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0$ | M₀ = repeat unit mass | g/mol | All polymers | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility — Why these formulas matter:** Equation 1 (Carothers) tells a process engineer that reactor design for step-growth polymers must achieve *extreme* conversion (often by removing byproduct water under vacuum) to push $\bar{X}_n$ above 100. Equation 4 (Mayo-Lewis) is the design tool for any industrial copolymer (SBR, EVA, SAN) — without it, you'd get a "block of one homopolymer" rather than a useful mixed material. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carothers' Equation for Number-Average Degree of Polymerization > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **favourite KTU question** (often asked for 7 marks as part (a) of a 14-mark problem). ### Given: - Equal stoichiometry of bifunctional monomers A–A and B–B - Let $N_0$ = total number of monomer molecules initially present - Let $N$ = total number of molecules (of *all* sizes) remaining at time t - Let $p$ = fraction of functional groups that have reacted (extent of reaction) ### Step 1: Count the reacted functional groups Each A–A or B–B monomer contributes **2 functional groups**. Initially, total functional groups = $2N_0$. After extent $p$, the number of reacted groups = $2N_0 \cdot p$. ### Step 2: Count the bonds formed Each bond formed consumes **2** functional groups (one A and one B). So number of bonds formed = $2N_0 \cdot p / 2 = N_0 \cdot p$. ### Step 3: Express the new molecule count A polymer of $X$ repeat units has $(X-1)$ bonds connecting it. If we let $N_X$ = number of molecules containing X repeat units, then: $$\text{Total bonds formed} = \sum_{X=2}^{\infty} (X-1) N_X$$ Therefore: $$N_0 \cdot p = \sum_{X=2}^{\infty} (X-1) N_X$$ ### Step 4: Sum the total molecules The total number of molecules at any time: $$N = \sum_{X=1}^{\infty} N_X$$ ### Step 5: Compute $N_0 - N$ The decrease in molecule count equals the bonds formed: $$N_0 - N = \sum_{X=1}^{\infty} (X-1) N_X = N_0 p$$ $$\therefore \quad N = N_0 (1-p)$$ ### Step 6: Compute $\bar{X}_n$ The number-average degree of polymerization is the ratio of original monomers to current molecules: $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$ **Interpretation:** At p = 0.99, $\bar{X}_n = 100$; at p = 0.999, $\bar{X}_n = 1000$. This is why step-growth polymers require extreme conversion. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mayo-Lewis Copolymer Composition Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU Polymer Science papers. Master every line. ### Given: Two monomers M₁ and M₂. The active chain-end can be either •M₁ or •M₂. There are four propagation reactions: $$^{\bullet}M_1 + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} ^{\bullet}M_1 \quad \text{rate} = k_{11}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_1]$$ $$^{\bullet}M_1 + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} ^{\bullet}M_2 \quad \text{rate} = k_{12}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_2]$$ $$^{\bullet}M_2 + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} ^{\bullet}M_1 \quad \text{rate} = k_{21}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_1]$$ $$^{\bullet}M_2 + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} ^{\bullet}M_2 \quad \text{rate} = k_{22}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_2]$$ ### Step 1: Rate of consumption of M₁ M₁ is consumed by reactions with •M₁ and •M₂: $$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_1] + k_{21}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_1]$$ ### Step 2: Rate of consumption of M₂ $$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_2] + k_{22}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_2]$$ ### Step 3: Take the ratio (instantaneous composition of copolymer) $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_1] + k_{21}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_1]}{k_{12}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_2] + k_{22}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_2]}$$ ### Step 4: Apply steady-state on active chain-ends At steady state, the rate of formation of •M₁ equals rate of disappearance: $$\frac{d[^{\bullet}M_1]}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow k_{21}[^{\bullet}M_2][M_1] = k_{12}[^{\bullet}M_1][M_2]$$ Solving for the ratio of active centres: $$\frac{[^{\bullet}M_1]}{[^{\bullet}M_2]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute and simplify Let $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$. Substituting the steady-state ratio into the composition equation and simplifying: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}}$$ This is the **Mayo-Lewis differential copolymerization equation** (1944). ### Step 6: Convert to mole-fraction form Divide numerator and denominator by $[M_2]^2$ and use $f_1 + f_2 = 1$: $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Q-e Scheme (Alfrey-Price, 1947) ### Concept Each monomer is assigned two empirical parameters: - **Q** → measures *general reactivity* (resonance stabilization of the radical) - **e** → measures *polarity* (electron-withdrawing/donating character of the substituent) The rate constant for a cross-propagation step is: $$k_{12} = P_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot \exp[-(e_1 \cdot e_2)]$$ where $P_1$ depends only on radical 1. ### Reactivity ratio r₁: $$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} = \frac{P_1 Q_1 \exp[-e_1^2]}{P_1 Q_2 \exp[-e_1 e_2]}$$ $$\boxed{r_1 = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)]}$$ Similarly: $$r_2 = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \exp[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)]$$ **Engineering utility:** The Q-e scheme is used to *predict* unknown reactivity ratios from tabulated Q, e values — a powerful tool for designing new copolymer formulations. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples > [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently gives 3-mark sub-parts that are direct numerical substitutions into the formulas above. Practice these *before* the exam. ### Example 1 — Carothers' Equation A nylon-6,6 synthesis reaches 99% conversion. Calculate $\bar{X}_n$. **Solution:** Using $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ with p = 0.99: $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$ The number-average degree of polymerization is **100**. ### Example 2 — Mayo-Lewis Composition Feed: $f_1 = 0.5$, $f_2 = 0.5$, $r_1 = 0.5$, $r_2 = 1.5$. Find $F_1$. **Solution:** $$F_1 = \frac{(0.5)(0.5)^2 + (0.5)(0.5)}{(0.5)(0.5)^2 + 2(0.5)(0.5) + (1.5)(0.5)^2}$$ $$F_1 = \frac{0.125 + 0.25}{0.125 + 0.5 + 0.375} = \frac{0.375}{1.000} = 0.375$$ The copolymer contains **37.5 mol % of M₁** even though the feed is 50/50 — the result of preferential incorporation. ### Example 3 — Reactivity Ratio from Q-e Given $Q_1 = 1.0$, $Q_2 = 0.5$, $e_1 = -0.8$, $e_2 = 0.4$. Calculate $r_1$. **Solution:** $$r_1 = \frac{1.0}{0.5} \cdot \exp[-(-0.8)(-0.8 - 0.4)]$$ $$r_1 = 2.0 \cdot \exp[-(-0.8)(-1.2)] = 2.0 \cdot \exp[-0.96]$$ $$r_1 = 2.0 \times 0.383 = 0.766$$ **Interpretation:** Since $r_1 < 1$, the •M₁ radical prefers to add M₂ — leading to alternating tendencies. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Initiation]:::init --> A1[Initiator I decomposes
by heat or light] A1 --> A2[Two primary radicals R* formed] A2 --> A3[R* attacks monomer C=C bond
forms M1* active centre] A3 --> B[Step 2: Propagation]:::prop B --> B1[Active centre adds monomer 1
transfers radical to new end] B1 --> B2[New chain end adds monomer 2
chain grows by one unit] B2 --> B3[Repeats n times
degree of polymerization increases] B3 --> C{Step 3: Termination}:::term C --> C1[Coupling
two radicals combine] C --> C2[Disproportionation
H transfer between chains] C1 --> D[Dead polymer chain] C2 --> D D --> E[Final polymer P_n] classDef init fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef prop fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef term fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[AA monomer
e.g. diacid]:::monomer --> R1[Reaction with BB] M2[BB monomer
e.g. diol]:::monomer --> R1 R1 --> Dimer[AB dimer plus H2O]:::inter Dimer --> R2[React with monomer] R2 --> Trimer[ABA trimer plus H2O]:::inter Dimer --> R3[React with another dimer] R3 --> Tetramer[ABAB tetramer plus 2H2O]:::inter Trimer --> R4[Continue reacting with any species] Tetramer --> R4 R4 --> Olig[Oligomer n mer]:::olig Olig --> R5[High conversion over 99 percent]:::highconv R5 --> Polymer[High MW polymer]:::poly classDef monomer fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inter fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef olig fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef highconv fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef poly fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Four Types of Copolymers (Architectural View) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SCOPE[COPOLYMER CLASSIFICATION] direction LR A1[ALTERNATING
A B A B A B]:::alt A2[RANDOM
A A B A B B A]:::rand A3[BLOCK
AAAA BBBB AAAA]:::block A4[GRAFT
Main A chain with B branches]:::graft end A1 --> P1[Properties:
Highly regular, often crystalline
Example: Styrene maleic anhydride]:::prop A2 --> P2[Properties:
Statistical arrangement
Example: Saran PVC PVDC]:::prop A3 --> P3[Properties:
Microphase separation
Example: SBS thermoplastic elastomer]:::prop A4 --> P4[Properties:
Combines backbone and branch features
Example: HIPS impact polystyrene]:::prop classDef alt fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rand fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef block fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef graft fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef prop fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid — Q-e Scheme Workflow (Predicting Reactivity Ratios) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Pick two monomers M1 and M2]:::start --> S2[Look up Q and e values
from literature tables]:::lookup S2 --> S3[Compute r1 using
r1 equals Q1 over Q2 times
exp of minus e1 times e1 minus e2]:::calc S2 --> S4[Compute r2 using
r2 equals Q2 over Q1 times
exp of minus e2 times e2 minus e1]:::calc S3 --> S5[Apply Mayo Lewis equation
to get copolymer composition]:::apply S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6{Is the result r1 r2 near 1?}:::check S6 -->|Yes| R1[Random copolymer expected]:::res S6 -->|No, r1 r2 near 0| R2[Alternating copolymer expected]:::res S6 -->|No, both r values large| R3[Block or blend tendency]:::res classDef start fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lookup fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef apply fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef check fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef res fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When asked to "draw a schematic of a copolymer type", the **graft copolymer** is most often requested. Draw a horizontal line for the backbone, then draw short lines branching off it, labelled with the graft monomer. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Addition Polymerization | Condensation Polymerization | |---|---|---| | Monomer type | Unsaturated (C=C) | Bifunctional/polyfunctional | | Byproduct | **None** | **Small molecule** (H₂O, HCl) | | Mechanism | Chain-growth via active centre | Step-growth via functional group reaction | | Initiator | Required (radical/ionic) | Not required | | Molecular weight rise | Sudden after long induction | Gradual, high conversion needed | **Examples:** - *Addition:* Ethylene → Polyethylene; Vinyl chloride → PVC - *Condensation:* Adipic acid + Hexamethylenediamine → Nylon-6,6; Terephthalic acid + Ethylene glycol → PET *(Allocation: 2 marks for the table, 1 mark for examples)* ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List any four types of copolymers with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Alternating** — e.g., Styrene–Maleic anhydride copolymer 2. **Random (Statistical)** — e.g., Saran (Vinyl chloride–Vinylidene chloride) 3. **Block** — e.g., SBS (Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene) 4. **Graft** — e.g., HIPS (Polystyrene backbone with Polybutadiene grafts) *(Allocation: 0.75 marks per correct type with example)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 1 Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme pattern, two questions are set from Module 1 and the student answers **one**. We provide both choices here with full sub-part solutions. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the free-radical addition polymerization mechanism with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss initiation, propagation, and termination steps.** **Model Solution:** **Initiation (2 marks):** - An initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, BPO) decomposes under heat: $$I \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 R^{\bullet}$$ - The primary radical R• adds to a monomer's C=C double bond, generating a chain-initiating radical: $$R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CHX \rightarrow R-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX$$ **Propagation (3 marks):** - The active chain-end radical adds monomer units sequentially. - The radical character is *transferred* to the new chain-end after each addition. - Reaction: $$R-CH_2-^{\bullet}CHX + n(CH_2=CHX) \rightarrow R-(CH_2-CHX)_n-^{\bullet}CHX$$ - This step determines the molecular weight. **Termination (2 marks):** - **Coupling:** Two chain radicals combine to form one dead polymer: $$P_n^{\bullet} + P_m^{\bullet} \rightarrow P_{n+m}$$ - **Disproportionation:** A hydrogen atom is transferred from one chain to the other, yielding two dead chains (one saturated, one with a terminal double bond). **Sketch (to be drawn on answer sheet):** A typical figure shows the three steps with arrows indicating electron transfer; the monomer is drawn as a small circle, the growing chain as a linked series, and the radical as a dot at the chain end. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Derive the Carothers' equation for the number-average degree of polymerization $\bar{X}_n$ in step-growth polymerization. Show that high molecular weight requires very high conversion.** **Model Solution:** (Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.) **Key derivation steps (as written on answer sheet):** 1. Let $N_0$ = total initial monomer molecules, $N$ = total molecules at time t, $p$ = conversion. 2. Bonds formed = $N_0 \cdot p$ (because each bond consumes 2 functional groups out of $2N_0$). 3. Each bond reduces molecule count by 1, so $N_0 - N = N_0 p$, giving $N = N_0(1-p)$. 4. Therefore: $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$ 5. **Numerical demonstration:** - At p = 0.90 → $\bar{X}_n = 10$ - At p = 0.99 → $\bar{X}_n = 100$ - At p = 0.999 → $\bar{X}_n = 1000$ **Conclusion:** To get commercially useful molecular weights (DP > 100), conversion must exceed **99%**, which is industrially achieved by removing the water byproduct (Le Chatelier's principle) and using excess of one reactant initially. *(Allocation: 4 marks for derivation, 2 marks for numerical demonstration, 1 mark for conclusion)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Define copolymerization. With neat sketches, explain the four major types of copolymers and their characteristic properties.** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** Copolymerization is the polymerization of a mixture of two or more chemically distinct monomers (M₁, M₂) to form a polymer containing more than one type of repeat unit in its backbone. **Types and Properties (6 marks — 1.5 marks each):** | Type | Schematic | Properties | |---|---|---| | **Alternating** | …A–B–A–B–A–B… | Perfectly regular; requires strong donor–acceptor pair; high crystallinity; predictable properties | | **Random** | …A–A–B–A–B–B–A… | Statistical arrangement governed by reactivity ratios; most common industrial type; average properties | | **Block** | …AAAAA–BBBBB–AAAAA… | Long runs of each monomer; microphase separation; thermoplastic elastomer behaviour; requires living/controlled polymerization | | **Graft** | Backbone A with B branches | Combines backbone and graft properties; impact modification (HIPS example); post-polymerization grafting | **Sketches:** Must show repeat-unit arrangements clearly. Use the diagrams from Section 4.3 of these notes as reference. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Derive the Mayo–Lewis copolymer composition equation and explain the significance of reactivity ratios r₁ and r₂.** **Model Solution:** (Full derivation is given in Section 3.2. Outline written for exam sheet:) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the four propagation rate equations for •M₁ and •M₂ radicals with M₁ and M₂ monomers. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Express rate of disappearance of M₁ and M₂ separately. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply steady-state to chain-end radicals: $$\frac{[^{\bullet}M_1]}{[^{\bullet}M_2]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Take the ratio and substitute steady-state result. Define $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$. **Step 5 (2 marks):** Obtain final equation: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$$ **Significance of reactivity ratios (2 marks):** - **r₁ = k₁₁/k₁₂**: Tendency of •M₁ radical to add M₁ relative to M₂. - **r₂ = k₂₂/k₂₁**: Tendency of •M₂ radical to add M₂ relative to M₁. - If r₁ × r₂ = 0 → alternating - If r₁ = r₂ = 1 → random - If r₁ × r₂ ≫ 1 → block/homopolymer blend tendency > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Confusing $f_1$ (feed mole fraction) with $F_1$ (copolymer mole fraction). These are **NOT equal** unless r₁ = r₂ = 1. > - **Common mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Writing the Mayo-Lewis equation without the steady-state derivation — KTU examiners award **only 2 of 7 marks** for "just the formula". Always show the four propagation steps. > - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert percentages to decimals in numerical problems (using p = 99 instead of 0.99 in Carothers' equation). > - **Common mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Drawing a copolymer schematic without *labelling* A and B units. Labelling earns full credit; unlabelled drawings earn partial credit only. > - **Common mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Stating that addition polymerization is faster than condensation. While kinetically true, the *reason* is the different chain-length-vs-conversion relationship — not just speed. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final-day revision summary** — read this 30 minutes before the exam. ### 🔑 Core Definitions (3-mark territory) - ✅ **Polymerization**: Monomer → Polymer chemical conversion. - ✅ **Addition (chain-growth)**: Initiation → Propagation → Termination; C=C required; no byproduct. - ✅ **Condensation (step-growth)**: Any species reacts with any species; small molecule eliminated. - ✅ **Copolymerization**: ≥2 different monomers → backbone with multiple repeat units. - ✅ **Copolymer types**: Alternating, Random, Block, Graft (memorize the "ARBG" mnemonic). ### 🔑 Essential Formulas - ✅ $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ — Carothers' equation. - ✅ $d[M_1]/d[M_2] = ([M_1]/[M_2]) \cdot (r_1[M_1] + [M_2])/([M_1] + r_2[M_2])$ — Mayo-Lewis. - ✅ $r_1 = (Q_1/Q_2) \cdot \exp[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)]$ — Q-e scheme. ### 🔑 Numerical Thresholds to Remember - ✅ Step-growth needs **p > 99%** for useful molecular weight. - ✅ p = 0.50 → $\bar{X}_n$ = 2; p = 0.90 → $\bar{X}_n$ = 10; p = 0.99 → $\bar{X}_n$ = 100. ### 🔑 Reactivity-Ratio Interpretation - ✅ r₁r₂ = 0 → perfectly alternating. - ✅ r₁ = r₂ = 1 → random (azeotropic). - ✅ r₁ > 1 and r₂ > 1 → block/homopolymer blend. ### 🔑 Industrial Examples (commonly asked) - ✅ **SBR** (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) — tyres, random copolymer. - ✅ **SBS** (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene) — thermoplastic elastomer, block copolymer. - ✅ **HIPS** (High-Impact Polystyrene) — graft copolymer. - ✅ **Nylon-6,6** — condensation polymerization with water release. - ✅ **PE, PP, PVC, PS, PTFE** — addition polymerization products. ### 🔑 Examiner Expectations - ✅ Always show mechanisms as *flow diagrams* or *numbered steps*. - ✅ Always *label* the four reactivity-ratio propagation steps in the Mayo-Lewis derivation. - ✅ Always *convert* percentage to decimal in numerical substitution. - ✅ Always include a *practical example* for every theoretical concept. - ✅ For 14-mark questions, allocate ~7 marks to each sub-part and maintain visible sub-headings.

topics.title: Molecular Weight of Polymers: Weight Average and Number Average Molecular Weight, Sedimentation and Viscosity Average Molecular Weights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Molecular Weight of Polymers: Weight Average and Number Average Molecular Weight, Sedimentation and Viscosity Average Molecular Weights ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is "Molecular Weight" in Polymers? In low-molecular-weight compounds (water, ethanol, glucose), every molecule has *essentially the same* molecular mass. Polymers are fundamentally different. A sample of polyethylene, polystyrene, or PVC is a **mixture of macromolecules of different chain lengths**, produced simultaneously during chain polymerisation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > The **molecular weight of a polymer** is therefore a *statistical average* over this distribution of chain lengths. It is not a single value, but a set of averages — each obtained by a different experimental technique, each sensitive to a different statistical moment of the molecular-weight distribution. Because of this, polymer chemists speak of several different average molecular weights, the four most important being: 1. **Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_n$)** 2. **Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_w$)** 3. **Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_v$)** 4. **Sedimentation-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_z$, $\overline{M}_{z+1}$)** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class of Students" Picture > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy (the easiest way to remember this forever):** > > Imagine a classroom of **1000 students** of *different* heights. > > - If you ask: *"How tall is the average student?"* — every student is **counted once**, tall or short. This gives an average biased toward the **many short ones**. → This is $\overline{M}_n$. > - If you ask: *"What is the average height, but each student is weighted by his/her own height?"* — tall students contribute *more*. The average is biased toward the **few tall ones**. → This is $\overline{M}_w$. > > Since every polymer chain in the sample is a "student" of different length, the two averages are **always different**, with $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$. The ratio $\overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n$ is called the **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** — a measure of how broad the molecular-weight distribution is. For a perfectly uniform (monodisperse) polymer, PDI = 1. For most commercial polymers, PDI lies between **1.5 and 4**. ### 1.3 Why is this Important? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** > Mechanical strength, melt viscosity, glass-transition temperature, impact resistance, solution viscosity, and processability all depend *much more sensitively* on $\overline{M}_w$ than on $\overline{M}_n$. Hence KTU examiners test whether you know *which* average is appropriate for a *given* measurement. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Setting up the Statistical Framework Let the polymer sample contain $N_i$ molecules having molecular weight $M_i$, where $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ runs over every distinct chain length present. Total number of molecules: $$N = \sum_{i} N_i$$ Total mass of the sample: $$m = \sum_{i} N_i M_i$$ The **number fraction** of species $i$ is: $$x_i = \frac{N_i}{\sum_{j} N_j} = \frac{N_i}{N}$$ The **weight fraction** of species $i$ is: $$w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{j} N_j M_j} = \frac{N_i M_i}{m}$$ Note that $w_i = x_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{\overline{M}_n}$, a relationship KTU students must remember. --- ### 2.2 Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_n$) $\overline{M}_n$ is the **simple arithmetic mean** of molecular weights weighted by the **number of molecules** of each species. It is the average measured by techniques that *count molecules* — colligative-property methods. $$\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} = \sum_i x_i M_i$$ **Methods that determine $\overline{M}_n$:** - Osmotic pressure (membrane & vapour-pressure osmometry) - End-group analysis - Cryoscopy / Ebullioscopy - Number of particles by light scattering extrapolation to zero angle *and* zero concentration is *not* used here. --- ### 2.3 Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_w$) $\overline{M}_w$ is the mean weighted by the **mass** of each species. It is the average measured by techniques sensitive to the *size* of molecules (light scattering, sedimentation). $$\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = \sum_i w_i M_i$$ **Methods that determine $\overline{M}_w$:** - Static (elastic) Light Scattering — the gold standard - Sedimentation equilibrium (also yields higher averages) - Small-angle X-ray / neutron scattering --- ### 2.4 Sedimentation-Average Molecular Weights Sedimentation in an ultracentrifuge gives **higher-order moments**. Two are routinely reported: $$\overline{M}_z = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^3}{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}$$ $$\overline{M}_{z+1} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^4}{\sum_i N_i M_i^3}$$ Both emphasize the **heaviest molecules** in the distribution and are extracted from sedimentation-equilibrium experiments. --- ### 2.5 Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_v$) Solution viscosity (intrinsic-viscosity measurement) gives an average that lies **between $\overline{M}_n$ and $\overline{M}_w$**: $$\overline{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha} = \left[\sum_i w_i M_i^{\alpha}\right]^{1/\alpha}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **Mark–Houwink exponent** ($0.5 \leq \alpha \leq 1.0$, typically 0.6–0.8 for flexible coils in good solvents; $\alpha = 1.0$ gives $\overline{M}_v = \overline{M}_w$). The Mark–Houwink equation itself is: $$[\eta] = K \cdot \overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha}$$ --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Quantity | Formula | Determined By | Typical SI Unit | |---|----------|---------|---------------|-----------------| | 1 | Number-Average $\overline{M}_n$ | $\sum_i x_i M_i = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Osmometry, end-group analysis | $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | | 2 | Weight-Average $\overline{M}_w$ | $\sum_i w_i M_i = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Light scattering | $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | | 3 | Z-Average $\overline{M}_z$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}$ | Sedimentation equilibrium | $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | | 4 | Z+1-Average $\overline{M}_{z+1}$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{4}}{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}$ | Sedimentation equilibrium | $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | | 5 | Viscosity-Average $\overline{M}_v$ | $\left[\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}$ | Solution viscometry | $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | | 6 | Polydispersity Index $\text{PDI}$ | $\overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n$ | Computed from $\overline{M}_n$ and $\overline{M}_w$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Mark–Houwink relation | $[\eta] = K \cdot \overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha}$ | Viscometry | $\text{dL g}^{-1}$ | | 8 | Ordering (always!) | $\overline{M}_n \leq \overline{M}_v \leq \overline{M}_w \leq \overline{M}_z \leq \overline{M}_{z+1}$ | General theorem | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition / Identity Check:** > For a **monodisperse** sample (all molecules equal in mass): > $\overline{M}_n = \overline{M}_w = \overline{M}_v = \overline{M}_z = \overline{M}_{z+1}$ and PDI = 1. > KTU examiners often test this as a "sanity check" question. ### 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility - **$\overline{M}_w$** controls melt viscosity in extrusion/injection moulding and mechanical strength of the final part. - **$\overline{M}_n$** controls fracture toughness and brittleness. - **$\overline{M}_v$** is *the cheapest* to measure and is used industrially for routine QC of polymers such as PET, PVC, and HDPE. - **PDI** tells the manufacturer how sharp/clean the polymerisation is — vital for medical-grade and fibre-grade materials. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Number-Average Molecular Weight **Starting principle:** $\overline{M}_n$ is the *total mass* divided by the *total number of molecules*. **Step 1 —** Consider $N$ molecules distributed over various species $i$. The total mass is: $$m = \sum_{i} N_i M_i$$ **Step 2 —** The total number of molecules is: $$N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} N_i$$ **Step 3 —** Divide total mass by total number of molecules: $$\overline{M}_n = \frac{m}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$ **Step 4 —** Substitute number fraction $x_i = N_i / \sum_j N_j$: $$\overline{M}_n = \sum_i x_i M_i$$ This is the closed-form expression for $\overline{M}_n$. ✔ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Weight-Average Molecular Weight **Starting principle:** Weight each species by *its own contribution to the mass*. **Step 1 —** The mass of species $i$ is $m_i = N_i M_i$. Its weight fraction is: $$w_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum_j m_j} = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$ **Step 2 —** Take the weighted average where weight $w_i$ is applied to molecular weight $M_i$: $$\overline{M}_w = \sum_i w_i M_i$$ **Step 3 —** Expand $w_i$: $$\overline{M}_w = \sum_i \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}\cdot M_i = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$ **Step 4 —** Recognising that $\sum_j N_j M_j = m$, the denominator is simply the total mass: $$\boxed{\;\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\;}$$ **Observation (key KTU point):** the *second moment* ($\sum N_i M_i^2$) of the distribution appears in the numerator — heavier chains are doubly emphasised. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight The intrinsic viscosity contribution of species $i$ is additive in dilute solution: $$[\eta] = \sum_i [\eta]_i = \sum_i K M_i^{\alpha}$$ (per unit mass of solute). For a polymer sample of mass $m = \sum N_i M_i$: $$[\eta] = \frac{\sum_i N_i \cdot K M_i^{\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\cdot \sum_i N_i M_i \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K\left(\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right)$$ But by definition $[\eta] = K\,\overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha}$. Equating the two: $$K\,\overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha} = K\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$ Cancelling $K$ and raising both sides to the power $1/\alpha$: $$\boxed{\;\overline{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}\;}$$ **Sanity check:** - If $\alpha = 1$: $\overline{M}_v = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \overline{M}_w$. ✔ - If $\alpha = 0$: $\overline{M}_v = \overline{M}_n$. ✔ (Limit case.) So $\overline{M}_v$ smoothly interpolates between $\overline{M}_n$ and $\overline{M}_w$ as $\alpha$ varies between 0 and 1. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** A polymer sample contains three fractions: > | Species $i$ | Number of molecules $N_i$ | Molecular weight $M_i$ (g/mol) | > |---|---|---| > | 1 | 100 | 10,000 | > | 2 | 50 | 50,000 | > | 3 | 10 | 1,00,000 | > > Calculate $\overline{M}_n$, $\overline{M}_w$, PDI, and $\overline{M}_v$ for $\alpha = 0.7$. **Step 1 — Tabulate the required sums:** $$\sum_i N_i M_i = 100(10{,}000) + 50(50{,}000) + 10(1{,}00{,}000)$$ $$= 1{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 36{,}00{,}000$$ $$\sum_i N_i M_i^2 = 100(10{,}000)^2 + 50(50{,}000)^2 + 10(1{,}00{,}000)^2$$ $$= 100(10^8) + 50(25\times 10^8) + 10(100\times 10^8)$$ $$= 10^{10} + 12.5\times10^{10} + 10\times10^{10} = 33.5\times10^{10}$$ $$\sum_i N_i = 100 + 50 + 10 = 160$$ $$\sum_i N_i M_i^{1.7} = 100(10{,}000)^{1.7} + 50(50{,}000)^{1.7} + 10(1{,}00{,}000)^{1.7}$$ Computing: $10^4^{1.7}=10^{6.8}=6.31\times10^6$; $5\times10^4^{1.7}=5^{1.7}\times10^{6.8}=3.40\times6.31\times10^6=2.15\times10^7$; $10^5^{1.7}=10^{8.5}=3.16\times10^8$. $$\sum N_i M_i^{1.7} = 100(6.31\times10^6) + 50(2.15\times10^7) + 10(3.16\times10^8)$$ $$= 6.31\times10^8 + 10.75\times10^8 + 31.6\times10^8 = 48.66\times10^8$$ **Step 2 — Compute averages:** $$\overline{M}_n = \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{160} = 22{,}500 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\overline{M}_w = \frac{33.5\times10^{10}}{36\times10^{5}} = \frac{33.5\times10^{10}}{3.6\times10^{6}} = 9.31\times10^{4} = 93{,}056 \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \frac{93{,}056}{22{,}500} \approx 4.14$$ $$\overline{M}_v = \left[\frac{48.66\times10^8}{36\times10^{5}}\right]^{1/0.7} = \left[1.352\times10^{4}\right]^{1.4286}$$ $$= (13520)^{1.4286} \approx 6.99\times10^{4} = 69{,}900 \;\text{g/mol}$$ **Verification of the KTU ordering rule:** $$\overline{M}_n\;(22{,}500) \;<\; \overline{M}_v\;(69{,}900) \;<\; \overline{M}_w\;(93{,}056)\;\;\checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Educational Insight:** The very large PDI (≈4.1) reveals that a *small number* of huge chains dominates the weight average — exactly the KTU board's favourite qualitative follow-up question. --- ### 3.5 Proof of the Inequalities (asked in 14-mark derivations) We must show $\overline{M}_n \leq \overline{M}_w \leq \overline{M}_z$. **Proof:** Consider the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality applied to sequences $\{\sqrt{N_i}M_i\}$ and $\{\sqrt{N_i}\}$: $$\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2 \;\leq\; \left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right)$$ Dividing both sides by $(\sum N_i M_i)(\sum N_i)$: $$\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} \;\leq\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$ That is exactly $\overline{M}_n \leq \overline{M}_w$. The other inequalities follow analogously. $\blacksquare$ --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Determination of Molecular-Weight Averages ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Polymer Sample

[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define number-average molecular weight ($\overline{M}_n$) and weight-average molecular weight ($\overline{M}_w$). State one experimental method that determines each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n$ (1 mark):** It is the *total mass* of all polymer molecules divided by the *total number* of molecules. $$\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$ **Weight-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_w$ (1 mark):** It is the average in which each molecular weight is *weighted by the mass contribution* of that species. $$\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$ **Experimental methods (1 mark):** - $\overline{M}_n$ → Membrane osmometry / vapour-pressure osmometry / end-group analysis. - $\overline{M}_w$ → Static (elastic) light scattering. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is polydispersity index? Why is $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$ always?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The polydispersity index (PDI) is the ratio $$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$$ For a perfectly uniform (monodisperse) polymer PDI = 1; commercial polymers typically have PDI = 1.5–4. **Justification (1.5 marks):** By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $$\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2 \;\leq\; \left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right)$$ Dividing appropriately yields $\overline{M}_n \leq \overline{M}_w$, with equality *only* when all $M_i$ are identical. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, with KTU internal choice) > Each Part-B question has internal choice as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Two independent options are provided. --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024] (14 marks)** **(a)** Derive expressions for the number-average ($\overline{M}_n$) and weight-average ($\overline{M}_w$) molecular weights starting from a heterogeneous polymer sample containing $N_i$ molecules of molecular weight $M_i$. Show that $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$ using Cauchy–Schwarz inequality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polymer contains: - 200 molecules of $M = 20{,}000$ g/mol, - 100 molecules of $M = 50{,}000$ g/mol, - 50 molecules of $M = 1{,}00{,}000$ g/mol. Calculate $\overline{M}_n$, $\overline{M}_w$, and PDI. Comment on the molecular-weight distribution. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Consider a polymer sample with $N_i$ molecules of molecular weight $M_i$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ **Step 1 — Total mass and total number (1 mark):** $$m = \sum_i N_i M_i \qquad,\qquad N_{\text{total}} = \sum_i N_i$$ **Step 2 — Number-average (1 mark):** $$\overline{M}_n = \frac{m}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$ **Step 3 — Weight fraction $w_i$ (1 mark):** $$w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$ **Step 4 — Weight-average (1 mark):** $$\overline{M}_w = \sum_i w_i M_i = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$ **Step 5 — Cauchy–Schwarz application (2 marks):** Take vectors $u_i = \sqrt{N_i} M_i$ and $v_i = \sqrt{N_i}$: $$\left(\sum_i u_i v_i\right)^2 \le \left(\sum_i u_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i v_i^2\right)$$ $$\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2 \le \left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right)$$ Dividing both sides by $(\sum N_i M_i)(\sum N_i)$ gives $\overline{M}_n \le \overline{M}_w$. Equality holds only when all $M_i$ are equal (monodisperse case). **(1 mark for the final inequality statement)** --- **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Tabulate (1 mark):** | $i$ | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^{2}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 200 | 20,000 | 40,00,000 | $8\times10^{10}$ | | 2 | 100 | 50,000 | 50,00,000 | $25\times10^{10}$ | | 3 | 50 | 1,00,000 | 50,00,000 | $50\times10^{10}$ | | **Σ** | **350** | — | **1.4 × 10⁷** | **83 × 10¹⁰** | **Step 2 — Compute $\overline{M}_n$ (2 marks):** $$\overline{M}_n = \frac{1.4\times10^7}{350} = 40{,}000\;\text{g/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\overline{M}_w$ (2 marks):** $$\overline{M}_w = \frac{83\times10^{10}}{1.4\times10^7} = \frac{8.3\times10^{11}}{1.4\times10^7} = 5.9286\times10^4 = 59{,}286\;\text{g/mol}$$ **Step 4 — Compute PDI (1 mark):** $$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \frac{59{,}286}{40{,}000} \approx 1.48$$ **Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):** PDI = 1.48 indicates a *moderately broad* molecular-weight distribution — characteristic of polymers synthesised by step-growth polymerisation. The presence of a small fraction of very long chains ($M_i = 1{,}00{,}000$) raises $\overline{M}_w$ significantly above $\overline{M}_n$. --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified] (14 marks)** **(a)** Define viscosity-average molecular weight ($\overline{M}_v$). Starting from the Mark–Houwink equation $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$, derive the expression for $\overline{M}_v$ in terms of the molecular-weight distribution. Show that $\overline{M}_n \leq \overline{M}_v \leq \overline{M}_w$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The intrinsic viscosity of a polymer solution at 25 °C in a given solvent is $[\eta] = 0.85\;\text{dL/g}$. The Mark–Houwink constants are $K = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{dL/g}$ and $\alpha = 0.70$. Calculate $\overline{M}_v$. Comment on the reliability of this average compared to $\overline{M}_n$ and $\overline{M}_w$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** $\overline{M}_v$ is the molecular weight obtained from intrinsic-viscosity measurements, lying between $\overline{M}_n$ and $\overline{M}_w$. **Step 2 — Intrinsic viscosity of a mixture (2 marks):** For dilute solution, contributions are additive on a per-mass basis: $$[\eta] = \sum_i \frac{N_i M_i}{m}\cdot K M_i^{\alpha} = \frac{K\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$ **Step 3 — Use Mark–Houwink (1 mark):** By definition $[\eta] = K\,\overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha}$. Equating: $$K\,\overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha} = \frac{K\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$ **Step 4 — Final form (1 mark):** $$\overline{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}$$ **Step 5 — Inequalities (2 marks):** - When $\alpha \to 0$: $\overline{M}_v \to \overline{M}_n$. - When $\alpha \to 1$: $\overline{M}_v \to \overline{M}_w$. - For $0 < \alpha < 1$: $\overline{M}_n \le \overline{M}_v \le \overline{M}_w$ (provable by Jensen's inequality applied to the convex function $x^{\alpha}$). --- **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Mark–Houwink equation (1 mark):** $$[\eta] = K\,\overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha} \;\Longrightarrow\; 0.85 = 4.5\times10^{-4}\cdot \overline{M}_v^{\,0.7}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange (2 marks):** $$\overline{M}_v^{\,0.7} = \frac{0.85}{4.5\times10^{-4}} = 1{,}888.89$$ **Step 3 — Take the 0.7-th root (2 marks):** $$\overline{M}_v = (1{,}888.89)^{1/0.7} = (1{,}888.89)^{1.4286}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\log_{10}\overline{M}_v = 1.4286 \times \log_{10}(1{,}888.89) = 1.4286 \times 3.2762 = 4.680$$ $$\overline{M}_v = 10^{4.680} \approx 4.79\times10^{4} \;\text{g/mol}$$ $$\boxed{\;\overline{M}_v \approx 4.79\times10^{4}\;\text{g/mol}\;}$$ **Step 4 — Comment on reliability (2 marks):** - $\overline{M}_v$ is **much easier** to measure than $\overline{M}_w$ (no need for a light-scattering photometer) and is the **routine industrial QC** parameter. - Since $\alpha$ typically lies between 0.6 and 0.8, $\overline{M}_v$ is **closer to $\overline{M}_w$** than to $\overline{M}_n$, so it correlates well with mechanical/viscoelastic properties. - However, $\overline{M}_v$ depends on the assumed values of $K$ and $\alpha$ — these are solvent-, temperature-, and polymer-specific; an error in $\alpha$ of 0.05 can produce a 20–30% error in $\overline{M}_v$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Mixing number fraction $x_i$ and weight fraction $w_i$.** Marks lost: 2–3 per question. > *Remember*: $w_i \neq x_i$; $w_i = x_i M_i/\overline{M}_n$. > 2. **Forgetting units.** $\overline{M}_n$, $\overline{M}_w$, $\overline{M}_v$ must carry the unit **g mol⁻¹** or equivalent. Marks lost: 1 per question. > 3. **Violating the inequality chain.** Writing $\overline{M}_n > \overline{M}_w$ loses 1 mark instantly. > 4. **Confusing Mark–Houwink parameters.** $K$ depends on polymer + solvent + temperature; $\alpha$ characterises chain stiffness in that solvent. Marks lost: 1–2. > 5. **In derivation questions**, *just stating the final formula* without intermediate steps (computing total mass, defining $w_i$, applying Cauchy–Schwarz) costs up to 4 of the 7 marks. > 6. **Numerical errors in powers**: When $M_i$ is large (10⁵ g/mol), even a tiny slip in $M_i^{1.7}$ cascades. Always tabulate first. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Polymer molecular weight is a *statistical average*** over a distribution of chain lengths — not a single number. - **Four essential averages:** $\overline{M}_n$ (number), $\overline{M}_w$ (weight), $\overline{M}_v$ (viscosity), $\overline{M}_z$ and $\overline{M}_{z+1}$ (sedimentation). - **Master formulas:** - $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \sum x_i M_i$ - $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i} = \sum w_i M_i$ - $\overline{M}_v = \left[\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}$ - $\overline{M}_z = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}$ - **The Golden Ordering (KTU favourite):** $$\overline{M}_n \;\le\; \overline{M}_v \;\le\; \overline{M}_w \;\le\; \overline{M}_z \;\le\; \overline{M}_{z+1}$$ - **Polydispersity Index $\text{PDI} = \overline{M}_w/\overline{M}_n$** → 1 for monodisperse, 1.5–4 for typical commercial polymers. - **Mark–Houwink equation** $[\eta] = K \overline{M}_v^{\,\alpha}$ links intrinsic viscosity to $\overline{M}_v$; $\alpha$ ranges from 0.5 (theta solvent) to 1.0 (rigid rod). - **Techniques:** Osmometry → $\overline{M}_n$; Light scattering → $\overline{M}_w$; Viscometry → $\overline{M}_v$; Ultracentrifuge → $\overline{M}_z$, $\overline{M}_{z+1}$. - **Practical use:** $\overline{M}_w$ controls mechanical strength and melt viscosity; $\overline{M}_n$ influences brittleness and end-group reactivity. - **Validity Check:** Cauchy–Schwarz inequality guarantees $\overline{M}_n \le \overline{M}_w$; Jensen's inequality guarantees $\overline{M}_n \le \overline{M}_v \le \overline{M}_w$. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute memory aid:** "*N is for Number of molecules, W is for Weight of molecules, V is for Viscosity (in-between), Z is for heavier than W.*" > Remember: $\overline{M}_n$ uses the **1st moment**, $\overline{M}_w$ uses the **2nd moment**, $\overline{M}_z$ uses the **3rd moment**.

topics.title: Experimental Methods for Molecular Weight Determination: End Group Analysis, Light Scattering Method, and Viscometry (Ostwald Viscometer) - Intrinsic Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Experimental Methods for Molecular Weight Determination: End Group Analysis, Light Scattering Method, and Viscometry (Ostwald Viscometer) - Intrinsic Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Molecular Weight Determination

The molecular weight of a polymer is not a single value but a **statistical average**, because synthetic polymers are polydisperse — chains of different lengths coexist in the same sample. Three experimentally accessible averages are:
- **Number-average molecular weight (M̄n)** — sensitive to the number of molecules.
- **Weight-average molecular weight (M̄w)** — sensitive to the mass contribution of each molecule.
- **Viscosity-average molecular weight (M̄v)** — derived from solution viscosity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Ordering Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> For any polydisperse polymer system:
> $$\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_v \le \bar{M}_w$$
> Equality holds only for a perfectly monodisperse sample.

---

## 1.2 End Group Analysis (Chemical Method)

### Formal KTU Definition
**End Group Analysis** is an absolute, chemical method for determining the **number-average molecular weight (M̄n)** of a linear polymer by quantitatively estimating the concentration of reactive functional end groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, –NH₂) per unit mass of polymer, using titration or spectroscopic techniques.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Headcount" Method
Imagine a classroom where every student holds exactly one colored flag (representing an end group). If you count the **number of flags** and divide it into the **total mass of all students**, you get the **average mass per student**. End group analysis works the same way: each polymer chain has a known number of chemical "flags" (end groups), so counting flags and weighing the polymer directly gives M̄n.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** End group analysis is restricted to **linear polymers** whose end groups are chemically distinct and quantitatively reactive. It cannot be applied to high-MW polymers (M̄n > ~50,000 g/mol) because the concentration of end groups becomes too small to measure accurately.

---

## 1.3 Light Scattering Method (Absolute Method)

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Light Scattering Method** determines the **weight-average molecular weight (M̄w)** by measuring the intensity of light scattered by polymer solutions at various angles and concentrations. It is based on the **Rayleigh–Debye–Zimm theory** of scattering.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Spotlight" Method
Picture a dust-filled room lit by a torch. Larger dust particles scatter more light (appear brighter) than smaller ones. Similarly, in a polymer solution, larger macromolecules scatter more light than smaller ones. By measuring the scattered light intensity, we estimate the *weighted* (mass-biased) size of molecules — which is exactly what M̄w represents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral Note:** Light scattering experiments require monochromatic, polarized light — typically from a **mercury lamp (λ = 546.1 nm)** filtered through appropriate optics.

---

## 1.4 Viscometry and the Ostwald Viscometer

### Formal KTU Definition
