- **Rubber elasticity:** $G = \nu_e R T$ — modulus *increases* with temperature (Gough–Joule effect).
- **HDPE vs LDPE** is the canonical example of architecture controlling properties in chemically identical polymers.
- **Examples to remember:** HDPE (linear), LDPE (branched), vulcanized rubber (lightly crosslinked), Bakelite (densely crosslinked thermoset).
- **Architecture always overrides monomer chemistry** in determining whether a polymer can be melt-processed, recycled, or made elastic.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Material selection begins with *architecture*, not chemistry.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystallinity and Glass Transition Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crystallinity and Glass Transition Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Crystallinity in Polymers

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Crystallinity in polymers is the degree of **long-range three-dimensional periodic ordering** of polymer chain segments into regions called **crystallites** (or lamellae). Since polymers are long-chain molecules, perfect crystallization is geometrically impossible; therefore, a semi-crystalline polymer consists of an **interpenetrating mixture** of ordered crystallites embedded in a disordered amorphous matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Crystallinity ($\% \chi_c$):** The fractional ratio of the mass (or volume) of the crystalline phase to the total mass (or volume) of the polymer sample. This value typically lies between **0 % (fully amorphous, e.g., atactic polystyrene)** and approximately **80–95 % (highly crystalline, e.g., high-density polyethylene, HDPE)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of cooked spaghetti poured into a square box.
- If you push down randomly, the noodles lie every which way — that is an **amorphous polymer** (rubbery, transparent).
- If you carefully align the noodles parallel to one another in neat bundles, the bundles pack tightly while the gaps remain disordered — that is a **semi-crystalline polymer** (stronger, opaque).
- You can never get 100 % alignment because the noodles are long, kinked, and tangled — just as polymer chains cannot crystallize fully due to chain length, entanglements, and stereochemical defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystallite (Lamella):** A plate-like ordered region, typically 10–20 nm thick, formed by folded polymer chains. Multiple lamellae may organize into larger superstructures called **spherulites** (visible under crossed polarizers, ~1 µm – 1 mm in diameter).

---

### 1.2 Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer undergoes a reversible transition from a hard, glassy, brittle state to a soft, rubbery, flexible state. It is a **second-order thermodynamic transition** characterized by a step change in specific volume, specific heat ($C_p$), and thermal expansion coefficient ($\alpha$) — *not* a true thermodynamic phase transition with latent heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** $T_g$ is *not* a sharp melting point. It occurs over a temperature range (typically 5–20 °C wide) and depends strongly on the **rate of cooling/heating**. The faster you cool, the higher the apparent $T_g$.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a tray of marbles on a vibrating table.
- At low vibration (low $T$): the marbles are frozen in place — they cannot move past each other. The system is **glassy** and rigid.
- As you increase vibration (raise $T$): at a critical vibration level, the marbles suddenly gain enough wiggle-room to slide past one another. The system becomes **rubbery**.
- The vibration level at which this "unlocking" happens is analogous to $T_g$. It is not a unique number — if you wait longer, the unlocking happens at a slightly lower vibration (the **kinetic** nature of $T_g$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values:**
> - Polystyrene (PS): $T_g \approx 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA): $T_g \approx 105\ ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Polyethylene (PE): $T_g \approx -120\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (well below room temperature)
> - Natural Rubber (Polyisoprene): $T_g \approx -73\ ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Polycarbonate (PC): $T_g \approx 150\ ^\circ\text{C}$

---

### 1.3 Why Crystallinity and $T_g$ Matter Together

Both phenomena govern **mechanical strength, optical clarity, thermal resistance, permeability, and processing behavior**. A single polymer — say, polyethylene — can be:
- A **wax** at $T < T_g$ and below its melting point,
- A **flexible plastic film** at room temperature (since $T_{room} > T_g$ but $< T_m$),
- A **viscous melt** at $T > T_m$.

Understanding where these transitions lie on the temperature axis is the **single most important task** in polymer material selection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Origin of Crystallinity

For a polymer to crystallize, its chains must adopt regular conformations (usually all-*trans* zigzag or helical) and pack efficiently. Crystallization is hindered by:

1. **Tacticity** — Isotactic and syndiotactic polymers crystallize; atactic polymers do not.
2. **Chain regularity** — Co-monomers, branches, and random substitution disrupt periodicity.
3. **Chain flexibility** — Highly rigid chains (e.g., aromatic polyimides) crystallize with difficulty.
4. **Cross-linking** — Permanent chemical bonds between chains prevent folding.
5. **Cooling rate** — Fast quenching yields low crystallinity; slow annealing yields higher crystallinity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fringed-Micelle Model (Classical):** Crystalline regions are interspersed with amorphous regions, where each polymer chain passes through multiple crystallites — like a thread stitching between ordered patches.
> **The Folded-Chain Lamella Model (Modern):** A single chain folds back and forth on itself to form a thin platelet (~10–20 nm thick). This is the dominant model supported by electron microscopy and NMR.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Frameworks for $T_g$

#### (A) Free-Volume Theory (Fox & Flory, 1950)
The total specific volume $V$ of a polymer is partitioned into:
- **Occupied volume** ($V_{occ}$): the volume physically filled by the polymer molecules.
- **Free volume** ($V_f$): the empty "wiggle room" between chains, allowing segmental motion.

$$V = V_{occ} + V_f$$

As temperature increases, both $V_{occ}$ and $V_f$ increase, but $V_f$ remains approximately constant until $T_g$. Above $T_g$, $V_f$ grows linearly with $T$. At $T_g$, the free volume reaches a critical minimum fraction $f_g$ required for cooperative segmental motion.

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $\alpha_f$ is the free-volume thermal expansion coefficient.

#### (B) Kinetic Theory (Gibbs–DiMarzio, 1958)
$T_g$ is the temperature at which the polymer falls out of thermodynamic equilibrium — configurational entropy decreases until a frozen-in state is reached. Below $T_g$, the polymer is a **non-ergodic glass**; the measured $T_g$ depends on the observation time.

#### (C) Thermodynamic Theory
A true equilibrium second-order transition exists below the kinetic $T_g$, but it is experimentally inaccessible because the relaxation times become astronomically large.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS — memorize the variables, units, and physical meaning for the KTU exam.**

| # | Equation | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Units | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\% \chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | $\rho_s$ = sample density, $\rho_a$ = amorphous density, $\rho_c$ = crystalline density | dimensionless (%) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\Delta H_f = \chi_c \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ$ | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ = heat of fusion for 100 % crystalline sample | $\text{J/g}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | Fox equation: $w_i$ = weight fraction, $T_{g,i}$ = glass temp of component $i$ | K | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Fox–Flory equation: $\bar{M}_n$ = number-average molecular weight | K | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | WLF equation: $a_T$ = shift factor, $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $T_g \approx \tfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (in Kelvin, for symmetric polymers) | Boyer–Beaman rule | K | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $\alpha_f = \alpha_L - \alpha_G$ | Free-volume expansion coefficient difference | $\text{K}^{-1}$ | ⭐⭐ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation Pitfall:** In WLF, the **universal constants** $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K apply **only** when $T_g$ is used as the reference temperature. If a different reference temperature is chosen, the constants must be re-fitted.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Injection molding**: Must cool the melt through both $T_m$ and $T_g$ zones; cooling rate controls crystallinity → dimensional shrinkage.
- **Film blowing**: Biaxial orientation above $T_g$ (but below $T_m$) produces **crystallization-induced alignment** → high-strength BOPP, PET films.
- **Tire design**: Natural rubber has $T_g \approx -73\ ^\circ\text{C}$, retaining elasticity at sub-zero temperatures — this is why synthetic rubbers with low $T_g$ are sought.
- **Optical polymers**: Amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PC) are transparent; semi-crystalline ones (HDPE, PP, Nylon) are translucent/opaque due to light scattering at spherulite boundaries.
- **3D printing (FDM)**: Glass- and crystallization-induced warpage must be controlled via heated build chambers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Degree of Crystallinity from Density Data

**Starting Principle:** The specific volume of a semi-crystalline polymer is the weighted average of the amorphous and crystalline specific volumes.

Let $v$ be the specific volume of the sample, $v_a$ that of 100 % amorphous polymer, and $v_c$ that of 100 % crystalline polymer. Define the volume fraction of crystalline phase as $\phi_c$ and amorphous as $\phi_a = 1 - \phi_c$.

**Step 1 — Linear mixing rule for specific volume:**

$$v = \phi_c \, v_c + (1 - \phi_c) \, v_a$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\phi_c$:**

$$v - v_a = \phi_c (v_c - v_a)$$

$$\phi_c = \dfrac{v - v_a}{v_c - v_a}$$

**Step 3 — Convert from specific volume to density** using $v = 1/\rho$:

$$\phi_c = \dfrac{(1/\rho_s) - (1/\rho_a)}{(1/\rho_c) - (1/\rho_a)}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\rho_a \rho_c$:**

$$\phi_c = \dfrac{\rho_a \rho_c \left[(1/\rho_s) - (1/\rho_a)\right]}{\rho_a \rho_c \left[(1/\rho_c) - (1/\rho_a)\right]} = \dfrac{\rho_a \rho_c / \rho_s - \rho_c}{\rho_a - \rho_c}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to weight fraction** $\chi_c = w_c$ using $w_c = \phi_c \rho_c / \rho_s$:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

This is Equation (1) in the cheat sheet. For polyethylene, $\rho_a = 0.855\ \text{g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_c = 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A sample of polyethylene has $\rho_s = 0.92\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_a = 0.855\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 1.00\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{1.00 \times (0.92 - 0.855)}{0.92 \times (1.00 - 0.855)} = \dfrac{0.065}{0.1334} \approx 0.487$$

So the sample is **48.7 % crystalline** by weight.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Fox Equation for Copolymer $T_g$

**Starting Principle:** For two monomers that copolymerize randomly, the **free volume** at $T_g$ of the copolymer is the linear combination of free volumes of the two homopolymers.

**Step 1 — Free volume at $T_g$ (Fox–Flory assumption):**

$$f(T_g^{copo}) = f(T_{g,1}) \cdot w_1 + f(T_{g,2}) \cdot w_2$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the common occupied-volume fraction:**

$$f_g - \alpha_{f,1} T_{g,1} = (f_g - \alpha_{f,1} T_{g,1}) w_1 + (f_g - \alpha_{f,2} T_{g,2}) w_2$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $f_g$ terms (assuming constant critical free volume):**

$$- \alpha_{f,1} T_{g,1} = -\alpha_{f,1} T_{g,1} w_1 - \alpha_{f,2} T_{g,2} w_2$$

**Step 4 — Group by weight fraction** and divide by the free-volume expansion coefficients. Empirically, setting $\alpha_{f,1} \approx \alpha_{f,2}$ yields:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g^{copo}} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

This is Equation (3). It is the form most commonly tested in KTU.

**Numerical Verification:**
Styrene ($T_{g,1} = 373\ \text{K}$) copolymerized 60:40 by weight with butadiene ($T_{g,2} = 188\ \text{K}$) — the classic SBR rubber.

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{0.6}{373} + \dfrac{0.4}{188} = 0.001608 + 0.002128 = 0.003736$$

$$T_g = \dfrac{1}{0.003736} \approx 267.7\ \text{K} \approx -5.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

This matches experimental $T_g$ values of SBR tires (~ -50 to 0 °C depending on composition).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Fox–Flory Molecular Weight Equation

**Starting Principle:** End-groups of polymer chains contribute extra free volume. A higher concentration of chain ends (low $\bar{M}_n$) lowers $T_g$.

**Step 1 — Free-volume contribution from chain ends:**

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g) + \beta \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{\bar{M}_n}$$

where $\beta$ is a constant capturing end-group free volume per gram of polymer.

**Step 2 — Set the condition for glass transition** ($f$ at $T_g$ is fixed):

$$f_g = f_g + \alpha_f (T_g - T_g) + \beta \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{\bar{M}_n} \quad \text{(at } T_g\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $T_g$ itself depends on $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$$

where $K = \beta \rho / \alpha_f$ is a polymer-specific constant (e.g., for PS, $K \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ K·g/mol).

**Step 4 — Asymptotic behavior:** As $\bar{M}_n \to \infty$, $T_g \to T_{g,\infty}$, the value for an infinitely long chain.

**Numerical Example:**
For polystyrene, $T_{g,\infty} = 373\ \text{K}$ and $K = 1.0 \times 10^5$ K·g/mol. Find $T_g$ at $\bar{M}_n = 10^4$ g/mol.

$$T_g = 373 - \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^4} = 373 - 10 = 363\ \text{K} = 90\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

This shows why low-molecular-weight PS is "softer" than high-molecular-weight PS.

---

### 3.4 WLF Equation — Origin of $C_1$ and $C_2$

The **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF)** equation expresses how the viscoelastic shift factor $a_T$ changes with temperature relative to $T_g$.

**Step 1 — Doolittle equation** for viscosity $\eta$:

$$\eta = A \exp\left(B \dfrac{V - V_f}{V_f}\right) = A \exp\left(\dfrac{B}{f}\right)$$

where $f = V_f / V$ is the fractional free volume.

**Step 2 — Assume $f$ varies linearly above $T_g$:**

$$f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio** $\eta(T) / \eta(T_g) = a_T$:

$$\log a_T = \log \dfrac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)} = \dfrac{B}{2.303}\left(\dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{f_g}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the linear free-volume relation:**

$$\log a_T = -\dfrac{B}{2.303 f_g} \cdot \dfrac{T - T_g}{f_g / \alpha_f + (T - T_g)}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the universal constants:**

$$C_1 = \dfrac{B}{2.303 f_g} \approx 17.44 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_g \approx 0.025$$

$$C_2 = \dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} \approx 51.6\ \text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "universal" values imply that all polymers share the same fractional free volume at $T_g$ — approximately 2.5 %.** This is one of the most remarkable empirical regularities in polymer physics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Phase Transitions vs. Temperature (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crystalline Solid Region] -->|Heat to T_m| B[Semi-crystalline or Molten Above T_m]
    B -->|Cool slowly through T_m| A
    A -.->|Amorphous regions only| C[Glass Transition at T_g]
    C -->|Cool below T_g| D[Hard Glassy State]
    D -->|Heat to T_g| C
    E[Amorphous Polymer] -->|Heat to T_g| F[Rubbery State]
    F -->|Heat further to T_f| G[Viscous Flow / Melt]
    F -->|Cool below T_g| H[Glassy Brittle State]
    H -->|Heat above T_g| F
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style D fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#595959
    style F fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d46b08
    style G fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00000
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Crystalline melting ($T_m$) and glass transition ($T_g$) are **independent** events. Crystalline regions *bypass* the glass transition entirely (going directly solid → melt). Only the amorphous fraction undergoes $T_g$.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Fringed-Micelle vs. Folded-Chain Lamella Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FML["Fringed Micelle Model (Classical)"]
        F1[Crystallite A] --- F2[Amorphous Tie Chain] --- F3[Crystallite B]
        F2 --- F4[Crystallite C]
    end
    subgraph FCL["Folded Chain Lamella Model (Modern)"]
        L1[Lamella 1] --> L2[Chain Folds Back] --> L3[Lamella 1 Continues]
        L3 --> L4[Loop Exits] --> L5[Adjacent Lamella 2]
        L5 --> L6[Folds and Returns]
    end
    style FML fill:#f0f5ff,stroke:#2c5282
    style FCL fill:#fff5f0,stroke:#c05621
```

> **Key insight:** Both models coexist — a real polymer has long tie-molecules that may thread through several lamellae, blending features of both.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Free Volume Schematic Across $T_g$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Below T_g: Free Volume locked at ~2.5 % — Segments CANNOT move — Glassy State"] --> P2["At T_g: Critical free volume reached — Cooperative segmental motion ON"]
    P2 --> P3["Above T_g: Free volume grows linearly with T — Rubber-like behavior"]
    P3 --> P4["Near T_m or T_f: Free volume large — Viscous flow possible"]
    P1 -.->|Heat| P2
    P2 -.->|Heat| P3
    P3 -.->|Heat further| P4
    style P1 fill:#b3c6e7,stroke:#1a3d7c
    style P2 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#bf9000
    style P3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#cc5500
    style P4 fill:#e06666,stroke:#990000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The "size of the holes" between molecules (free volume) is what determines whether a polymer behaves as a glass, a rubber, or a viscous fluid.

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Block Architecture of $T_g$ Determination by DSC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer Sample and Reference in DSC Pans] --> B[Temperature Programmed Ramp 10 K per min]
    B --> C[Heat Flow Difference Recorded as Function of T]
    C --> D{Identify Transition}
    D -->|Step shift in C_p| E[T_g Detected Midpoint of Step]
    D -->|Endothermic Peak| F[T_m Detected Peak Onset or Peak Maximum]
    G[Crystallization Exotherm on Cooling] --> D
    style A fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587
    style B fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000
    style E fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125
    style F fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** DSC is the workhorse technique. $T_g$ shows a *step* (no latent heat), while $T_m$ shows a *peak* (latent heat of fusion). Recognizing the difference is a KTU-favorite question.

---

### 4.5 Diagram E — Effect of Molecular Weight on $T_g$ (Fox–Flory Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Molecular Weight M_n on x axis g per mol"] --> Y["T_g on y axis K"]
    Y --> Q["Curve rises steeply at low M_n"]
    Q --> R["Asymptotic plateau at T_g infinity for very high M_n"]
    R --> S["K divided by M_n is the offset from plateau"]
    style X fill:#d9d9d9
    style Y fill:#d9d9d9
    style Q fill:#fce5cd
    style R fill:#d9ead3
    style S fill:#cfe2f3
```

> **Quantitative reading:** Doubling $\bar{M}_n$ from $10^4$ to $2 \times 10^4$ raises $T_g$ by $K/(2\times 10^4) = 5\ \text{K}$. Above $\sim 10^5$ g/mol, $T_g$ becomes essentially independent of $\bar{M}_n$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the glass transition temperature $T_g$. Why is it not considered a true thermodynamic phase transition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (2 marks):* The glass transition temperature $T_g$ is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state, accompanied by a step change in specific heat $C_p$, thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha$, and free volume.
- *Why not true thermodynamic transition (1 mark):* It is a **kinetic, second-order transition** with no latent heat and no discontinuity in entropy or enthalpy — only changes in their derivatives. The measured $T_g$ depends on cooling rate; faster cooling yields higher apparent $T_g$.

---

**Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between crystallites and spherulites in a semi-crystalline polymer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Crystallite / Lamella (1.5 marks):* A plate-like, ordered region ~10–20 nm thick formed by folded polymer chains. Building block of crystalline order.
- *Spherulite (1.5 marks):* A spherical superstructure (~1 µm – 1 mm in diameter) composed of many lamellae radiating outward from a central nucleation point, separated by amorphous regions. Visible under optical microscopy with crossed polarizers as a characteristic "Maltese cross" pattern.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** 
**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — With the help of free-volume theory, explain the glass transition phenomenon. Derive the expression for free volume as a function of temperature.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — A sample of high-density polyethylene has the following data: $\rho_s = 0.965\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_a = 0.855\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\Delta H_f^\circ = 293\ \text{J/g}$. The DSC-measured heat of fusion of the sample is $\Delta H_f = 175\ \text{J/g}$. Compute (i) the degree of crystallinity from density data, (ii) the degree of crystallinity from DSC data, and (iii) comment on the agreement.

---

##### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**Free-Volume Theory of Glass Transition (5 marks):**

The total specific volume $V$ of an amorphous polymer is the sum of:
1. **Occupied volume $V_{occ}$**: the volume physically occupied by the polymer molecules themselves.
2. **Free volume $V_f$**: the empty space between molecules that allows chain segments to wiggle and rearrange.

$$V = V_{occ} + V_f$$

**Below $T_g$:** Free volume is essentially "frozen" at a critical value $V_{f,g}$. Chain segments cannot execute cooperative motion. The polymer behaves as a rigid glass.

**Above $T_g$:** Free volume increases linearly with temperature, enabling segmental motion. The polymer behaves as a rubber.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Define fractional free volume $f = V_f / V$ and occupied fraction $f_{occ} = 1 - f$. Below $T_g$, $f \approx f_g$ (a constant ~0.025). Above $T_g$:

$$f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $\alpha_f$ is the free-volume thermal expansion coefficient (difference between expansion coefficients above and below $T_g$):

$$\alpha_f = \alpha_L - \alpha_G$$

The transition occurs because at $T_g$ the free volume reaches a critical threshold required for cooperative motion of ~10–20 monomer units.

---

##### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- $\rho_s = 0.965\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_a = 0.855\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_c = 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $\Delta H_f^\circ = 293\ \text{J/g}$
- $\Delta H_f = 175\ \text{J/g}$

**(i) Degree of crystallinity from density (3 marks):**

$$\chi_c^{(\rho)} = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} = \dfrac{1.000 \times (0.965 - 0.855)}{0.965 \times (1.000 - 0.855)}$$

$$\chi_c^{(\rho)} = \dfrac{0.110}{0.965 \times 0.145} = \dfrac{0.110}{0.1399} \approx 0.786 \text{ or } 78.6\ \%$$

**(ii) Degree of crystallinity from DSC (3 marks):**

$$\chi_c^{(DSC)} = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100 = \dfrac{175}{293} \times 100 \approx 59.7\ \%$$

**(iii) Comment on agreement (1 mark):**
The two values differ by ~19 %. This discrepancy is common because:
- DSC depends on the accuracy of $\Delta H_f^\circ$ for a hypothetical 100 % crystalline polymer.
- Density measurements require perfect knowledge of $\rho_a$ and $\rho_c$.
- The real polymer may contain voids, oriented regions, or non-equilibrium conformations.
- The accepted "true" crystallinity is usually an average of multiple techniques (XRD, density, DSC, NMR).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the WLF equation. What are its universal constants and what physical meaning do they carry? Discuss its engineering applications in polymer processing.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]** — A random copolymer is made from methyl methacrylate ($T_{g,1} = 378\ \text{K}$) and butyl acrylate ($T_{g,2} = 219\ \text{K}$) in weight ratio 35 : 65. Using the Fox equation, calculate the $T_g$ of the copolymer and comment on whether the resulting polymer will be useful as a coating adhesive at room temperature (25 °C).

---

##### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**The WLF Equation (4 marks):**

$$\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

where $a_T$ is the **time–temperature shift factor** that horizontally shifts viscoelastic curves (e.g., $G'$, $G''$, $\tan \delta$) measured at different temperatures onto a single master curve at a reference temperature $T_g$.

**Universal Constants (2 marks):**
- $C_1 \approx 17.44$
- $C_2 \approx 51.6\ \text{K}$

These are "universal" in the sense that they apply to virtually all amorphous polymers when $T_g$ is the reference. They encode two physical facts:
- $C_2 = f_g / \alpha_f \approx 51.6\ \text{K}$ implies $f_g \approx 0.025$ (fractional free volume at $T_g$).
- $C_1 = B / (2.303 f_g) \approx 17.44$ reflects the Doolittle equation parameter $B \approx 1$.

**Engineering Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Master-curve generation** for long-term creep and stress relaxation from short-term DMA tests.
2. **Predicting processing windows**: molding, extrusion, and thermoforming temperatures can be related to relaxation times.
3. **Design of rubber components**: predicting stiffness at low or high service temperatures from data at one reference temperature.

---

##### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:** $w_1 = 0.35$, $T_{g,1} = 378\ \text{K}$ (PMMA); $w_2 = 0.65$, $T_{g,2} = 219\ \text{K}$ (PBA).

**Apply Fox equation (5 marks):**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}} = \dfrac{0.35}{378} + \dfrac{0.65}{219}$$

$$= 9.259 \times 10^{-4} + 2.968 \times 10^{-3} = 3.894 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g = \dfrac{1}{3.894 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 256.8\ \text{K} = -16.4\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Comment on coating adhesive use (2 marks):**

For a pressure-sensitive adhesive coating, the polymer must be **above its $T_g$ at room temperature** so it can flow and wet surfaces under light pressure (tack).
- Service temperature: 25 °C = 298 K
- $T_g^{copo} = 256.8\ \text{K}$
- Difference: $298 - 256.8 = +41\ \text{K}$ above $T_g$

This is an excellent position for a **tacky adhesive** — well within the rubbery plateau but not so far above $T_g$ as to lose cohesive strength. The copolymer will function as a useful coating adhesive at room temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Mixing °C and K in the Fox equation.** Always convert to Kelvin before plugging in. Many students write 378 °C and lose 1–2 marks for a units error.
> 2. **Confusing weight fraction with mole fraction.** The Fox equation is given in *weight fraction* form. If the problem gives mole fractions, you must first convert using monomer molecular weights.
> 3. **Ignoring the WLF reference temperature convention.** Students often quote $C_1 = 17.44$ without specifying that the reference is $T_g$. If the reference is a different $T$, the constants change.
> 4. **Forgetting the rate-dependence of $T_g$.** Quoting a single value without acknowledging that it depends on cooling rate loses a mark in "explain" type answers.
> 5. **In DSC problems**, using peak onset vs. peak maximum for $T_m$ inconsistently. KTU examiners expect **peak onset** for $T_m$ and **midpoint** of the step for $T_g$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-fire revision pointers for the KTU 2024 exam hall:**

- **Crystallinity $\% \chi_c$** is always between 0 % and ~95 % — never 100 % for polymers.
- **Semi-crystalline polymer = crystallites + amorphous matrix**, with **tie molecules** linking them.
- **Spherulites** are micron-scale; **lamellae** are nanometer-scale; **unit cell** is angstrom-scale.
- **$T_g$** is for **amorphous** phase; **$T_m$** is for **crystalline** phase. They are *separate* transitions.
- **Below $T_g$**: glassy, brittle, rigid; small free volume.
- **Above $T_g$**: rubbery, flexible; free volume increases with $T$.
- **Boyer–Beaman rule**: $T_g / T_m \approx 2/3$ (in Kelvin) — useful sanity check.
- **Fox equation**: reciprocal additivity of weight fractions — memorize this form.
- **Fox–Flory equation**: $T_g$ rises with $\bar{M}_n$, plateauing at $\bar{M}_n \to \infty$.
- **WLF equation**: valid in the range $T_g < T < T_g + 100\ \text{K}$; outside this range, use Arrhenius.
- **Universal constants**: $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$ — only with $T_g$ as reference.
- **Free volume at $T_g$** $\approx 2.5\ \%$ of total volume for nearly all polymers.
- **Crystallization enhancers**: isotacticity, regular head-to-tail structure, slow cooling, annealing above $T_g$.
- **Crystallization inhibitors**: atactic configuration, bulky side groups, cross-linking, rapid quenching, plasticizers.
- **Measuring techniques** (mention at least one in descriptive answers):
  - Crystallinity: **XRD** (sharp peaks + amorphous halo), **DSC** ($\Delta H_f$), **Density gradient column**, **NMR**.
  - $T_g$: **DSC** (step in $C_p$), **DMA** (peak in $\tan \delta$ or drop in $E'$), **Dilatometry** (slope change in $V$ vs $T$).
- **Rate of measurement matters!** Always quote heating/cooling rate when reporting $T_g$ values (KTU standard: 10 K/min for DSC).
- **Engineering impact**: amorphous = transparent, isotropic, no melting peak; semi-crystalline = opaque, stronger, has clear melting point, can be oriented for strength.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Crystallinity and Glass Transition Temperature.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties: Elasticity, Viscoelasticity, and Yield
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties: Elasticity, Viscoelasticity, and Yield

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mechanical Properties — The Big Picture

When a polymer is subjected to an external force, it responds in a characteristic way. The three foundational mechanical responses studied in this module are:

- **Elasticity** — Instantaneous, fully recoverable deformation governed by Hooke's law.
- **Viscoelasticity** — Time-dependent, partially recoverable deformation that combines viscous flow and elastic stretching.
- **Yield** — The critical stress at which the polymer transitions from elastic (reversible) to plastic (permanent) deformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polymers are unique because their mechanical response lies on a *spectrum* — purely elastic (a Hookean spring) on one end and purely viscous (a Newtonian dashpot) on the other. **Real polymers are somewhere in between**, and understanding this spectrum is the key to polymer engineering.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions

**Elasticity** is the ability of a material to return to its original shape immediately after the removal of an applied stress. For an ideal elastic polymer, the strain is proportional to the applied stress, and the relationship is time-independent.

**Viscoelasticity** is the property of a material that exhibits both viscous (fluid-like) and elastic (solid-like) characteristics when deformed. The deformation depends on **both the magnitude of the stress and the rate (time) at which it is applied**.

**Yield** is the point on the stress–strain curve beyond which the polymer undergoes permanent, non-recoverable (plastic) deformation. The corresponding stress is called the **yield stress (σ_y)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogies (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Silly Putty (Viscoelasticity):** Take a piece of silly putty. If you pull it quickly, it **snaps** (elastic behavior). If you let it sit on a table, it slowly **flows** into a puddle (viscous behavior). This dual personality is **viscoelasticity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Rubber Band vs. Plastic Ruler (Elasticity):** Stretch a rubber band and release it — it snaps back instantly. This is **elastic recovery**. Bend a plastic ruler and release — the same thing happens but with a different modulus. Both are elastic but with very different stiffness values (Young's modulus).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Aluminum Can (Yield):** Take an empty aluminum can and press on its side. Initially, it springs back (elastic). But press too hard, and it **dents permanently**. The threshold force is the **yield point**. Polymers behave similarly — beyond yield, the deformation is permanent.

### 1.4 Key Metrics and Standard Parameters

- **Young's Modulus (E)** — Units: **Pa** or **MPa** — Measures stiffness.
- **Shear Modulus (G)** — Units: **Pa** — Resistance to shear deformation.
- **Viscosity (η)** — Units: **Pa·s** — Resistance to flow.
- **Relaxation Time (τ = η/E)** — Units: **seconds** — Characteristic time for a material to respond.
- **Yield Stress (σ_y)** — Units: **Pa** or **MPa** — Onset of plastic deformation.
- **Creep Compliance (J(t))** — Units: **Pa⁻¹** — Time-dependent strain per unit stress.
- **Relaxation Modulus (G(t))** — Units: **Pa** — Time-dependent stress per unit strain.

### 1.5 The Three Mechanical Elements

The mechanical behavior of polymers is modeled using three fundamental building blocks:

| Element | Symbol | Equation | Physical Realization |
|---------|--------|----------|----------------------|
| **Spring** (Hookean) | — | σ = Eε | Steel wire, rubber band |
| **Dashpot** (Newtonian) | — | σ = η(dε/dt) | Shock absorber oil, glycerol |
| **Friction Slider** (Saint-Venant) | — | σ = σ_y when ε evolves | Dry friction contact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** By **combining** these elements in series or in parallel, we can model **any** real polymer's behavior. The Maxwell model (spring + dashpot in series) captures **stress relaxation**, while the Kelvin–Voigt model (spring + dashpot in parallel) captures **creep**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hookean Elasticity in Polymers

For small deformations, an ideal elastic polymer obeys **Hooke's Law**:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where **σ** is the applied stress, **ε** is the resulting strain, and **E** is Young's modulus. The deformation is **instantaneous**, **reversible**, and **linear** in stress.

In three dimensions, the generalized form is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \cdot \delta_{ij} \cdot \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \cdot \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where **λ** and **μ** are the **Lamé constants**, and **μ** is identical to the shear modulus **G**.

**The 'Why':** The polymer chains stretch along the stress direction but recover once the stress is removed because the coiled chains possess **internal conformational entropy**. When stretched, the chains lose entropy; the second law of thermodynamics drives them back to the high-entropy coiled state.

### 2.2 Viscoelasticity — The Core of Polymer Mechanics

Polymers are **never** purely elastic or purely viscous. Their mechanical response is time-dependent, and two experiments reveal this:

#### 2.2.1 Creep Experiment
- Apply a **constant stress σ₀** at time t = 0.
- Measure strain **ε(t)** as a function of time.
- The ratio **J(t) = ε(t)/σ₀** is the **creep compliance**.
- After removing the stress, some strain is recovered (elastic part) and some remains (viscous part).

#### 2.2.2 Stress Relaxation Experiment
- Apply a **constant strain ε₀** at time t = 0.
- Measure stress **σ(t)** as a function of time.
- The ratio **G(t) = σ(t)/ε₀** is the **relaxation modulus**.
- The stress decays as chains rearrange to relieve internal tension.

**The 'How':** Viscoelasticity arises because polymer chains have **finite mobility** — they take time to untangle, rotate around backbone bonds, and reconfigure. This molecular motion is **thermally activated** and follows Arrhenius-type kinetics.

### 2.3 The Maxwell Model (Stress Relaxation)

A spring (E) and a dashpot (η) connected **in series** form the Maxwell element. Under applied strain, both elements experience the **same stress**, but the strains add:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

Rearranging into the canonical Maxwell equation:

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = \eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

where **τ = η/E** is the **relaxation time**.

**Engineering Utility:** Used to model **stress relaxation** in polymer melts, elastomers above Tg, and amorphous polymers under high-frequency loading.

### 2.4 The Kelvin–Voigt Model (Creep)

A spring (E) and a dashpot (η) connected **in parallel** form the Kelvin–Voigt element. Under applied stress, both elements experience the **same strain**, but the stresses add:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$$

This gives the Kelvin–Voigt constitutive equation:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used to model **creep** in solid polymers below Tg, damped vibration in polymers, and the long-term deformation of structural plastics.

### 2.5 The Standard Linear Solid (SLS) Model

Combining a Maxwell element in parallel with a free spring produces the **Standard Linear Solid (Zener) model**, which captures **both creep and stress relaxation**:

$$\sigma + \tau_{\sigma} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E_{\infty} \varepsilon + (E_{\infty} \tau_{\varepsilon} + E_{0} \tau_{\sigma}) \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} - E_{0} \tau_{\sigma} \frac{d^{2}\varepsilon}{dt^{2}}$$

### 2.6 Yield Behavior in Polymers

The yield point marks the onset of **plastic flow**. Several yield criteria exist:

- **Tresca Criterion:** Yield occurs when maximum shear stress reaches a critical value.

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{3}}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{2}$$

- **von Mises Criterion:** Yield occurs when the distortional energy reaches a critical value.

$$\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{2})^{2} + (\sigma_{2} - \sigma_{3})^{2} + (\sigma_{3} - \sigma_{1})^{2}\right] = \sigma_{y}^{2}$$

- **Coulomb–Mohr Criterion** (used for polymers): Accounts for pressure dependence.

$$\tau = \tau_{0} - \mu_{p} \cdot \sigma_{n}$$

where **μ_p** is the internal friction coefficient of the polymer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Eyring Theory of Yield:** The yield stress of a polymer is **temperature and strain-rate dependent**. The Eyring equation relates them:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{0} \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G}{RT}\right) \sinh\left(\frac{v \cdot \sigma}{RT}\right)$$

where **v** is the **activation volume** of a polymer segment, **ΔG** is the activation energy, and **R** is the universal gas constant. This explains why polymers yield more easily at high temperature or low strain rate.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Pa | Linear elastic regime |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Pa | Pure shear deformation |
| 3 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$ | dimensionless | Volume change analysis |
| 4 | Bulk Modulus | $K = E/\left[3(1-2\nu)\right]$ | Pa | Hydrostatic compression |
| 5 | Relation between E and G | $G = E/\left[2(1+\nu)\right]$ | Pa | Mode conversion |
| 6 | Maxwell Model ODE | $\sigma + \tau\dot{\sigma} = \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Pa | Stress relaxation |
| 7 | Relaxation Time | $\tau = \eta/E$ | s | Time scale of response |
| 8 | Kelvin–Voigt Model ODE | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Pa | Creep behavior |
| 9 | Creep Compliance | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_{0}$ | Pa⁻¹ | Long-term strain |
| 10 | Relaxation Modulus | $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_{0}$ | Pa | Stress decay |
| 11 | Maxwell Relaxation Modulus | $G(t) = E \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Pa | Stress decay curve |
| 12 | Kelvin–Voigt Creep | $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_{0}/E)\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | dimensionless | Creep curve |
| 13 | Eyring Yield Equation | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{0}\exp(-\Delta G/RT)\sinh(v\sigma/RT)$ | s⁻¹ | Rate-dependent yield |
| 14 | Tresca Yield | $\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{3} = \sigma_{y}$ | Pa | Yield onset criterion |
| 15 | von Mises Yield | $(\sigma_{1}-\sigma_{2})^{2} + (\sigma_{2}-\sigma_{3})^{2} + (\sigma_{3}-\sigma_{1})^{2} = 2\sigma_{y}^{2}$ | Pa² | Ductile yield criterion |
| 16 | Time–Temperature Superposition | $\log(a_{T}) = -C_{1}(T-T_{ref})/\left[C_{2} + (T-T_{ref})\right]$ | dimensionless | WLF equation |
| 17 | Dynamic Storage Modulus | $E^{\prime}(\omega) = \sigma_{0}\cos\delta/\varepsilon_{0}$ | Pa | Elastic energy storage |
| 18 | Dynamic Loss Modulus | $E^{\prime\prime}(\omega) = \sigma_{0}\sin\delta/\varepsilon_{0}$ | Pa | Viscous energy dissipation |
| 19 | Loss Tangent (Tan Delta) | $\tan\delta = E^{\prime\prime}/E^{\prime}$ | dimensionless | Damping capability |
| 20 | Complex Modulus | $E^{\ast} = E^{\prime} + i E^{\prime\prime}$ | Pa | Frequency response |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — Why this matters in the real world:**
> - **Packaging Industry:** Predicting long-term creep of plastic bottles on shelves.
> - **Automotive Industry:** Designing rubber mounts that dampen engine vibration (loss tangent).
> - **Biomedical Devices:** Selecting polymers for artificial heart valves that must flex millions of cycles without yielding.
> - **3D Printing:** Predicting warping and yield in printed polymer parts.
> - **Aerospace:** Understanding the time-dependent behavior of polymer composites in extreme temperatures.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell Model Constitutive Equation

**Objective:** Derive the differential equation governing stress relaxation for a Maxwell element (spring E in series with dashpot η).

**Step 1: Set up the geometry.**

In a series arrangement, the total strain is the sum of the strains in each element:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2: Apply Hooke's law to the spring.**

The spring obeys Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_{spring} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's law of viscosity to the dashpot.**

The dashpot obeys Newtonian flow:

$$\sigma = \eta \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 4: Sum the strain rates.**

Since the strains are additive in series:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 5: Multiply through by E to obtain canonical form.**

$$E \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\sigma$$

**Step 6: Define the relaxation time and finalize.**

Let $\tau = \eta/E$. Then $E/\eta = 1/\tau$, giving:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

This is the **Maxwell constitutive equation**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Relaxation Modulus G(t) for Maxwell Model

**Objective:** Show that under a step strain ε₀ applied at t = 0, the stress decays exponentially as G(t) = E·e^(-t/τ).

**Step 1: Apply a step strain.**

At t = 0, apply a constant strain:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{0} \cdot H(t)$$

where H(t) is the Heaviside step function. Therefore:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \varepsilon_{0} \cdot \delta(t)$$

**Step 2: Simplify the constitutive equation.**

For t > 0, dε/dt = 0 (the strain is held constant), so the Maxwell equation reduces to:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\tau} = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve the first-order linear ODE.**

This is a separable ODE. Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

Integrating both sides from 0 to t:

$$\int_{\sigma_{0}}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dt}{\tau}$$

$$\ln\left[\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_{0}}\right] = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate to obtain the solution.**

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 5: Apply the initial condition.**

At t = 0⁺, the spring carries the entire load (dashpot cannot respond instantaneously):

$$\sigma_{0} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{0}$$

**Step 6: Write the final form of the relaxation modulus.**

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_{0} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Therefore the **relaxation modulus** is:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_{0}} = E \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The stress decays exponentially because the dashpot gradually relieves the spring of its load. After one relaxation time (t = τ), the stress has dropped to about **36.8%** of its initial value. After five relaxation times, it is essentially zero.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Creep Compliance J(t) for Kelvin–Voigt Model

**Objective:** Derive the strain response of a Kelvin–Voigt element under a step stress σ₀.

**Step 1: Apply a step stress.**

At t = 0, apply:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0} \cdot H(t)$$

**Step 2: Write the Kelvin–Voigt constitutive equation.**

$$\sigma_{0} = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange into standard form.**

$$\eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + E \cdot \varepsilon = \sigma_{0}$$

Dividing by η:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}$$

**Step 4: Identify the integrating factor.**

Let τ = η/E. The ODE becomes:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}$$

**Step 5: Solve using the integrating factor method.**

Multiplying by e^(t/τ):

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta} e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrating from 0 to t:

$$\varepsilon(t) \cdot e^{t/\tau} - \varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta} \int_{0}^{t} e^{t'/\tau} dt'$$

$$\varepsilon(t) \cdot e^{t/\tau} - 0 = \frac{\sigma_{0} \tau}{\eta} \left[e^{t/\tau} - 1\right]$$

**Step 6: Simplify and write the final creep expression.**

Since τ/η = 1/E:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$

Therefore, the **creep compliance** is:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_{0}} = \frac{1}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The strain grows asymptotically toward the equilibrium value σ₀/E. The dashpot delays the response, but the spring ultimately determines the final strain. Upon unloading, the strain decays back to zero — there is **no permanent deformation** in the Kelvin–Voigt model.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Eyring Plot for Yield Stress

**Objective:** Show that the yield stress depends linearly on the logarithm of the strain rate.

**Step 1: Recall the Eyring equation.**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{0} \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G}{RT}\right) \sinh\left(\frac{v\sigma_{y}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2: Consider the high-stress limit.**

When vσ_y ≫ RT, sinh(x) ≈ (1/2)·e^x. The equation becomes:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \approx \frac{\dot{\varepsilon}_{0}}{2}\exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G}{RT}\right) \exp\left(\frac{v\sigma_{y}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\dot{\varepsilon} = \ln\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}_{0}}{2}\right) - \frac{\Delta G}{RT} + \frac{v\sigma_{y}}{RT}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the yield stress.**

$$\frac{v\sigma_{y}}{RT} = \ln\dot{\varepsilon} - \ln\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}_{0}}{2}\right) + \frac{\Delta G}{RT}$$

$$\sigma_{y} = \frac{RT}{v}\left[\ln\dot{\varepsilon} - \ln\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}_{0}}{2}\right) + \frac{\Delta G}{RT}\right]$$

**Step 5: Recognize the linear form.**

$$\sigma_{y} = \frac{RT}{v}\ln\dot{\varepsilon} + \text{constant}$$

A plot of **σ_y vs ln(ε̇)** at constant temperature is a **straight line** with slope **RT/v**. This is the basis of the experimental Eyring plot used in polymer yield studies.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Behavior Comparison

| Property | Maxwell Model | Kelvin–Voigt Model | Standard Linear Solid |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Configuration | Spring + Dashpot (series) | Spring + Dashpot (parallel) | Maxwell + Free Spring (parallel) |
| Constitutive ODE | $\dot{\sigma}/\sigma + 1/\tau = (E/\eta)\dot{\varepsilon}$ | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Combines both behaviors |
| Creep response | Unbounded flow $\varepsilon = \sigma_{0}/\eta \cdot t$ | Bounded $\varepsilon = (\sigma_{0}/E)(1-e^{-t/\tau})$ | Bounded with equilibrium |
| Stress relaxation | Exponential decay $G(t) = Ee^{-t/\tau}$ | No relaxation (stress is constant) | Two-mode exponential decay |
| Recovery | Incomplete (viscous flow remains) | Complete (returns to zero) | Partial recovery |
| Best suited for | Polymer melts, amorphous polymers above Tg | Glassy polymers below Tg, creep-resistant design | Real polymers with both behaviors |
| Number of parameters | 2 (E, η) | 2 (E, η) | 4 (E₁, E₂, η, τ) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Viscoelastic Model Architecture

The following block diagram shows how the spring, dashpot, and friction slider combine to model the three fundamental mechanical responses of polymers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Stress or Strain Applied to Polymer] --> B{Type of Mechanical Test}
    B -->|Constant Stress| C[Creep Test]
    B -->|Constant Strain| D[Stress Relaxation Test]
    B -->|Cyclic Loading| E[Dynamic Mechanical Analysis]

    C --> F[Kelvin-Voigt Model]
    D --> G[Maxwell Model]
    E --> H[Standard Linear Solid Model]

    F --> F1[Spring E parallel Dashpot eta]
    F1 --> F2[Strain grows as 1 minus exp of minus t over tau]
    F2 --> F3[Full elastic recovery on unloading]

    G --> G1[Spring E series Dashpot eta]
    G1 --> G2[Stress decays as E exp of minus t over tau]
    G2 --> G3[Permanent viscous flow remains]

    H --> H1[Maxwell unit parallel to free spring E2]
    H1 --> H2[Two mode exponential decay]
    H2 --> H3[Partial recovery with equilibrium]

    F3 --> I[Mechanical Response Output: Strain vs Time]
    G3 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Mechanical Response of Polymers

This diagram maps the decision logic for analyzing the mechanical response of a polymer specimen.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Polymer Specimen Under Load] --> Q1{Is the deformation time independent?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the deformation fully recoverable?}
    Q1 -->|No| Visco[Viscoelastic Behavior]

    Q2 -->|Yes| Elastic[Elastic Region]
    Q2 -->|No| Yield[Yield Region]

    Elastic --> E1[Apply Hookes Law: sigma equals E epsilon]
    E1 --> E2[Young Modulus E in Pa]
    E2 --> E3[Energy stored and fully released]

    Yield --> Y1[Stress exceeds Yield Stress sigma_y]
    Y1 --> Y2[Plastic deformation begins]
    Y2 --> Y3[Use Tresca or von Mises Criterion]
    Y3 --> Y4[Rate dependent: apply Eyring Equation]

    Visco --> V1{Which test?}
    V1 -->|Creep| V2[Kelvin-Voigt Model]
    V1 -->|Relaxation| V3[Maxwell Model]
    V1 -->|Dynamic| V4[Standard Linear Solid]

    V2 --> V5[Strain grows asymptotically]
    V3 --> V6[Stress decays exponentially]
    V4 --> V7[Complex modulus E star and tan delta]
```

The following flow diagram shows the five characteristic regions of a polymer stress–strain curve.

graph TD
