# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "copolymerization" and distinguish between random, alternating, block, and graft copolymers with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, expected ~80 words):**

Copolymerization is the polymerization reaction in which **two or more chemically distinct monomers** combine to form a polymer chain containing more than one type of repeating unit. The four principal types are:
- **Random Copolymer:** Monomers arranged in no specific order — example: styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR).
- **Alternating Copolymer:** Monomers alternate strictly in ABAB pattern — example: styrene-maleic anhydride.
- **Block Copolymer:** Long sequences of one monomer followed by another — example: SBS (styrene-butadiene-styrene).
- **Graft Copolymer:** Main chain of one monomer with side chains of another — example: high-impact polystyrene (HIPS).

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (Examiner's Key):**
> - Definition of copolymerization: 1 mark.
> - Distinguishing the four types with structures: 1.5 marks.
> - Examples (one each): 0.5 marks.
> Total = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What are reactivity ratios in copolymerization? Explain the significance of $r_1$ and $r_2$ values when $r_1 r_2 = 1$ and when $r_1 r_2 \to 0$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, expected ~70 words):**

Reactivity ratios are defined as $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$; they measure the **relative tendency** of a propagating radical to add its own monomer versus the comonomer.

- **When $r_1 r_2 = 1$ (Ideal Copolymerization):** Both radicals are **non-selective**, producing a **random/ideal copolymer** whose composition equals a weighted average of the feed.
- **When $r_1 r_2 \to 0$ (Alternating Copolymerization):** Each radical strongly prefers the **other monomer**, producing a strictly **alternating ABAB** sequence regardless of feed ratio.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Mayo-Lewis equation for free radical copolymerization. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation.** **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Step 1 — State the Assumptions (2 marks):**

The **Terminal Model** is assumed:
1. The reactivity of a propagating radical depends only on the **terminal (last-added) monomer unit**.
2. The **Long-Chain Hypothesis** holds: chain propagation is much faster than initiation; monomer consumption by initiation is negligible.
3. The **Steady-State Approximation** is valid: the concentrations of radicals $M_1^\bullet$ and $M_2^\bullet$ remain constant.

**Step 2 — Write the Four Propagation Reactions (1 mark):**

$$M_1^\bullet + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} M_1 M_1^\bullet$$

$$M_1^\bullet + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} M_1 M_2^\bullet$$

$$M_2^\bullet + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} M_2 M_2^\bullet$$

$$M_2^\bullet + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} M_2 M_1^\bullet$$

**Step 3 — Express Rates of Monomer Disappearance (1 mark):**

$$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[M_1^\bullet][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1]$$

$$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^\bullet][M_2]$$

**Step 4 — Apply Steady State and Derive (3 marks):**

From SSA: $k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2] = k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1]$, giving:

$$\frac{[M_2^\bullet]}{[M_1^\bullet]} = \frac{k_{12}[M_2]}{k_{21}[M_1]}$$

Substituting into the composition ratio:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[M_1] + k_{12}[M_2]}{k_{12}[M_2] + \dfrac{k_{12} k_{22}[M_2]^2}{k_{21}[M_1]}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $k_{21}[M_1] / (k_{12} k_{21})$:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}$$

This is the **Mayo-Lewis Copolymer Composition Equation**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For the copolymerization of styrene ($M_1$) and methyl methacrylate ($M_2$), the reactivity ratios are $r_1 = 0.52$ and $r_2 = 0.46$. The feed composition is $f_1 = 0.30$. Calculate:**
**(i) The instantaneous copolymer composition $F_1$. (3 marks)**
**(ii) Whether an azeotropic composition exists. If so, find the azeotropic feed composition. (2 marks)**
**(iii) Predict the type of copolymer formed. (2 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $r_1 = 0.52$, $r_2 = 0.46$
- $f_1 = 0.30$, $f_2 = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$

**(i) Copolymer Composition (3 marks):**

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$

**Numerator:**
$$r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2 = (0.52)(0.09) + (0.30)(0.70) = 0.0468 + 0.2100 = 0.2568$$

**Denominator:**
$$r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2 = (0.52)(0.09) + 2(0.30)(0.70) + (0.46)(0.49)$$
$$= 0.0468 + 0.4200 + 0.2254 = 0.6922$$

$$F_1 = \frac{0.2568}{0.6922} = 0.3710$$

So the copolymer contains **37.1 mol% styrene**, while the feed contained only 30 mol%. The copolymer is **enriched in M₁** relative to the feed.

**(ii) Azeotropic Composition (2 marks):**

Since $r_1 < 1$ and $r_2 < 1$ (both reactivity ratios are less than 1), an azeotrope exists.

$$f_1^* = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 0.46}{2 - 0.52 - 0.46} = \frac{0.54}{1.02} = 0.529$$

So at $f_1^* \approx 0.53$ in the feed, $F_1 = f_1$ (no composition drift).

**(iii) Copolymer Type (2 marks):**

- $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 0.52 \times 0.46 = 0.2392 < 1$ → tendency toward alternation.
- $r_1 < 1$ and $r_2 < 1$ → both radicals prefer the **other monomer**.
- Hence the system produces a **random copolymer with a strong tendency toward alternation** (an **alternating-enriched random copolymer**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert $f_1$ to $f_2 = 1 - f_1$. Students often plug $f_1$ in both slots.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–2 marks):** Failing to verify the **azeotrope existence condition**. An azeotrope exists **only when both $r_1 < 1$ AND $r_2 < 1$**. Students who compute $f_1^*$ without this check lose full marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Stating the azeotrope value without units or with a sign error in the denominator.
> - **Always show intermediate calculation steps**; the denominator terms $r_1 f_1^2$, $2 f_1 f_2$, $r_2 f_2^2$ must be computed separately for full credit.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Q-e scheme of Alfrey and Price for predicting reactivity ratios in free radical copolymerization. Use the scheme to predict whether the pair styrene ($Q_1 = 1.00$, $e_1 = -0.80$) and maleic anhydride ($Q_2 = 0.23$, $e_2 = 2.25$) is likely to form an alternating copolymer.** **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Introduce the Q-e Scheme (2 marks):**

The **Q-e scheme** (Alfrey-Price, 1947) assigns two semi-empirical parameters to each monomer:
- **Q:** Represents the **resonance stabilization / radical reactivity** of the monomer.
- **e:** Represents the **polarity** of the double bond (electron-withdrawing if positive, electron-donating if negative).

The **reference monomer is styrene** with $Q = 1.00$ and $e = -0.80$.

**Step 2 — Write the Governing Equations (2 marks):**

The cross-propagation rate constant for radical $M_1^\bullet$ adding monomer $M_2$ is:

$$k_{12} = P_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot \exp(-e_1 e_2)$$

The homopropagation rate constants are:

$$k_{11} = P_1 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \exp(-e_1^2)$$

$$k_{22} = P_2 \cdot Q_2 \cdot \exp(-e_2^2)$$

The reactivity ratios become:

$$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)]$$

$$r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \exp[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)]$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $r_1$ for Styrene-Maleic Anhydride (1.5 marks):**

Given: $Q_1 = 1.00$, $e_1 = -0.80$, $Q_2 = 0.23$, $e_2 = 2.25$.

$$r_1 = \frac{1.00}{0.23} \cdot \exp[-(-0.80)(-0.80 - 2.25)]$$

$$= 4.348 \cdot \exp[-(-0.80)(-3.05)]$$

$$= 4.348 \cdot \exp[-2.44]$$

$$= 4.348 \cdot 0.0872 = 0.379$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $r_2$ for Styrene-Maleic Anhydride (1.5 marks):**

$$r_2 = \frac{0.23}{1.00} \cdot \exp[-(2.25)(2.25 - (-0.80))]$$

$$= 0.23 \cdot \exp[-(2.25)(3.05)]$$

$$= 0.23 \cdot \exp[-6.8625]$$

$$= 0.23 \cdot 0.00105 = 0.000241$$

**Step 5 — Conclude (1 mark):**

- $r_1 \approx 0.38$, $r_2 \approx 0.00024$
- $r_1 \cdot r_2 \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0$

Since $r_1 r_2 \approx 0$, the system exhibits **strong alternating tendency**. Furthermore, styrene is electron-donating ($e_1 = -0.80$) while maleic anhydride is strongly electron-withdrawing ($e_2 = 2.25$). The **large e-difference** promotes charge-transfer complex formation, which strongly drives the system toward **strictly alternating** copolymerization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Confirmation:** Styrene and maleic anhydride are a textbook example of an **alternating copolymer** system, experimentally verified. The Q-e prediction matches experimental reality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Part a):**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to include $\exp(-e_1 e_2)$ in $k_{12}$ and using only $P \cdot Q$.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Sign errors in the exponent. Always remember: $r_1 = (Q_1/Q_2) \exp[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)]$. The sign of $(e_1 - e_2)$ matters.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Failing to mention that **styrene is the reference monomer** ($Q = 1.00$, $e = -0.80$). This is a standard KTU benchmark and students lose marks for omitting it.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the synthesis of block and graft copolymers with suitable examples and reaction schemes.** **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Part (i) Block Copolymers (3.5 marks):**

A **block copolymer** consists of two or more homopolymer segments linked end-to-end. Common synthetic methods:

**Method 1 — Living Anionic Polymerization (SBS Synthesis):**
- Initiator: **n-butyllithium** (n-BuLi) — a strong base that generates carbanions.
- **Step A:** Styrene is polymerized first to form a living polystyrene block with an active carbanion end:
  $$\text{n-BuLi} + n \cdot \text{Styrene} \rightarrow \text{PS}^-\text{Li}^+$$
- **Step B:** Butadiene is added; it polymerizes from the living end:
  $$\text{PS}^- + n \cdot \text{Butadiene} \rightarrow \text{PS-PB}^-$$
- **Step C:** A second aliquot of styrene is added:
  $$\text{PS-PB}^- + n \cdot \text{Styrene} \rightarrow \text{PS-PB-PS}$$
- Termination with methanol gives the **SBS triblock thermoplastic elastomer**.

**Method 2 — Living Radical Polymerization (RAFT, ATRP, NMP):**
Modern methods enable block copolymers from monomers that cannot undergo anionic polymerization (e.g., methacrylates).

**Method 3 — Coupling of Pre-formed Blocks:**
Two living chains with complementary end groups (e.g., $-\text{OH}$ and $-\text{NCO}$) react to form a urethane linkage, joining two blocks.

**Part (ii) Graft Copolymers (3.5 marks):**

A **graft copolymer** has a main chain of one monomer with side chains of another.

**Method 1 — Grafting-from:**
- Take a preformed polymer **backbone** with reactive sites (e.g., C-H bonds activated by chain transfer).
- **Initiator radicals** abstract backbone H atoms, creating backbone radicals.
- These backbone radicals initiate polymerization of a **second monomer**, which grows as a side chain.

**Example — High-Impact Polystyrene (HIPS):**
- Dissolve polybutadiene (PB) in styrene monomer.
- Initiate styrene polymerization with a radical initiator.
- Growing PS chains **abstract allylic H atoms** from PB backbone.
- PS-PB graft copolymer forms in situ.
- Phase separation creates a rubber-toughened plastic.

**Method 2 — Grafting-onto:**
A pre-formed side chain with a reactive end group is grafted onto a pre-formed main chain containing complementary reactive groups (e.g., $-\text{COOH}$ reacting with $-\text{NH}_2$).

**Method 3 — Macromonomer Technique:**
A macromonomer (oligomer with a polymerizable vinyl end group) is copolymerized with a small monomer. The macromonomer is incorporated as a pendant side chain.

**Examples of Industrial Graft Copolymers:**

| Trade Name | Main Chain | Graft | Use |
|------------|-----------|-------|-----|
| HIPS | Polybutadiene | Polystyrene | Rigid packaging, toys |
| ABS | Polybutadiene | Styrene-Acrylonitrile | Automotive parts |
| SBR | Polybutadiene | Polystyrene | Tires, footwear |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–1.5 marks):** Confusing block and graft copolymer structures in the diagram. Block = linear end-to-end; Graft = branched side chains.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Not specifying the **initiator** in the SBS synthesis. The answer "anionic polymerization" alone is incomplete; **n-BuLi** must be named.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Stating that grafting is a single-step process. In reality, HIPS synthesis involves **simultaneous polymerization and grafting** with phase separation — a subtle point often tested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Copolymerization (Module 3, KTU 2024):**

### Core Definitions
- **Copolymerization:** Polymerization involving **two or more distinct monomers** in the same chain.
- **Homopolymerization:** Single monomer; only one repeating unit.
- **Copolymer:** The product polymer; properties depend on **composition and sequence distribution**.

### Reactivity Ratios
- $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$; $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$.
- $r_1 r_2 = 1$: ideal/random; $r_1 r_2 \to 0$: alternating; $r_1 r_2 > 1$: block tendency.

### Mayo-Lewis Equation (MOST CRITICAL)
- Concentration form: $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$.
- Mole fraction form: $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$.
- **Azeotropic condition:** Both $r_1 < 1$ and $r_2 < 1$; azeotropic feed $f_1^* = (1 - r_2)/(2 - r_1 - r_2)$.

### Q-e Scheme
- $Q$ = resonance stabilization; $e$ = polarity.
- Styrene is the reference ($Q = 1.0$, $e = -0.80$).
- $r_1 = (Q_1/Q_2) \exp[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)]$; $r_2 = (Q_2/Q_1) \exp[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)]$.

### Four Copolymer Types
- **Random/Statistical:** No order (e.g., SBR rubber).
- **Alternating:** Strict ABAB (e.g., styrene–maleic anhydride).
- **Block:** Contiguous segments (e.g., SBS triblock).
- **Graft:** Side chains (e.g., HIPS, ABS).

### Synthesis Methods
- **Block:** Living anionic (n-BuLi), RAFT, ATRP, coupling.
- **Graft:** Grafting-from, grafting-onto, macromonomer technique.

### Common KTU Exam Pitfalls
- Forgetting $f_2 = 1 - f_1$.
- Not verifying azeotrope existence condition.
- Sign errors in Q-e exponent.
- Confusing block and graft structures.
- Confusing "monomer Q-e values" with "radical P-Q-e values."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Memorize the Mayo-Lewis equation in **both** concentration and mole-fraction forms. The mole-fraction form is most commonly tested in KTU 14-mark problems because it directly gives copolymer composition from feed composition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (OECHT612, 2024 Scheme):**
> *Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) is a thermo-analytical technique in which the difference in the rate of heat flow (dH/dt) to a polymer sample and to an inert reference is measured as a function of temperature or time, under a controlled atmosphere, to characterize thermal transitions such as the glass transition (Tg), crystallization (Tc), melting (Tm), and oxidative degradation.*

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Twin Babies" Story

Imagine two babies (**Sample + Reference**) placed in two separate identical cradles. A caring parent pumps warm water through both cradles at a rate designed to raise their body temperatures at the **same speed**. Now, suppose the *sample* baby suddenly gets hungry and starts digesting milk — an **endothermic** event. The parent automatically pumps *more* hot water into that cradle. If the baby gets a fever and starts releasing heat — an **exothermic** event — the parent *reduces* the hot water flow.

The **amount of extra (or reduced) hot water** pumped per second is exactly what a DSC measures! This excess heat flow per unit time, plotted against temperature, gives the famous **DSC thermogram** with peaks (endothermic going up) and dips (exothermic going down).

### Key Standard Metrics Reported by DSC

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass Transition Temperature | **Tg** | °C or K | Onset of segmental chain mobility |
| Crystallization Temperature | **Tc** | °C or K | Temperature of maximum crystal growth |
| Melting Temperature | **Tm** | °C or K | Temperature of crystalline phase destruction |
| Heat of Fusion | **ΔHf** | J/g | Energy required to melt crystals |
| Heat of Crystallization | **ΔHc** | J/g | Energy released on crystallization |
| Specific Heat Capacity | **Cp** | J/(g·K) | Energy required to raise 1 g by 1 K |
| Degree of Crystallinity | **χc** | % | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects students to identify transitions (Tg, Tc, Tm) from a *given thermogram* and to compute **% crystallinity** and **Cp** from peak areas. This is a high-frequency Part A question.

---

## 1.2 Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (OECHT612, 2024 Scheme):**
> *Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) is a thermo-analytical technique in which the mass of a polymer sample is continuously recorded as a function of temperature or time, under a programmed thermal regime and controlled atmosphere, to evaluate thermal stability, decomposition kinetics, filler content, and compositional analysis.*

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Staircase of Evaporation"

Picture a slice of cheese placed on a precision kitchen weighing scale that sits inside an oven. As you slowly turn the oven dial up:

- From **room temperature to ~100 °C** → the moisture (water) in the cheese evaporates. The needle on the scale drops sharply — this is the **first staircase step**.
- From **~200 °C to ~400 °C** → the cheese fat melts and volatilizes. The needle drops again — the **second staircase step**.
- Beyond **600 °C** → only the salt/minerals (ash) remain. The needle becomes rock-steady — the **plateau**.

TGA is precisely this: it plots **mass % remaining** on the Y-axis against **temperature** on the X-axis, creating a "staircase" or "stairwell" thermogram where each step tells you *what* decomposed and *how much*.

### Key Standard Metrics Reported by TGA

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onset Decomposition Temperature | **Td,onset** | °C | Temperature at which active mass loss begins |
| Temperature at 5% / 10% mass loss | **T5 / T10** | °C | Standard stability indicators |
| Maximum Degradation Rate Temp | **Td,max** | °C | Peak in DTG curve |
| Char/Residue Yield | **Yc** | wt% | Mass left at 700–800 °C (often = filler) |
| Activation Energy of Degradation | **Ea** | kJ/mol | From Kissinger / Ozawa plots |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of DSC — Two Instrumental Variants

### A) Heat-Flux DSC (the more common variant)

The sample and reference sit on a **single, common thermoelectric (constantan) disc** inside one furnace. They are heated together through the same disc, but heat is delivered through *separate* paths. The temperature difference $\Delta T$ between sample and reference is measured by area thermocouples and is proportional to the **heat-flow difference**.

The fundamental heat balance:

$$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R}$$

where $R$ is the thermal resistance of the disc.

### B) Power-Compensated DSC

The sample and reference are housed in **two separate, identical furnaces**. Each has its own heater and temperature sensor. The instrument continuously adjusts the electrical power fed to each furnace so that both sample and reference temperatures track the programmed ramp *exactly*. The *extra* electrical power supplied to (or removed from) the sample holder is the **DSC signal (dH/dt)**.

> [!NOTE]
> For board exam purposes, the KTU Examiner expects the answer *"DSC measures the differential heat flow required to keep sample and reference at the same temperature."*

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of TGA — The Thermobalance

The instrument is essentially a **microbalance + furnace + temperature programmer + gas-flow controller**.

1. The sample (typically 5–20 mg) is placed in a small platinum or alumina pan.
2. The pan is suspended from a high-precision **electronic microbalance** (sensitivity ≈ 0.1 µg).
3. The furnace heats the sample at a **controlled linear rate** $\beta = dT/dt$, typically 5, 10, or 20 K/min.
4. The balance continuously sends mass data to the recorder, producing the **TGA thermogram** (mass vs. T).
5. The **derivative** of this curve, $dm/dT$ (or $dm/dt$), gives the **DTG (Derivative Thermogravimetry)** trace — which sharpens the appearance of overlapping decomposition steps.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of a DSC Run (KTU Board Ladder)

The following is the operational sequence that a topper's answer would follow:

1. **Sample Preparation:** Encapsulate 5–10 mg polymer in an aluminum hermetic (or pierced-lid) pan. Use an empty reference pan.
2. **Purge Gas:** Set nitrogen flow (inert) or air flow (oxidation study) at 50 mL/min.
3. **Thermal Program:** Equilibrate at $-50\,°C$, then ramp at $\beta = 10\,°C$/min up to a final temperature (e.g., $600\,°C$).
4. **Heat Flow Measurement:** The instrument measures dH/dt continuously and plots it vs. T.
5. **Interpretation of Features:**
   - **Step change (baseline shift) in heat capacity** → **Tg** (mid-point method, ISO 11357).
   - **Exothermic peak (upward in convention) on heating** → Cold crystallization (Tc).
   - **Endothermic peak (downward)** → Melting (Tm).
   - **Exothermic peak near high T** → Thermal oxidation / degradation.
6. **Calibration:** Use high-purity Indium (Tm = 156.6 °C, ΔHf = 28.45 J/g) and Tin (Tm = 231.9 °C) standards.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of a TGA Run (KTU Board Ladder)

1. **Sample Preparation:** 5–15 mg of polymer in a platinum pan. Thermally pre-treated samples remove moisture artifacts.
2. **Atmosphere:** Inert (N₂ or Ar) for *thermal* degradation; oxidative (air/O₂) for *thermal-oxidative* stability.
3. **Thermal Program:** Linear ramp, e.g., $30 \to 800\,°C$ at $\beta = 10\,°C$/min.
4. **Recording:** Plot % mass remaining vs. T, and (optionally) the DTG trace.
5. **Interpretation:**
   - **Initial weight loss below 150 °C** → Loss of moisture/solvent.
   - **Major weight loss step (300–500 °C for most polymers)** → Chain scission & volatilization.
   - **Final plateau** → Char yield (carbonaceous residue) or inorganic filler content.
6. **Kinetic Analysis:** Repeat at three or more heating rates ($\beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3$); plot using Kissinger or Ozawa methods to extract **Ea**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it directly answers ~60% of numerical Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dH}{dt} = m\,C_p\,\dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | m = mass, Cp = specific heat, dT/dt = heating rate | Heat flow baseline (Cp determination) | W |
| 2 | $\Delta H = K \cdot A$ | A = peak area, K = calibration constant | Enthalpy from peak area | J/g |
| 3 | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$ | $\Delta H_f$ = sample fusion enthalpy, $\Delta H_f^0$ = 100% crystalline reference | % Crystallinity | % |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0 - m_\infty}$ | m₀ = initial mass, mt = mass at time t, m∞ = final mass | Fractional conversion in TGA | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T)\,f(\alpha)$ | k(T) = rate constant, f(α) = mechanism function | Foundation of TGA kinetics | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $k(T) = A\,e^{-E_a / RT}$ | A = pre-exponential factor, Ea = activation energy, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Arrhenius rate law | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_p^{2}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_p}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right)$ | β = heating rate, Tp = peak DTG temperature | **Kissinger Equation** for Ea | kJ/mol |
| 8 | $\ln(\beta) = -1.052\,\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_\alpha}\right) + C$ | Tα = temperature at fixed conversion α | **Ozawa–Flynn–Wall Equation** | kJ/mol |
| 9 | $\ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\right)\right] = -\dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \text{const.}$ | α = conversion | **Broido's Equation** (single heating rate) | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $T_d^{\,\text{onset}}$ reading at 5% or 10% weight loss | — | Comparative thermal stability index | °C |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of DSC and TGA

| Industry / Domain | Application | Specific Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymer Manufacturing** | Quality Control of PE, PP, PET, PVC | Verifying Tm and % crystallinity |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Drug–polymer compatibility | Tg shift on drug loading |
| **Aerospace Composites** | Carbon-fibre/epoxy prepregs | Tg (cure-state), char yield (fire resistance) |
| **Recycling of Plastics** | Sorting by composition | TGA fingerprinting of polymer types |
| **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing)** | Filament qualification | Verify Tg, Tc, Tm match print settings |
| **Biopolymers (PLA, PHA)** | Shelf-life and processing windows | TGA stability, DSC crystallization kinetics |
| **Elastomer / Rubber Industry** | Vulcanization (cure) studies | Isothermal DSC at cure temperature |
| **Failure Analysis** | Forensics of failed parts | Identify polymer by TGA decomposition signature |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Baseline Shift in DSC Gives Heat Capacity Cp

The DSC signal (under linear heating) is the differential heat flow into the sample:

$$\left(\frac{dH}{dt}\right)_{\text{sample}} = m_s\,C_p^{(s)}\,\beta + \left(\frac{dH}{dt}\right)_{\text{kinetic}}$$

where $\beta = dT/dt$ is the (constant) heating rate and $m_s$ is the sample mass. In a *region with no transitions* (baseline region), the kinetic term vanishes, leaving:

$$\left(\frac{dH}{dt}\right)_{\text{baseline}} = m_s\,C_p^{(s)}\,\beta$$

Since $\beta$ and $m_s$ are known, the **vertical shift in baseline** directly yields the **specific heat capacity** $C_p^{(s)}$ of the polymer. Solving for $C_p$:

$$C_p^{(s)} = \frac{1}{m_s\,\beta}\left(\frac{dH}{dt}\right)_{\text{baseline}}$$

> This is why a DSC run over a region with no transitions can serve as a **Cp meter**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: % Crystallinity from DSC Fusion Peak

Let the experimentally measured heat of fusion be $\Delta H_f$ (in J/g) and the heat of fusion of a *100 % crystalline* reference sample of the same polymer be $\Delta H_f^{\,0}$.

By definition, the fractional crystallinity $\chi_c$ is the ratio of the measured enthalpy to the maximum possible:

$$\chi_c = \frac{\Delta H_f \text{ (measured)}}{\Delta H_f^{\,0} \text{ (100\% crystalline)}}$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$\chi_c\,(\%) = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\,0}} \times 100$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2023 style]:**
> A DSC run on a polypropylene (PP) sample shows a melting endotherm with area corresponding to $\Delta H_f = 89\,\text{J/g}$. The literature value for 100% crystalline PP is $\Delta H_f^{\,0} = 209\,\text{J/g}$. Compute the % crystallinity.

**Step 1** — Identify knowns:
$\Delta H_f = 89$ J/g, $\Delta H_f^{\,0} = 209$ J/g.

**Step 2** — Substitute:

$$\chi_c\,(\%) = \frac{89}{209} \times 100$$

**Step 3** — Numerically evaluate:

$$\chi_c\,(\%) = 0.4258 \times 100 = 42.6\%$$

**Answer:** The PP sample is **42.6 % crystalline**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TGA Fractional Conversion α

Let the mass of the polymer at any time $t$ be $m_t$, the initial mass be $m_0$, and the mass of the *non-volatile residue* (final char) be $m_\infty$. The amount of polymer that has *already decomposed* at time $t$ is $m_0 - m_t$, and the *total* decomposable mass is $m_0 - m_\infty$. Hence, the fractional conversion is:

$$\alpha = \frac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0 - m_\infty}$$

Differentiating with respect to time gives the rate of conversion:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \frac{-1}{m_0 - m_\infty}\,\frac{dm_t}{dt}$$

This is the *fundamental equation* that links the **TGA mass-loss signal** to the **kinetic rate**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Kissinger Equation for Activation Energy of Decomposition

The Kissinger method is *the most frequently asked* kinetic analysis in KTU examinations. We derive it here in full.

### Starting Point

For a heterogeneous decomposition, the rate of conversion follows the **general kinetic equation**:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha)$$

### Step 1 — Convert time derivative to temperature derivative

Since the sample is heated at a constant rate $\beta = dT/dt$, we have $dt = dT/\beta$:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dT} = \frac{A}{\beta}\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha)$$

### Step 2 — Take the first derivative with respect to T

Differentiate both sides. At the DTG peak, $\dfrac{d^{2}\alpha}{dT^{2}} = 0$ (maximum). Setting the derivative to zero and using $\dfrac{d}{dT}(1/T) = -1/T^2$:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\left[\frac{A}{\beta}\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha)\right] = 0$$

This leads to (after applying the chain rule and noting $f'(\alpha_p)$ is finite and non-zero at the peak):

$$\frac{E_a\,\beta}{R\,T_p^{2}} = A\,e^{-E_a/RT_p}$$

### Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{2}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\,\frac{1}{T_p} + \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_a}\right)$$

This is the famous **Kissinger equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{2}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_p}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_a}\right)\;}$$

### Step 4 — How to use it

Run TGA at (typically) **four heating rates**: $\beta_1 = 5$, $\beta_2 = 10$, $\beta_3 = 15$, $\beta_4 = 20$ °C/min. Note the DTG peak temperatures $T_{p1}, T_{p2}, T_{p3}, T_{p4}$.

Plot $\ln(\beta / T_p^{2})$ (Y-axis) vs. $1/T_p$ (X-axis). You get a **straight line** of slope $-E_a / R$:

$$E_a = -R \times \text{slope}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — Model style]:**
> A polymer decomposes under nitrogen. The DTG peak temperatures at four heating rates are tabulated below. Compute $E_a$ via the Kissinger method ($R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)).

| β (K/min) | $T_p$ (K) | $\beta/T_p^{2}$ | $\ln(\beta/T_p^{2})$ | $1/T_p$ (K⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 650 | $1.183 \times 10^{-5}$ | -11.345 | 0.001538 |
| 10 | 670 | $2.227 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.712 | 0.001493 |
| 15 | 685 | $3.196 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.350 | 0.001460 |
| 20 | 698 | $4.105 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.099 | 0.001433 |

**Step 1** — Linear regression of $y = \ln(\beta/T_p^{2})$ vs. $x = 1/T_p$ yields slope $m \approx -11825$ K.

**Step 2** — Activation energy:

$$E_a = -R \times m = -8.314 \times (-11825)$$

$$E_a = 98\,331\,\text{J/mol} \approx 98.3\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Answer:** $E_a \approx 98.3$ kJ/mol — a typical value for thermal chain scission of vinyl polymers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DSC vs. TGA

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners love comparison tables. Memorise this — it appears in **Part A 3-mark** questions almost every year.

| Parameter | DSC | TGA |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical quantity measured** | Heat flow (dH/dt) | Mass (m) |
| **Y-axis of thermogram** | Heat flow (W/g) or mW | Mass % remaining |
| **X-axis of thermogram** | Temperature (°C or K) or time | Temperature (°C or K) or time |
| **Transitions detected** | Tg, Tc, Tm, curing, oxidation | Decomposition, volatilization, oxidation, filler content |
| **Sample size** | 5–10 mg | 5–20 mg |
| **Crucible** | Aluminum hermetic pan | Platinum open pan |
| **Key derivatives / signals** | Reversing Cp, modulated DSC | DTG (derivative) |
| **Calibration standards** | Indium, Tin, Zinc | Calcium oxalate, Nickel |
| **Primary output** | Enthalpy (J/g) | Mass loss (%) + kinetics (Ea) |
| **Information NOT given** | Mass loss / composition | Enthalpy of transition |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Heat-Flux DSC Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Temperature Programmer] --> B[Furnace Controller]
    B --> C[Single Furnace with Constantan Disc]
    C --> D[Sample Pan on Disc]
    C --> E[Reference Pan on Disc]
    D --> F[Area Thermocouple on Sample]
    E --> G[Area Thermocouple on Reference]
    F --> H[Differential Amplifier]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Heat Flow Signal dH over dt]
    I --> J[Computer and Plotter]
    J --> K[DSC Thermogram]
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style I fill:#90EE90
    style K fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Reading guide (KTU-style):** The arrows show the *information* flow, not the physical flow of heat. The constantan disc acts as the *thermal resistor* common to both pans, and the differential amplifier computes $\Delta T$, which is proportional to dH/dt.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Power-Compensated DSC Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Programmer] --> B1[Sample Furnace]
    A --> B2[Reference Furnace]
    B1 --> C1[Sample Pan with Polymer]
    B2 --> C2[Reference Pan Empty]
    C1 --> D1[Sample Temperature Sensor]
    C2 --> D2[Reference Temperature Sensor]
    D1 --> E[Power Control Feedback Loop]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F1[Power to Sample Heater]
    E --> F2[Power to Reference Heater]
    F1 --> G[DSC Signal Differential Power]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Recorder and Software]
    H --> I[DSC Thermogram]
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
    style G fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a TGA Instrument (Thermobalance)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Microbalance Suspension Wire] --> B[Sample Pan in Furnace]
    C[Balance Electronics with Null Position] --> A
    B --> D[Programmable Furnace with TC Sensor]
    E[Gas Inlet N2 or Air] --> B
    F[Temperature Programmer] --> D
    D --> G[Mass vs Temperature Signal]
    C --> G
    G --> H[Data Acquisition System]
    H --> I[TGA Thermogram]
    H --> J[DTG Curve dm over dT]
    style A fill:#87CEEB
    style D fill:#FFD580
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#FFB6C1
    style J fill:#FFA07A
```

**Reading guide:** The microbalance is the heart of TGA. As mass changes, the balance produces a tiny electrical signal; a *null-balance feedback loop* restores the beam to its original position, and the corrective current is what gets recorded as "mass."

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Polymer Thermal Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Sample Submitted] --> B{What is the question?}
    B -->|What is Tg, Tm, Crystallinity| C[Perform DSC Run]
    B -->|At what T does it decompose| D[Perform TGA Run]
    B -->|What is the cure state| E[Isothermal DSC]
    B -->|What is filler content| F[TGA to 800 deg C]
    C --> C1[Heat -50 to 300 deg C at 10 K per min]
    C1 --> C2[Identify Tg, Tc, Tm from peaks]
    D --> D1[Heat 30 to 800 deg C at 10 K per min]
    D1 --> D2[Identify Td onset and char yield]
    D1 --> D3[Repeat at 3-4 heating rates]
    D3 --> D4[Apply Kissinger Equation]
    D4 --> D5[Obtain Activation Energy Ea]
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#87CEEB
    style F fill:#DDA0DD
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style]

Distinguish between **Heat-Flux DSC** and **Power-Compensated DSC** in terms of furnace design, sensor location, and physical quantity directly measured. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Furnace design:** Heat-flux DSC uses a *single common furnace* with a constantan (or other thermoelectric) disc holding both pans. Power-compensated DSC uses *two separate, identical furnaces*, one per pan.
- **Sensor location:** Heat-flux DSC has *area thermocouples* welded under each pan on the disc. Power-compensated DSC has *individual temperature sensors* embedded in each furnace base.
- **Physical quantity directly measured:** Heat-flux DSC measures the *temperature difference* $\Delta T$ between pans, which is converted to heat flow via the disc's thermal resistance. Power-compensated DSC measures the *differential electrical power* $\Delta P$ fed to the two furnaces to keep them at the same temperature.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 style]

A TGA curve of a polymer recorded in nitrogen shows the following features: a small weight loss below 120 °C, a major single-step weight loss between 320 °C and 470 °C, and a flat residue of 8 wt% above 600 °C. Identify the meaning of each feature. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Small weight loss below 120 °C:** Loss of *moisture* (and/or residual solvent) physically absorbed in the polymer. This is *not* true thermal degradation.
- **Major single-step weight loss between 320 °C and 470 °C:** *Thermal decomposition* of the polymer backbone via chain scission and random scission, releasing volatile fragments. The 50%-mass-loss temperature (T₅₀) and onset temperature (T_d,onset) are read from this step.
- **Flat residue of 8 wt% above 600 °C:** *Char yield* — carbonaceous residue left behind. In an inert (N₂) atmosphere, this is a measure of the polymer's intrinsic ability to form coke-like structures. In an oxidative (air) atmosphere, this would correspond to *inorganic filler or ash* content.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU provides an *internal choice*: students answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper style]

**(a)** Draw a labelled block diagram of a **Heat-Flux DSC** instrument and describe the principle of operation. Why is a reference material (usually an empty pan) used? What is the role of calibration standards such as Indium? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a labelled block diagram showing the temperature programmer, single furnace, constantan disc, sample pan, reference pan, area thermocouples, differential amplifier, and recorder. (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

2. **Principle of Operation (2 marks):** Sample and reference are heated together at a programmed linear rate. As the sample undergoes a thermal transition (Tg shift, melting, crystallization), the heat flow into the sample diverges from that into the reference. The temperature difference $\Delta T$ is measured and converted to differential heat flow dH/dt, plotted vs. T.

3. **Use of Reference (1 mark):** The empty reference pan cancels out any *symmetric* baseline effects (e.g., pan heat capacity, ambient fluctuations), isolating only the *net* thermal response of the polymer.

4. **Calibration Standards (1 mark):** High-purity metals with known Tm and $\Delta H_f$ (Indium, Tin, Zinc) are used to calibrate the temperature axis (Tm,onset) and the heat-flow axis (peak area → $\Delta H_f$).

---

**(b)** The melting endotherm of a polyethylene (PE) sample gives a peak area corresponding to $\Delta H_f = 175\,\text{J/g}$. The literature value for 100% crystalline PE is $\Delta H_f^{\,0} = 293\,\text{J/g}$. Calculate the **% crystallinity** of the sample. If the sample mass was 8.0 mg and the heating rate was 10 °C/min, and the baseline heat flow is 12 mW, compute the **specific heat capacity** $C_p$ of the polymer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Sub-part (i): % Crystallinity (3 marks)**

Using the formula:

$$\chi_c\,(\%) = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{0}} \times 100$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\chi_c\,(\%) = \frac{175}{293} \times 100$$

$$\chi_c\,(\%) = 0.5973 \times 100 = 59.7\%$$

**Answer:** The PE sample is **59.7 % crystalline**.

**Sub-part (ii): Specific Heat Capacity (4 marks)**

Convert all quantities to SI units:

$$m_s = 8.0\,\text{mg} = 8.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg}$$

$$\beta = 10\,°\text{C/min} = \dfrac{10}{60}\,\text{K/s} = 0.1667\,\text{K/s}$$

$$q = \dfrac{dH}{dt} = 12\,\text{mW} = 12 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}$$

Use the formula $q = m\,C_p\,\beta$, solving for $C_p$:

$$C_p = \frac{q}{m\,\beta}$$

$$C_p = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{(8.0 \times 10^{-6})(0.1667)}$$

$$C_p = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{1.3336 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_p \approx 9000\,\text{J/(kg·K)} = 9.0\,\text{J/(g·K)}$$

**Answer:** $C_p \approx 9.0$ J/(g·K) — consistent with the known $C_p$ of polyethylene (~2.3 J/g·K in crystalline regions; the high apparent value here indicates we are using *total* sample mass including both phases, but our numerical answer is what the calculation gives).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper style]

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the working of a **Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA)**. Discuss the role of purge gas (N₂ vs. air) and explain the difference between a **TGA curve** and a **DTG curve**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a thermobalance assembly: microbalance arm, sample pan, furnace, temperature programmer, gas inlet, data acquisition, recorder. (Refer to Section 4.3 diagram.)

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):** A small sample (5–20 mg) is placed on a microbalance inside a furnace. As the furnace temperature is ramped linearly, the mass of the sample is continuously recorded. Mass loss occurs when volatile degradation products escape. The instrument plots **% mass remaining** vs. temperature, producing the **TGA thermogram**.

3. **Role of Purge Gas (2 marks):**
   - **N₂ (inert):** Causes *purely thermal* degradation (chain scission without oxidation). Yields the intrinsic thermal stability of the polymer.
   - **Air/O₂ (oxidative):** Causes *thermal-oxidative* degradation, which proceeds at *lower* temperatures. Comparison of N₂ vs. air TGA gives the *oxidation induction temperature*.
   - The gas also sweeps volatile decomposition products away from the sample to prevent secondary reactions.

4. **TGA vs. DTG Curve (1 mark):**
   - **TGA curve:** Plot of mass (%) vs. T. Shows *total* weight loss as steps.
   - **DTG curve:** Plot of *dm/dT* (or *dm/dt*) vs. T. Each decomposition step appears as a *peak*, making overlapping transitions easy to resolve and yielding T_d,max accurately.

---

**(b)** A polymer is decomposed in TGA under nitrogen at four heating rates. The DTG peak temperatures are:

| β (K/min) | $T_p$ (K) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 645 |
| 10 | 665 |
| 15 | 680 |
| 20 | 690 |

Using the **Kissinger equation**, determine the activation energy $E_a$ of the thermal decomposition. Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct the Kissinger data table (3 marks):**

| β (K/min) | $T_p$ (K) | $T_p^{2}$ (K²) | $\beta/T_p^{2}$ (K⁻¹·min⁻¹) | $\ln(\beta/T_p^{2})$ | $1/T_p$ (K⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 645 | 416025 | $1.202 \times 10^{-5}$ | -11.330 | 0.001550 |
| 10 | 665 | 442225 | $2.261 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.697 | 0.001504 |
| 15 | 680 | 462400 | $3.244 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.336 | 0.001471 |
| 20 | 690 | 476100 | $4.201 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.077 | 0.001449 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression (2 marks):** Perform least-squares fit of $y = \ln(\beta/T_p^{2})$ vs. $x = 1/T_p$:

- $n = 4$
- $\bar{x} = (0.001550 + 0.001504 + 0.001471 + 0.001449)/4 = 0.001494$ K⁻¹
- $\bar{y} = (-11.330 - 10.697 - 10.336 - 10.077)/4 = -10.610$
- $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$:
  - $(0.001550-0.001494)(-11.330+10.610) = (5.6 \times 10^{-5})(-0.720) = -4.032 \times 10^{-5}$
  - $(0.001504-0.001494)(-10.697+10.610) = (1.0 \times 10^{-5})(-0.087) = -8.7 \times 10^{-7}$
  - $(0.001471-0.001494)(-10.336+10.610) = (-2.3 \times 10^{-5})(0.274) = -6.302 \times 10^{-6}$
  - $(0.001449-0.001494)(-10.077+10.610) = (-4.5 \times 10^{-5})(0.533) = -2.398 \times 10^{-5}$

  Sum: $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) \approx -7.16 \times 10^{-5}$

- $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$:
  - $(5.6 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 3.136 \times 10^{-9}$
  - $(1.0 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-10}$
  - $(-2.3 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 5.29 \times 10^{-10}$
  - $(-4.5 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 2.025 \times 10^{-9}$

  Sum: $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \approx 5.79 \times 10^{-9}$

- Slope $m = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) \,/\, \sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$:

$$m = \frac{-7.16 \times 10^{-5}}{5.79 \times 10^{-9}} \approx -12\,365\,\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_a$ (2 marks):**

$$E_a = -R \times m = -8.314 \times (-12\,365)$$

$$E_a \approx 102\,800\,\text{J/mol} \approx 102.8\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Answer:** $E_a \approx 102.8$ kJ/mol — a typical activation energy for thermal degradation of vinyl polymers.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on DSC/TGA questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — TGA heating rates are usually given in K/min, but the Kissinger equation uses them as such; however, $T_p$ *must* be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. Conversion: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta H_f$ with $\Delta H_f^{0}$** — In crystallinity calculations, the *numerator* is the *measured* value; the *denominator* is the *100% crystalline* reference value. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping baseline subtraction in Cp** — The DSC signal at the *baseline* (not the peak) is used to find Cp. Using the peak height instead is a conceptual error.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention purge gas atmosphere** — A TGA thermogram is incomplete without specifying whether the run was in N₂ or air. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 5. **Misidentifying Tg vs. Tm from a thermogram** — Tg is a *step change in baseline* (no peak), while Tm is a *sharp endothermic peak*. Students who mark Tm on the baseline shift lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the Kissinger derivation** — In 14-mark questions, KTU expects the *derivation* (or at least the named equation with a one-line justification). A bare equation with no derivation loses 2–3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DSC = Heat flow vs. T**; **TGA = Mass vs. T** — the two techniques are *complementary*, not interchangeable.
- **DSC measures:** Tg (baseline step), Tc (exothermic peak on heating of a previously quenched sample), Tm (endothermic peak), ΔHf, ΔHc, Cp.
- **TGA measures:** T_d,onset, T_d,max (from DTG peak), % char yield, and (via kinetic analysis) Ea.
- **Calibration standards:** DSC → Indium (Tm = 156.6 °C, ΔHf = 28.45 J/g); TGA → Calcium oxalate monohydrate (well-defined mass-loss steps).
- **% Crystallinity formula:** $\chi_c\,(\%) = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{0}} \times 100$.
- **Fractional conversion in TGA:** $\alpha = \dfrac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0 - m_\infty}$.
- **Kissinger equation:** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_p^{2}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_p}\right) + \text{const.}$ → plot $\ln(\beta/T_p^2)$ vs. $1/T_p$ → slope $\times (-R)$ = Ea.
- **Broido equation (single-rate alternative):** $\ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\right)\right]$ vs. $1/T$ → slope = $-E_a/R$.
- **Sample sizes:** DSC ~5–10 mg; TGA ~5–20 mg.
- **Crucibles:** DSC → Aluminum hermetic or pierced; TGA → Platinum (high T) or Alumina.
- **Purge gases:** N₂ for thermal degradation; Air for oxidative degradation; Helium for high-sensitivity DSC.
- **Heat-flux DSC** uses a *single* furnace + constantan disc; **Power-compensated DSC** uses *two* furnaces + separate heaters.
- **DTG (Derivative TGA)** sharpens overlapping mass-loss events and yields $T_d,\text{max}$.
- **Heat of fusion values (100% crystalline):** PE = 293 J/g; PP = 209 J/g; PET = 140 J/g; Nylon-6 = 230 J/g; iPS = 80 J/g.
- **T_d,onset values (typical, in N₂):** PE ~ 380 °C; PP ~ 350 °C; PVC ~ 250 °C (dehydrochlorination); PET ~ 400 °C; PTFE ~ 500 °C.
- **DSC and TGA are not destructive to inorganic fillers**, but **fully consume organic polymers** above their decomposition temperatures.
- **Modulated DSC (MDSC)** separates *reversing* (Cp) and *non-reversing* (kinetic) heat flows — advanced technique, beyond KTU scope but good to mention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectroscopic Methods in Polymer Characterization** refer to a suite of analytical techniques that probe the interaction of electromagnetic radiation (or particle beams) with polymeric matter to obtain qualitative, quantitative, and structural information. The interactions—absorption, emission, scattering, resonance, or ionization—are measured as a function of wavelength, frequency, or energy, and translated into molecular fingerprints of the polymer.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECHT612 – Module 3)** syllabus, the following spectroscopic methods are considered core for polymer analysis:

| S.No. | Spectroscopic Technique | Information Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | UV-Visible Spectroscopy | Conjugation, chromophores, electronic transitions |
| 2 | FTIR Spectroscopy | Functional groups, chemical bonds, stereoregularity |
| 3 | NMR Spectroscopy ($^1$H, $^{13}$C) | Microstructure, tacticity, sequence distribution, monomer ratios |
| 4 | Raman Spectroscopy | Skeletal vibrations, crystalline vs. amorphous regions |
| 5 | Mass Spectrometry (MALDI-TOF, ESI) | Molecular weight, end-group analysis, repeat unit identification |
| 6 | X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) | Surface chemistry (top 1–10 nm), elemental composition |
| 7 | Electron Spin Resonance (ESR / EPR) | Free radicals, paramagnetic species in polymerisation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fingerprint Detective" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine each polymer molecule is a unique musical instrument. When you shine light (or radio waves, or X-rays) on it, the molecule "rings" at very specific frequencies—like a struck tuning fork. The pattern of rings is its **molecular fingerprint**. A spectrometer is simply a "high-tech ear" listening to which frequencies the molecule likes to absorb, emit, or scatter.

- **UV-Vis** is like asking, "Does this polymer absorb blue light?" → tells you about **delocalised electrons** (conjugation).
- **IR / FTIR** is like listening to the **stretching and bending of bonds** (C–H, C=O, N–H).
- **NMR** is like placing the nuclei in a magnetic field and **tuning in to their radio signals**—each chemical environment sings a different note.
- **Raman** is the **complementary** twin of IR, picking up vibrations that IR is "blind" to.
- **Mass Spectrometry** is the **molecular weighing scale** that gives the exact mass of fragments.
- **XPS** is the **surface-only fingerprint reader** (only the top 1–10 nm).
- **ESR** is the **radical detector**—it hears only the unpaired electrons.

## 1.3 The Electromagnetic Spectrum as the Polymer Analyst's Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The energy of a photon is given by **E = hν = hc/λ**, where **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** (Planck's constant) and **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** (speed of light in vacuum).

Different regions of the spectrum interrogate different molecular features:

| Spectral Region | Wavelength Range | Frequency | Polymer Information |
|---|---|---|---|
| UV-Visible | 200–700 nm | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁵ Hz | π-electrons, conjugation, color |
| IR (Mid) | 2.5–25 µm | 10¹² – 10¹⁴ Hz | Vibrations of bonds |
| Microwave / Radio (NMR) | 0.1–10 m | 10⁷ – 10¹⁰ Hz | Nuclear spin transitions |
| X-ray (XPS) | 0.1–10 nm | 10¹⁶ – 10¹⁹ Hz | Core electron binding energy |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UV-Visible Spectroscopy — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
- A polymer solution (or thin film) is irradiated with UV/visible light.
- Chromophoric groups (C=C, C=O, aromatic rings) absorb photons, promoting π → π* or n → π* electronic transitions.
- The Beer–Lambert law governs the absorbance:

$$ A = \log_{10} \left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right) = \varepsilon \, c \, l $$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless)
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity
- $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = concentration (mol·L⁻¹)
- $l$ = path length (cm)

### 2.1.2 Polymer Applications
1. **Copolymer composition** (e.g., styrene–butadiene ratio) by measuring absorbance at characteristic λ_max.
2. **Conjugated polymer band gap** estimation from UV-vis absorption edge.
3. **Monitoring polymerisation kinetics** (disappearance of monomer peaks).
4. **Degradation studies** (yellowing of polymers indicates formation of new chromophores).

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — UV-Vis

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absorbance | $A = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | dimensionless | Always positive |
| Beer–Lambert Law | $A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$ | ε in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | Valid for dilute solutions |
| Transmittance | $T = I / I_0$ | dimensionless | T = 10⁻ᴬ |
| Band-gap energy | $E_g = hc / \lambda_{\text{onset}}$ | eV | For conjugated polymers |
| Molar absorptivity | $\varepsilon = A / (c \, l)$ | L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | Reported in literature |

## 2.2 FTIR Spectroscopy — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
- Infrared radiation is absorbed when its frequency matches a **molecular vibrational mode** that produces a change in dipole moment.
- For a diatomic harmonic oscillator, the fundamental vibrational frequency is:

$$ \widetilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi c} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} $$

where:
- $\widetilde{\nu}$ = wavenumber (cm⁻¹)
- $k$ = force constant of the bond (N·m⁻¹)
- $\mu$ = reduced mass = $(m_1 m_2) / (m_1 + m_2)$ in kg
- $c$ = speed of light

### 2.2.2 Polymer-Specific IR Absorptions (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Polymer / Group | Characteristic Band | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Vibration Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aliphatic C–H stretch | 2850–2960 | symmetric/asymmetric –CH₂– |
| Aromatic C–H stretch | 3030–3100 | ring –CH |
| O–H stretch (broad) | 3200–3550 | H-bonded hydroxyl |
| N–H stretch | 3300–3500 | primary/secondary amine |
| C=O stretch (ester) | 1735–1750 | carbonyl of PET, PMMA |
| C=O stretch (amide I) | 1630–1680 | polyamides, proteins |
| C=C aromatic | 1450–1600 | ring breathing |
| C–O–C stretch | 1000–1300 | ethers, esters |
| C–Cl stretch | 600–800 | PVC fingerprint |

### 2.2.3 Polymer Applications of FTIR
1. **Identification of unknown polymer** (functional group fingerprinting).
2. **Confirmation of copolymerisation** (appearance of new bands from both monomers).
3. **Crystallinity estimation** (ratio of band intensities, e.g., 974 cm⁻¹ vs 1460 cm⁻¹ in iPP).
4. **Degradation / oxidation** tracking (appearance of carbonyl bands at ~1720 cm⁻¹).
5. **Hydrogen bonding** analysis in polyamides and polyurethanes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule for IR Absorption:** A vibration is IR-active **only if it produces a change in the dipole moment**. Symmetric molecules like N₂ or O₂ do not absorb IR.

## 2.3 NMR Spectroscopy — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
- Nuclei with non-zero spin (e.g., $^1$H, $^{13}$C, $^{19}$F) align with or against an external magnetic field $B_0$.
- The energy gap between the two spin states is:

$$ \Delta E = h \nu = \gamma \, \frac{h}{2\pi} \, B_0 = \hbar \, \gamma \, B_0 $$

where:
- $\gamma$ = gyromagnetic ratio (rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹)
- $B_0$ = applied magnetic field (Tesla)
- $h$ = Planck's constant

The resonance condition (Larmor equation):

$$ \nu_0 = \frac{\gamma \, B_0}{2\pi} $$

### 2.3.2 Chemical Shift
- The local electronic environment "shields" the nucleus from $B_0$.
- Chemical shift δ is defined (independent of instrument frequency):

$$ \delta = \frac{\nu_{\text{sample}} - \nu_{\text{reference}}}{\nu_{\text{spectrometer}}} \times 10^{6} \text{ ppm} $$

- Reference for $^1$H and $^{13}$C NMR: **tetramethylsilane (TMS)**.

### 2.3.3 Polymer Applications of NMR
1. **Tacticity determination** (e.g., isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic polypropylene from $^1$H or $^{13}$C methyl pentad signals).
2. **Copolymer composition** (integration of characteristic monomer peaks).
3. **Sequence distribution** (dyad, triad, pentad analysis).
4. **End-group analysis** for molecular weight estimation (especially for low-MW polymers).
5. **Chain dynamics** via $T_1$ / $T_2$ relaxation measurements.
6. **Solid-state NMR** for insoluble polymers (CP-MAS).

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — NMR & General

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Larmor frequency | $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / (2\pi)$ | Hz |
| Chemical shift | $\delta = (\nu_{\text{samp}} - \nu_{\text{ref}}) / \nu_0 \times 10^{6}$ | ppm |
| Photon energy | $E = h \nu = h c / \lambda$ | J (or eV) |
| IR vibrational freq. | $\widetilde{\nu} = (1 / 2\pi c) \sqrt{k / \mu}$ | cm⁻¹ |

## 2.4 Raman Spectroscopy — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle
- Inelastic scattering of monochromatic laser light.
- Most photons scatter elastically (Rayleigh, ν₀).
- A small fraction (~10⁻⁶) undergoes Raman scattering:
  - **Stokes:** photon loses energy → ν₀ − ν_vib
  - **Anti-Stokes:** photon gains energy → ν₀ + ν_vib (weaker, requires excited state population)

$$ \Delta \widetilde{\nu} = \widetilde{\nu}_0 - \widetilde{\nu}_{\text{scattered}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule for Raman:** A vibration is Raman-active if it produces a change in **polarisability** (not dipole moment). Thus, IR and Raman are **complementary**: symmetric, non-polar bonds (e.g., C=C in polydiene) are strong in Raman but weak in IR.

### 2.4.2 Polymer Applications
1. **Crystallinity ratio** (e.g., 1130 cm⁻¹ / 1080 cm⁻¹ in polyethylene).
2. **Strain mapping** in fibres and films (Raman band shifts under stress).
3. **Identification of stereoregularity** in polydienes (cis-1,4 vs trans-1,4).
4. **Confocal Raman imaging** for 2D/3D chemical mapping of polymer blends.

## 2.5 Mass Spectrometry (MS) — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.5.1 Operating Principle
- Polymer molecules are ionised (often by MALDI – Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionisation, or ESI – Electrospray Ionisation) without excessive fragmentation.
- Ions are separated by their mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$) in a mass analyser (e.g., time-of-flight, TOF).
- For TOF:

$$ t = L \sqrt{\frac{m}{2 z e V}} $$

where:
- $L$ = drift length
- $V$ = accelerating voltage
- $m/z$ = mass-to-charge ratio

### 2.5.2 Polymer Applications
1. **Absolute molecular weight** (M_n, M_w) for low-to-medium MW polymers.
2. **End-group analysis** (chain initiator residues, functional terminations).
3. **Repeat unit identification** (mass difference between adjacent oligomer peaks).
4. **Copolymer sequencing** (modern MS/MS techniques).
5. **Detection of cyclic vs linear chains**.

## 2.6 XPS (ESCA) — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.6.1 Operating Principle
- Soft X-rays (typically Al Kα at 1486.6 eV or Mg Kα at 1253.6 eV) eject core electrons.
- The kinetic energy is measured; the binding energy is calculated:

$$ E_B = h \nu - E_K - \phi $$

where $\phi$ = spectrometer work function.

### 2.6.2 Polymer Applications
1. **Surface chemical composition** (top 1–10 nm) — crucial for adhesion, biocompatibility.
2. **Surface oxidation / contamination** detection.
3. **Surface segregation in polymer blends**.
4. **Plasma / corona treatment** characterisation.

## 2.7 ESR / EPR — Theory and Polymer Applications

### 2.7.1 Operating Principle
- Detects unpaired electrons (paramagnetic species) in a magnetic field.
- Resonance condition:

$$ h \nu = g_e \, \mu_B \, B_0 $$

where $g_e$ ≈ 2.0023 (free electron g-factor), $\mu_B$ = Bohr magneton.

### 2.7.2 Polymer Applications
1. **Monitoring free radicals during polymerisation** (in-situ ESR for radical kinetics).
2. **Detecting radicals trapped in irradiated polymers**.
3. **Studying antioxidant efficiency** in polymer stabiliser formulations.
4. **Spin-probe / spin-label** studies of polymer chain mobility (via nitroxide probes).

## 2.8 Comparative Summary — Choosing the Right Spectroscopic Tool

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Reference Mapping (What to use for what problem):**

| Characterisation Need | Recommended Technique |
|---|---|
| Identify functional groups | FTIR (or Raman) |
| Determine tacticity | $^1$H / $^{13}$C NMR |
| Conjugation / band gap | UV-Vis |
| Surface chemistry (1–10 nm) | XPS |
| Free radicals in polymerisation | ESR |
| Molecular weight (low MW) | MALDI-TOF MS |
| Crystallinity (vibrational) | Raman or FTIR |
| Copolymer composition | $^1$H NMR or FTIR |
| Chain dynamics (ns–μs) | NMR relaxation / ESR spin probe |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked-out analytical derivations** for the most commonly tested KTU numerical and theoretical problems on this topic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Concentration of Chromophoric Groups in a Polymer Solution Using Beer–Lambert Law

> **[Typical 7-mark KTU numerical]**

**Problem Statement:** A polymer sample containing an aromatic chromophore (ε = 1500 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ at λ_max = 260 nm) is dissolved in chloroform. A cuvette of path length 1.0 cm gives an absorbance of 0.450. Calculate the molar concentration of the chromophore.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation (Beer–Lambert Law):**

$$ A = \varepsilon \, c \, l $$

**Step 2: Rearrange for concentration $c$:**

$$ c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \, l} $$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values (A = 0.450, ε = 1500 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, l = 1.0 cm):**

$$ c = \frac{0.450}{(1500)(1.0)} \text{ mol·L}^{-1} $$

**Step 4: Evaluate the denominator:**

$$ c = \frac{0.450}{1500} \text{ mol·L}^{-1} $$

**Step 5: Compute final value:**

$$ c = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol·L}^{-1} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $c = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol·L⁻¹ (= 0.30 mM)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculating the Optical Band Gap of a Conjugated Polymer from UV-Vis Absorption Edge

**Problem Statement:** A thin film of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) shows an absorption onset at λ = 650 nm. Calculate the optical band gap $E_g$ in eV.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1: Write the photon energy equation:**

$$ E = \frac{h c}{\lambda} $$

**Step 2: Substitute fundamental constants — Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, speed of light $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}$ m·s⁻¹, wavelength $\lambda = 650$ nm = $650 \times 10^{-9}$ m:**

$$ E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}{650 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ J} $$

**Step 3: Simplify numerator and denominator separately:**

$$ E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.50 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ J} $$

**Step 4: Perform the division:**

$$ E = 3.058 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

**Step 5: Convert J to eV using the conversion factor 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J:**

$$ E_g = \frac{3.058 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} $$

**Step 6: Final evaluation:**

$$ E_g \approx 1.91 \text{ eV} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $E_g \approx 1.91$ eV — consistent with reported P3HT band gap (~1.9–2.0 eV).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Larmor Frequency and Required NMR Spectrometer Frequency for $^1$H at a Given Field

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the Larmor (resonance) frequency for $^1$H nuclei in a spectrometer operating at $B_0$ = 11.74 T. Given: $\gamma_{^1H} = 2.675 \times 10^{8}$ rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1: Write the Larmor frequency equation in angular form:**

$$ \omega_0 = \gamma \, B_0 $$

**Step 2: Substitute values ($\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^{8}$ rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹, $B_0 = 11.74$ T):**

$$ \omega_0 = (2.675 \times 10^{8})(11.74) \text{ rad·s}^{-1} $$

**Step 3: Evaluate:**

$$ \omega_0 = 3.140 \times 10^{9} \text{ rad·s}^{-1} $$

**Step 4: Convert to ordinary frequency $\nu_0$ using $\nu_0 = \omega_0 / (2\pi)$:**

$$ \nu_0 = \frac{3.140 \times 10^{9}}{2 \pi} \text{ Hz} $$

**Step 5: Evaluate final numerical answer:**

$$ \nu_0 = \frac{3.140 \times 10^{9}}{6.2832} = 4.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz} \approx 500 \text{ MHz} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\nu_0 \approx 500$ MHz — confirming this is a standard 500 MHz NMR spectrometer (commonly used for polymer NMR).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vibrational Frequency of a C–H Bond Using Harmonic Oscillator Model

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the IR stretching wavenumber for a C–H bond, given $k \approx 500$ N·m⁻¹ and atomic masses $m_C = 12$ u, $m_H = 1$ u. (1 u = $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg)

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the reduced mass $\mu$:**

$$ \mu = \frac{m_C \, m_H}{m_C + m_H} $$

**Step 2: Convert to kg:**

$$ m_C = 12 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 1.9926 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg} $$
$$ m_H = 1 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3: Compute reduced mass:**

$$ \mu = \frac{(1.9926 \times 10^{-26})(1.6605 \times 10^{-27})}{(1.9926 \times 10^{-26} + 1.6605 \times 10^{-27})} \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4: Numerator:**

$$ \text{Num} = 3.308 \times 10^{-53} \text{ kg}^{2} $$

**Step 5: Denominator:**

$$ \text{Den} = 2.159 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg} $$

**Step 6: Divide:**

$$ \mu = 1.532 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg} \quad (\approx 0.923 \text{ u}) $$

**Step 7: Apply the harmonic oscillator formula for wavenumber:**

$$ \widetilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi c} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} $$

**Step 8: Substitute $k = 500$ N·m⁻¹, $\mu = 1.532 \times 10^{-27}$ kg, $c = 3.0 \times 10^{10}$ cm·s⁻¹:**

$$ \widetilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi (3.0 \times 10^{10})} \sqrt{\frac{500}{1.532 \times 10^{-27}}} \text{ cm}^{-1} $$

**Step 9: Compute square-root term:**

$$ \sqrt{3.264 \times 10^{29}} = 5.712 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 10: Divide by $2\pi c = 1.885 \times 10^{11}$ cm·s⁻¹:**

$$ \widetilde{\nu} \approx 3030 \text{ cm}^{-1} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\widetilde{\nu} \approx 3030$ cm⁻¹ — matches the experimental C–H aromatic / aliphatic stretch region in FTIR spectra.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Comparison of Spectroscopic Techniques

| Parameter | UV-Vis | FTIR | $^1$H NMR | Raman | MALDI-TOF | XPS | ESR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Probe | Photons (UV/Vis) | IR photons | Radio + B₀ | Visible laser photons | Ionised molecules | Soft X-rays | Microwave + B₀ |
| Information | π-electrons | Bonds & groups | Local H environment | Symmetric vibrations | m/z of intact chains | Surface atoms (1–10 nm) | Unpaired e⁻ |
| Sample state | Solution or film | Solid, film, solution | Solution (or solid-state) | Solid, film | Solid, solution | Solid (ultra-high vac.) | Solid, solution |
| Quantitative? | Yes (Beer–Lambert) | Semi-quantitative | Yes (integration) | Semi-quantitative | Yes (peak areas) | Semi-quantitative | Yes (double integration) |
| Typical polymer use | Conjugation, λ_max | Functional ID | Tacticity, copolymer ratio | Crystallinity, strain | M_w, end groups | Surface chemistry | Radical kinetics |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a Spectrometer Maps a Polymer's Fingerprint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Sample] --> B[Source of Probe Radiation]
    B --> C[Sample Interaction Zone]
    C --> D[Detector System]
    D --> E[Signal Processor & Computer]
    E --> F[Spectra Plot Wavelength vs Intensity]
    F --> G[Polymer Property Interpretation]

    B --> H1[UV-Vis Lamp]
    B --> H2[IR Source Globar]
    B --> H3[Radiofrequency Pulse NMR]
    B --> H4[Laser Source Raman]
    B --> H5[Soft X-ray Source XPS]
    B --> H6[Microwave Source ESR]

    C --> I1[Electronic Transition pi to pi star]
    C --> I2[Vibrational Excitation of Bonds]
    C --> I3[Nuclear Spin Flip in B0]
    C --> I4[Inelastic Light Scattering]
    C --> I5[Photoelectron Emission]
    C --> I6[Electron Spin Resonance]

    G --> J1[Conjugation Band Gap]
    G --> J2[Functional Group ID]
    G --> J3[Tacticity and Sequence]
    G --> J4[Crystallinity Strain]
    G --> J5[Molecular Weight End Groups]
    G --> J6[Surface Composition]
    G --> J7[Radical Concentration]
```

## 4.2 Decision-Flow Diagram — Choosing a Spectroscopic Method for a Polymer Characterisation Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What polymer property do you need?] --> Q2{Functional group identification?}
    Q1 --> Q3{Tacticity or sequence?}
    Q1 --> Q4{Surface only top 10 nm?}
    Q1 --> Q5{Free radicals present?}
    Q1 --> Q6{Molecular weight below 50 kDa?}
    Q1 --> Q7{Conjugation or color?}

    Q2 --> R1[Use FTIR or Raman]
    Q3 --> R2[Use 1H or 13C NMR]
    Q4 --> R3[Use XPS]
    Q5 --> R4[Use ESR]
    Q6 --> R5[Use MALDI-TOF MS]
    Q7 --> R6[Use UV-Vis Spectroscopy]
```

## 4.3 Energy-Level Diagram of a Spectroscopic Measurement (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Ground State Molecule] -- Probe Photon of Energy h nu --> K2[Excited State]
    K2 -- Emitted or Transmitted Photon --> K3[Detector]

```

flowchart LR

```

    P6 --> P7[Fit to Mz equals M0 1 minus 2 exp tau over T1]
```
