# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing IR and Raman selection rules** — students often write "all vibrations are IR-active". Always state: **IR needs a change in dipole moment; Raman needs a change in polarisability**.
> 2. **Beer–Lambert Law is only valid in dilute solutions** — high concentrations show deviations due to solute–solute interactions.
> 3. **In NMR chemical shift, students forget the 10⁶ factor** — δ is reported in **ppm**, not in Hz.
> 4. **For MALDI-TOF MS, students must specify the ionisation source** — answers mentioning "mass spec" alone will be marked down.
> 5. **ESR detects ONLY unpaired electrons** — diamagnetic polymers give no signal. Mentioning this distinction earns full credit.
> 6. **Units mismatch in numerical problems** — always state final units explicitly (e.g., mol·L⁻¹, cm⁻¹, MHz).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between the IR and Raman selection rules. Give one example of a vibration that is strong in Raman but weak in IR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | IR Spectroscopy | Raman Spectroscopy |
|---|---|---|
| Selection rule | Change in **dipole moment** | Change in **polarisability** |
| Bond-type preference | Polar bonds (C=O, O–H, N–H) | Non-polar / symmetric bonds (C=C, S–S) |
| Typical polymer use | Ester, amide, hydroxyl group detection | Skeletal C–C, C=C in polydienes, crystallinity |

**Example:** The C=C stretch in **polybutadiene (or natural rubber)** appears strongly in Raman (~1660 cm⁻¹) but is weak in IR because the symmetric C=C bond has little dipole change.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**State the Beer–Lambert Law. Mention two polymer-characterisation applications of UV-Vis spectroscopy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The Beer–Lambert law states that the absorbance of a solution is directly proportional to the molar concentration of the absorbing species and the optical path length:

$$ A = \varepsilon \, c \, l $$

where $A$ = absorbance, $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient, $c$ = concentration, $l$ = path length.

**Two polymer applications:**
1. **Copolymer composition analysis** — measuring the absorbance of a styrene unit at 260 nm to determine the styrene content in SBR.
2. **Band-gap estimation of conjugated polymers** — the absorption onset wavelength λ gives $E_g = hc/\lambda$ for polymers like P3HT and polypyrrole.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Both alternatives (A and B) are provided below for full practice coverage.**

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of FTIR spectroscopy. Discuss its application in the identification of functional groups and crystallinity estimation in polymers with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the principle of $^1$H NMR spectroscopy. With a neat sketch, explain how NMR can be used to determine the **tacticity** of polypropylene.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — FTIR Principle & Applications (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- FTIR uses an **interferometer (Michelson type)** to produce an **interferogram**, which is mathematically converted (Fourier transform) into a conventional IR spectrum.
- A polymer sample absorbs IR radiation when the photon frequency matches a **molecular vibrational mode** (stretching, bending, scissoring, rocking).
- The fundamental condition is the **change in dipole moment** during vibration.

The wavenumber of absorption is given by:

$$ \widetilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi c} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} $$

**Identification of functional groups (2 marks):**

| Group | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Polymer Example |
|---|---|---|
| C=O ester | 1735–1750 | PET, PMMA |
| N–H amide | 3300 (stretch), 1640 (amide I) | Nylon-6,6 |
| O–H broad | 3200–3550 | PVA, cellulose |
| C=C aromatic | 1450–1600 | Polystyrene |
| C–H aliphatic | 2850–2960 | Polyethylene, PP |

**Crystallinity estimation (2 marks):**
- In **isotactic polypropylene (iPP)**, the bands at 974 cm⁻¹ (crystalline) and 1460 cm⁻¹ (reference) give a crystallinity index:

$$ X_c (\%) = \frac{A_{974}}{A_{1460}} \times 100 $$

- For **PET**, the ratio of 1340 cm⁻¹ (trans conformation, crystalline) to 1370 cm⁻¹ (gauche, amorphous) is used.

---

**Part (b) — NMR Principle & Tacticity of PP (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- $^1$H nuclei behave as tiny magnets. In an external field $B_0$, they precess at the **Larmor frequency** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / (2\pi)$.
- A radiofrequency (RF) pulse at $\nu_0$ flips the spins; the emitted signal (FID — free induction decay) is Fourier-transformed to a spectrum.
- The local **chemical environment** shifts the resonance — this is the **chemical shift δ (ppm)**.
- The peak area (integral) is proportional to the number of nuclei producing it.

**Tacticity of Polypropylene (4 marks):**

Polypropylene repeat unit: –[CH₂–CH(CH₃)]ₙ–

The three tactic forms differ in the **relative stereochemistry** of consecutive CH₃ groups:

- **Isotactic (it-PP):** all CH₃ on the same side → produces a sharp, well-defined $^{13}$C pentad.
- **Syndiotactic (s-PP):** CH₃ groups alternating → distinct pentad pattern.
- **Atactic (a-PP):** random placement → broad unresolved peaks.

In the $^1$H NMR spectrum, the **methyl (–CH₃) proton resonance** at ~0.85–0.95 ppm splits into multiple peaks depending on **triad tacticity (mm, mr, rr)**:

| Triad | Chemical Shift (ppm) | Assignment |
|---|---|---|
| mm (isotactic) | ~0.85 | All-CH₃-on-same-side triad |
| mr (heterotactic) | ~0.88 | Mixed |
| rr (syndiotactic) | ~0.92 | Alternating |

**Tacticity is calculated** by integrating the three peaks:

$$ \% \, mm = \frac{I_{mm}}{I_{mm} + I_{mr} + I_{rr}} \times 100 $$

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Sketch (Isotactic vs Syndiotactic vs Atactic PP):** Draw three zig-zag carbon backbones with –CH₃ side groups; in *iso*, all CH₃ wedge up; in *syndio*, CH₃ alternates up/down; in *atactic*, CH₃ placement is random.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the principle of **UV-Vis spectroscopy** and explain its application in determining the **optical band gap of a conjugated polymer** (e.g., polythiophene). Show the calculation of $E_g$ from a given absorption onset.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of **MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry** for polymers. Discuss its application in determining **molecular weight** and **end-group identification** of a polymer synthesised by anionic polymerisation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — UV-Vis Principle & Band Gap Calculation (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- UV-Vis spectroscopy measures the absorption of ultraviolet/visible light by a polymer.
- Absorption corresponds to **electronic transitions** of chromophoric groups: $\pi \rightarrow \pi^*$ (in C=C, aromatic) and $n \rightarrow \pi^*$ (in C=O).
- Quantitative analysis uses the **Beer–Lambert law**: $A = \varepsilon c l$.

**Band-gap determination of polythiophene (4 marks):**
1. Record the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of a thin film.
2. Identify the **absorption onset wavelength** $\lambda_{\text{onset}}$ — the wavelength where the absorbance begins to rise from the baseline.
3. Apply the Tauc relation for direct band-gap semiconductors (typical for conjugated polymers):

$$ (\alpha h \nu)^{2} = A (h \nu - E_g) $$

4. Alternatively, approximate $E_g$ from $\lambda_{\text{onset}}$:

$$ E_g = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\text{onset}}} $$

**Numerical example:** A poly(3-hexylthiophene) film shows $\lambda_{\text{onset}} = 650$ nm.

$$ E_g = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{650 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ E_g = 3.06 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = \frac{3.06 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.91 \text{ eV} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $E_g \approx 1.91$ eV — matches P3HT literature.

---

**Part (b) — MALDI-TOF MS for Polymers (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- The polymer is co-crystallised with a small "matrix" molecule (e.g., **2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, DHB**).
- A pulsed laser desorbs and ionises the polymer–matrix mix — this is the **Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionisation (MALDI)** step.
- The ionised polymer is accelerated by a known voltage $V$ and travels through a field-free **time-of-flight (TOF)** tube of length $L$.

The TOF equation:

$$ t = L \sqrt{\frac{m}{2 z e V}} $$

- Lighter ions reach the detector first.
- A plot of intensity vs $m/z$ gives a **mass spectrum** showing a series of peaks separated by the **repeat unit mass**.

**Molecular weight determination (2 marks):**
- The peak series is identified. Each adjacent peak differs by $M_0$ (mass of repeat unit).
- Number-average $M_n$ is calculated from:

$$ M_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} $$

where $N_i$ is the abundance of oligomer with mass $M_i$.

**End-group identification (2 marks):**
- For **anionic polymerisation of styrene** initiated by *n*-BuLi, the polymer has the structure: *n*-Bu–(CH₂–CHPh)ₙ–H.
- The mass of each oligomer is:

$$ M_i = M_{\text{end-groups}} + n \cdot M_0 = (57 + 1) + n \cdot 104.15 = 58 + 104.15 n $$

where 57 = mass of *n*-butyl, 1 = mass of terminal H, 104.15 = mass of styrene repeat unit.

- Plot of $m/z$ vs $n$ yields a straight line; the **y-intercept** reveals the **end-group mass**, confirming the initiator residue and termination mechanism.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bulletins — Pin These!**

- ✅ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — valid only for **dilute solutions**.
- ✅ **IR selection rule** = change in **dipole moment**; **Raman selection rule** = change in **polarisability**.
- ✅ **IR & Raman are complementary**, not competing.
- ✅ **NMR chemical shift δ is in ppm**; TMS is the reference at 0 ppm.
- ✅ **NMR Larmor equation:** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / (2\pi)$ — at 11.74 T, $^1$H resonates at ~500 MHz.
- ✅ **PP tacticity** is determined by integrating the **methyl $^1$H triad peaks** (mm, mr, rr) at ~0.85–0.92 ppm.
- ✅ **Band gap of conjugated polymer:** $E_g = hc / \lambda_{\text{onset}}$; for P3HT, ~1.9 eV.
- ✅ **MALDI-TOF** gives absolute $M_n$ for **low-MW polymers (< 50 kDa)**.
- ✅ **XPS** probes only the **top 1–10 nm** — ideal for adhesion, biocompatibility, surface oxidation.
- ✅ **ESR** detects **unpaired electrons only** — used in radical polymerisation kinetics and antioxidant studies.
- ✅ **FTIR wavenumber for C=O (ester) ≈ 1735 cm⁻¹**; **N–H ≈ 3300 cm⁻¹**; **O–H (broad) ≈ 3200–3550 cm⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Crystallinity of iPP** = $A_{974} / A_{1460}$ ratio from FTIR.
- ✅ **Plank's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; **1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J**.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers; examiners award **partial marks** for correct unit even if the number is wrong.
- ✅ For **KTU 14-mark questions**, structure the answer in **headings + sub-points + neat diagram** for easy valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Processing and Engineering Applications: Plastic Processing Technology - Injection Moulding, Compression Moulding, Calendaring, Blow Moulding, Extrusion, and Thermoforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polymer Processing and Engineering Applications: Plastic Processing Technology - Injection Moulding, Compression Moulding, Calendaring, Blow Moulding, Extrusion, and Thermoforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Polymer Processing — The Engineering Discipline

**Polymer Processing** is defined as the set of manufacturing operations by which raw polymeric materials (in the form of powders, granules, melts, or sheets) are converted into finished or semi-finished products of desired shape, size, and mechanical integrity. The discipline combines elements of **fluid mechanics**, **heat transfer**, **rheology**, and **tool/die design**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Plastic processing technology refers to the conversion of thermoplastic or thermosetting polymers into finished articles through the application of heat, pressure, and mechanical forming operations using specially engineered machinery.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polymer is Like a Cooked Food"

Imagine a polymer as a **cooked noodle dough**:

- **Heating** the dough makes it soft and moldable (analogous to melting the polymer above its $T_g$ or $T_m$).
- **Pressing/Squeezing** through a shaped opening shapes it (analogous to extrusion).
- **Filling** into a closed shaped box and cooling locks the geometry (analogous to moulding).
- **Blowing air** into a molten bubble creates hollow containers (analogous to blow moulding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Universal Variables in ALL Polymer Processing:**
> 1. **Temperature (T)** — controls melt viscosity and flow.
> 2. **Pressure (P)** — drives flow into the cavity.
> 3. **Time (t)** — determines cooling/solidification rate.

### 1.3 Classification of Plastic Processing Technologies

| Category | Mechanism | Processes Covered |
|---|---|---|
| **Melt Processing (Thermoplastics)** | Molten polymer is shaped by pressure | Injection Moulding, Extrusion, Blow Moulding |
| **Form Processing** | Soft polymer sheet is reshaped | Thermoforming, Calendaring |
| **Compression Processing (Thermosets)** | Heat + pressure cure the resin | Compression Moulding |
| **Casting** | Liquid resin poured into mould | (Not in this module) |

### 1.4 The Six Processes at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Snapshot — Six Processes:**

| # | Process | Feedstock Form | Product Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Injection Moulding** | Granules / Powder | Complex 3D solid parts |
| 2 | **Compression Moulding** | Powder / Preform | Large flat/curved parts |
| 3 | **Calendaring** | Melt/Paste | Thin sheets/films |
| 4 | **Blow Moulding** | Molten Parison | Hollow containers |
| 5 | **Extrusion** | Granules | Continuous profiles |
| 6 | **Thermoforming** | Sheet | Shallow drawn parts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 INJECTION MOULDING (IM)

### 2.1.1 Definition
**Injection Moulding** is a cyclic, closed-loop manufacturing process in which a measured quantity of molten thermoplastic (or thermoset) polymer is injected under high pressure into a closed, water-cooled mould cavity; the part solidifies by cooling, and the mould opens to eject the solidified part.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Theory

The process operates as a **cyclic batch process** with the following sequential phases:

1. **Feeding (Metering):** Plastic granules fall from the hopper into the heated barrel via gravity; the rotating screw (or plunger in older machines) conveys them forward. Friction + external heaters melt the polymer.
2. **Melting (Plasticization):** As the screw rotates, the polymer melts due to conduction from barrel heaters and viscous shear dissipation. The molten polymer accumulates in front of the screw, pushing the screw backward like a syringe.
3. **Injection (Filling):** The screw stops rotating and acts as a plunger. It moves forward axially, injecting the melt through the nozzle into the mould cavity at pressures of **70–140 MPa**.
4. **Packing/Holding:** After the cavity is filled (~95–98%), additional melt is forced in to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** during cooling. Pressure is held.
5. **Cooling:** Water circulating through mould channels extracts heat. The part solidifies when temperature drops below $T_g$ (for amorphous) or $T_c$ (for semi-crystalline).
6. **Ejection:** The mould opens; ejector pins push the part out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle Time = Fill Time + Hold Time + Cooling Time + Mold Open/Close Time**
> Cooling typically consumes **50–80%** of the total cycle time.

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Injection Moulding

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shrinkage Allowance** | $S = \dfrac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}{L_{mould}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical: 0.3–3% for thermoplastics |
| **Cavity Pressure** | $P_c \approx P_{inj} - \Delta P_{runner}$ | MPa | Drives fill |
| **Clamping Force** | $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \cdot A_{proj}$ | kN | $A_{proj}$ = projected area |
| **Heat to be Removed** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot \Delta H_f$ | kJ | Sensible + Latent heat |
| **Cooling Time (approx)** | $t_c \approx \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}$ | s | $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $h$ = wall thickness |
| **Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q_v = \dfrac{V_{part}}{\theta_f}$ | cm³/s | $\theta_f$ = fill time |

---

## 2.2 COMPRESSION MOULDING

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Compression Moulding** is a process in which a predetermined charge of thermosetting polymer (powder, preform, or SMC/BMC sheet) is placed directly into a heated mould cavity; the mould is then closed under high pressure, forcing the material to flow and conform to the cavity shape while heat triggers an irreversible crosslinking reaction (cure).

### 2.2.2 Theory — Stepwise

1. **Charge Preparation:** A pre-weighed amount of resin (with filler, catalyst, pigment) is placed in the lower (female) mould half.
2. **Mould Closing:** The upper (male) half descends, compressing the charge. Pressure: **10–200 MPa**.
3. **Flow & Cure:** The heat (typically **150–200 °C**) causes the resin to flow, fill the cavity, and undergo crosslinking. Viscosity first decreases (melting), then increases sharply as cure proceeds.
4. **Holding:** Pressure is maintained during cure to prevent flash and voids.
5. **Mould Opening & Ejection:** Once cured (rigid), the part is ejected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compression vs Injection:** Compression moulding is preferred for **thermosets** (phenolic, melamine, epoxy, BMC, SMC) because the long cure time makes screw plasticization inefficient. Compression moulded parts have **lower residual stresses** but **longer cycle times** than injection moulded parts.

### 2.2.3 Formula Sheet — Compression Moulding

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Cure Time (rule of thumb)** | $t_{cure} \approx k \cdot h^2$ | $k$ depends on resin |
| **Mould Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{cavity}}$ | MPa |
| **Bulk Factor** | $BF = \dfrac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{charge}}$ | >1 for powder → mould must overfill |

---

## 2.3 CALENDARING

### 2.3.1 Definition
**Calendaring** is a continuous process in which a heated polymer mass (often PVC plastisol or melt) is passed through a series of counter-rotating heated rolls (a "calendar") to produce thin sheets or films of uniform thickness.

### 2.3.2 Theory — The Calendar Stack

A typical **4-roll calendar (inverted L)** arrangement:

$$\text{Roll 1 (Feed)} \rightarrow \text{Roll 2} \rightarrow \text{Roll 3} \rightarrow \text{Roll 4 (Take-off)}$$

- The **nip** (gap between two adjacent rolls) determines the final sheet thickness.
- Each successive nip has a smaller gap.
- Roll speeds are matched; the **friction ratio** (typically 1.1–1.5) introduces shear that smooths the surface.

### 2.3.3 Stepwise Operation

1. **Compounding:** PVC resin + plasticizer + stabilizer + filler are mixed in a Banbury mixer or extruder to form a homogeneous dough at **140–180 °C**.
2. **Feeding:** The dough is fed continuously into the first nip.
3. **Rolling:** Material passes through successive nips, each reducing thickness by **~50%**.
4. **Take-off & Cooling:** The thin sheet (0.05–1 mm) is cooled on chilled rolls and wound.

### 2.3.4 Formula Sheet — Calendaring

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Sheet Thickness** | $h = 2 R \left(1 - \cos\theta\right)$ | mm |
| **Throughput** | $Q = \pi D N h W$ | mm³/s |
| **Friction Ratio** | $FR = \dfrac{V_{faster}}{V_{slower}}$ | dimensionless |

where: $D$ = roll diameter, $N$ = roll speed, $h$ = sheet thickness, $W$ = sheet width, $R$ = roll radius, $\theta$ = nip angle.

---

## 2.4 BLOW MOULDING

### 2.4.1 Definition
**Blow Moulding** is a process in which a molten thermoplastic tube (parison) is inflated by compressed air inside a closed mould cavity to take the shape of a hollow container.

### 2.4.2 Three Sub-Types

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Variants of Blow Moulding:**
> 1. **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** — parison is extruded.
> 2. **Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)** — parison is injection-moulded on a preform.
> 3. **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** — parison is stretched axially + radially (used for PET bottles).

### 2.4.3 Stepwise (Extrusion Blow Moulding)

1. **Parison Extrusion:** A continuous molten tube is extruded vertically downward from an annular die.
2. **Mould Closing:** A two-piece mould closes around the parison, pinching one end.
3. **Inflation:** A blow pin injects compressed air (**0.3–1 MPa**) into the parison, which expands to fill the mould cavity.
4. **Cooling:** Water-cooled mould chills the part.
5. **Ejection & Deflashing:** Mould opens; excess flash is trimmed.

### 2.4.4 Formula Sheet — Blow Moulding

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Blow Ratio (B)** | $B = \dfrac{D_{bottle}}{D_{parison}}$ | Typically 2–4 |
| **Stretch Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{bottle}}{L_{parison}}$ | SBM: 2.5–4 |
| **Total Orientation** | $\lambda_{total} = B \times \lambda$ | Higher → better barrier |
| **Die Swell** | $B_s = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | Due to elastic recovery |
| **Parison Length** | $L_p = L_{bottle} + L_{pinch-off}$ | Includes flash allowance |

---

## 2.5 EXTRUSION

### 2.5.1 Definition
**Extrusion** is a continuous process in which a polymer melt is forced through a shaping **die** to produce a product of constant cross-section (rods, pipes, sheets, films, fibres).

### 2.5.2 Theory — The Single Screw Extruder

The extruder is the **workhorse** of polymer processing. Its zones:

1. **Feed Zone (Hopper End):** Solid pellets are conveyed forward; temperature ≈ ambient.
2. **Compression/Melting Zone:** Polymer melts due to barrel heat + shear; screw depth decreases.
3. **Metering Zone:** Fully molten polymer is pressurized and pumped at uniform rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extruder Output Equation:**
> $$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N D \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D^3 \sin^2\phi \, \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}$$
> where:
> - $Q$ = volumetric throughput (m³/s)
> - $D$ = screw diameter
> - $N$ = screw rotational speed (rev/s)
> - $\phi$ = helix angle (typically **17–20°**)
> - $\Delta P$ = pressure rise along the screw
> - $\eta$ = melt viscosity
> - $L$ = screw length

This is called the **Newtonian model** for single-screw extrusion.

### 2.5.3 Operating Characteristics — Drag Flow vs Pressure Flow

| Flow Component | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Drag Flow ($Q_d$)** | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi$ | Forward flow due to screw rotation |
| **Pressure Flow ($Q_p$)** | $Q_p = -\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\phi \, \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ | Backward flow due to die resistance |
| **Net Flow ($Q_{net}$)** | $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$ | Actual extruder output |

where $H$ = channel depth.

### 2.5.4 Extrusion Process Steps

1. **Feeding** — Pellets enter via hopper.
2. **Conveying & Melting** — Screw transports and melts polymer.
3. **Metering & Pressurization** — Screw pumps melt uniformly.
4. **Die Forming** — Melt is shaped by die (profile, pipe, sheet die).
5. **Calibration & Cooling** — Sizing sleeves + water/spray coolers solidify.
6. **Haul-off & Cutting** — Pullers (caterpillar) advance; cutter cuts to length.

### 2.5.5 Formula Sheet — Extrusion

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **L/D Ratio** | $L/D$ | Typical 20–30 (modern: 36–40) |
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{H_{feed}}{H_{meter}}$ | Typical 2:1 to 4:1 |
| **Melt Temperature** | $T_{melt} = T_{barrel} + \Delta T_{shear}$ | $\Delta T_{shear}$ from viscous dissipation |
| **Specific Energy** | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{Q_{mass}}$ | kWh/kg, typically 0.15–0.30 |
| **Die Swell** | $B_s = \dfrac{D_{out}}{D_{die}}$ | Function of $\gamma$ and relaxation time $\lambda$ |

---

## 2.6 THERMOFORMING

### 2.6.1 Definition
**Thermoforming** is a secondary process in which a flat thermoplastic sheet is heated to a pliable (rubbery) state and forced against a mould by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means; the part is cooled, trimmed, and ejected.

### 2.6.2 Stepwise Theory

1. **Sheet Clamping:** Thermoplastic sheet is clamped in a frame.
2. **Heating:** Sheet is heated to forming temperature, typically **$T_g + 30\text{ to }60$ °C** for amorphous polymers.
3. **Forming (Shaping):** Sheet is forced against the mould by one of:
   - **Vacuum Forming** — air evacuation through mould pores (most common).
   - **Pressure Forming** — compressed air pushes sheet.
   - **Mechanical Forming** — matched die punches sheet.
   - **Drape Forming** — sheet drapes over a male mould.
4. **Cooling:** Sheet cools in contact with mould.
5. **Trimming:** Excess material (skeleton) is removed.

### 2.6.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Thermoforming

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Forming Temperature (Amorphous)** | $T_f \approx T_g + (30 \text{ to } 60)°C$ | |
| **Forming Temperature (Semi-Crystalline)** | $T_f \approx T_m - (5 \text{ to } 20)°C$ | Narrow window |
| **Vacuum Required** | $P_{vac} \geq 0.09 \text{ MPa}$ | Atmospheric pressure pushes sheet |
| **Material Distribution** | Depends on plug-assist design | Plug-assist improves wall uniformity |
| **Male vs Female** | Female → wider, easier; Male → better detail | |
| **Draw Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Width}}$ | <1 easy; 1–2 plug-assist needed |

---

## 2.7 COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS — All Six Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Selection Decision Matrix:**

| Feature | Injection | Compression | Calendaring | Blow | Extrusion | Thermoforming |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cycle Type** | Cyclic | Cyclic | Continuous | Cyclic | Continuous | Cyclic |
| **Polymer Type** | Thermo/Thermoset | Mostly Thermoset | Mostly PVC | Thermoplastic | Thermoplastic | Thermoplastic |
| **Shape Complexity** | Very High | Moderate | Sheet only | Hollow only | Profile only | Shallow |
| **Production Volume** | High | Medium | Very High | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Tool Cost** | High | High | Very High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Wall Thickness Control** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Typical Products** | Buckets, gears, housings | Switch boards, helmets | PVC sheets | Bottles, tanks | Pipes, films, rods | Cups, trays, tubs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Single Screw Extruder Output Equation

We start with the geometry of the melt channel "unwrapped" into a flat rectangular channel of width $W = \pi D \sin\phi$ and depth $H$, with the upper wall (barrel) moving at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ relative to the lower wall (screw root).

**Step 1: Velocity Field**
We assume steady, laminar, isothermal flow with no-slip at walls. The general velocity field is:

$$v_z = v_z(x)$$

where $x$ is the cross-channel coordinate (0 at root, $H$ at barrel).

**Step 2: Apply the Equation of Motion**
For pressure + drag flow in the $z$-direction (channel length), the Navier-Stokes reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial x^2}$$

Solving twice with boundary conditions $v_z(0) = 0$ and $v_z(H) = V_b$:

$$v_z(x) = V_b \cdot \frac{x}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(Hx - x^2\right)$$

**Step 3: Integrate to Obtain Flow Rate**
The volumetric flow rate per unit width is:

$$q = \int_0^H v_z \, dx$$

Substituting:

$$q = \int_0^H \left[V_b \cdot \frac{x}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(Hx - x^2\right)\right] dx$$

Evaluating the integrals term-by-term:

$$q = \frac{V_b H}{2} - \frac{H^3}{12\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate (3D)**
Multiply by channel width $W = \pi D \sin\phi$, and use $V_b = \pi D N$:

$$Q = q \cdot W = \frac{V_b H W}{2} - \frac{H^3 W}{12\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

Now, $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ (pressure drops linearly along channel length $L$), and substituting:

$$Q = \underbrace{\frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi}{2}}_{Q_{drag}} - \underbrace{\frac{\pi D H^3 \sin\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}_{Q_{pressure}}$$

Using $V_b = \pi D N$:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}$$

This is the **fundamental extrusion equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - **First term (Drag flow):** Forward flow caused by the moving barrel relative to the screw channel.
> - **Second term (Pressure flow):** Backward flow caused by the die resistance at the screw tip.
> - Net output = Drag − Pressure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Injection Moulding Cycle

**Problem:** An injection moulding machine produces a polypropylene part of mass **50 g**. Specific heat $c_p = 1.9$ kJ/kg·K. Latent heat $\Delta H_f = 100$ kJ/kg. Melt temperature $T_m = 230 °C$. Mould temperature $T_{mould} = 40 °C$.

**Find:** (a) Heat to be removed per part; (b) Cooling time for $h = 3$ mm wall thickness, given $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s.

### Step 1: Convert Units
$$m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.050 \text{ kg}$$
$$\Delta T = 230 - 40 = 190 °C = 190 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Heat (Sensible + Latent)
Total heat = sensible heat + latent heat:

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 0.050 \times 1.9 \times 190$$

Computing stepwise:
$$0.050 \times 1.9 = 0.095$$
$$0.095 \times 190 = 18.05 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot \Delta H_f = 0.050 \times 100 = 5.00 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 18.05 + 5.00 = 23.05 \text{ kJ}}$$

### Step 3: Cooling Time
$$t_c \approx \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{(0.003)^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Computing:
$$(0.003)^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$$
$$9 \times 10^{-6} / 9.8696 \times 10^{-7} \approx 9.12 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 9.1 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Calendaring Throughput

**Problem:** A 4-roll calendar has roll diameter $D = 600$ mm, sheet thickness $h = 0.5$ mm, sheet width $W = 1500$ mm, and roll speed $N = 30$ rpm.

**Find:** Volumetric throughput $Q$.

### Step 1: Convert RPM to rev/s
$$N = \frac{30}{60} = 0.5 \text{ rev/s}$$

### Step 2: Apply Throughput Formula
$$Q = \pi D N h W$$
$$Q = \pi \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.0005 \times 1.5$$

Step-by-step:
$$\pi \times 0.6 = 1.885$$
$$1.885 \times 0.5 = 0.9425$$
$$0.9425 \times 0.0005 = 4.712 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$4.712 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 7.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to cm³/s:
$$\boxed{Q \approx 707 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for a Given Product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix (Score 1–5, higher = better suitability):**

| Product | Injection | Compression | Calendaring | Blow | Extrusion | Thermoforming |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PET bottle | 2 | 1 | 1 | **5** | 2 | 1 |
| PVC pipe | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **5** | 1 |
| Helmet (ABS) | 3 | **5** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| Gear (Nylon) | **5** | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| PVC sheet (transparent) | 1 | 1 | **5** | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| Cup (PS) | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | **5** |
| Fuel tank (HDPE) | 3 | 2 | 1 | **5** | 2 | 1 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define injection moulding. List any three advantages and three disadvantages.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Injection moulding is a cyclic manufacturing process in which a measured quantity of molten polymer is injected under high pressure (70–140 MPa) into a closed, water-cooled mould cavity, where it solidifies into the desired shape before being ejected.

**Advantages (½ mark each):**
1. Very high production rate (cycle time 5–60 s).
2. Excellent dimensional accuracy and surface finish.
3. Low scrap rate (sprue/runner can be reground).

**Disadvantages (½ mark each):**
1. High initial tool cost.
2. Limited to small-to-medium part size.
3. Long lead time for mould fabrication.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between extrusion blow moulding and injection blow moulding.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM) | Injection Blow Moulding (IBM) |
|---|---|---|
| Parison formation | Continuous extrusion | Injection-moulded preform |
| Process | Two stages (extrude + blow) | Three stages (inject + condition + blow) |
| Material waste | Higher (flash + neck) | Lower (no flash) |
| Production rate | Higher for simple bottles | Slower but more accurate |
| Neck finish | Trimming required | No trimming (moulded precisely) |
| Typical products | HDPE detergent bottles, tanks | PET bottles, pharma containers |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Describe the injection moulding process with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the **screw**, **mould temperature controller**, and **clamping unit** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polystyrene part of mass 60 g is injection moulded. Given: $c_p = 1.3$ kJ/kg·K, latent heat = 0 (amorphous), $T_m = 220 °C$, $T_{mould} = 50 °C$, and wall thickness = 4 mm with $\alpha = 8 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s. Calculate: (i) heat to be removed per part, and (ii) approximate cooling time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Injection Moulding Process (3 marks):**
1. **Feeding** — Granules from hopper fall into the feed throat.
2. **Plasticization** — Screw rotates, conveying polymer; heaters melt it.
3. **Injection** — Screw acts as plunger, forcing melt into cavity at 70–140 MPa.
4. **Packing** — Additional melt compensates for shrinkage.
5. **Cooling** — Water channels extract heat; part solidifies below $T_g$.
6. **Ejection** — Mould opens; ejector pins push part out.

**Role of the Screw (1½ marks):**
- Performs **three functions**: conveying, melting, and pumping.
- During metering, it builds pressure at the screw tip.
- During injection, it translates axially (like a plunger) without rotation.
- Compression ratio (typically 2:1 to 4:1) ensures proper melt compaction.

**Role of Mould Temperature Controller (1½ marks):**
- Maintains mould temperature at **30–80 °C** (typically below $T_g$).
- Prevents premature skin formation; controls surface finish.
- Uses water/oil circulation through drilled channels.
- Improves cycle consistency and reduces warpage.

**Role of Clamping Unit (1 mark):**
- Holds the two mould halves closed against injection pressure.
- Prevents mould opening ("flashing").
- Clamp force $F = P_{inj} \times A_{projected}$.
- Two types: **Toggle** (fast, mechanical) and **Hydraulic** (smoother, controllable).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Heat to be removed:**

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.060 \times 1.3 \times (220 - 50)$$

Step-by-step:
$$\Delta T = 170 \text{ K}$$
$$0.060 \times 1.3 = 0.078$$
$$0.078 \times 170 = 13.26 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 13.26 \text{ kJ per part}}$$

**(ii) Cooling time:**

$$t_c \approx \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{(0.004)^2}{\pi^2 \times 8 \times 10^{-8}}$$

Step-by-step:
$$(0.004)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\pi^2 \approx 9.87$$
$$\pi^2 \times 8 \times 10^{-8} = 7.896 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$t_c = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{7.896 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 20.26 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 20.3 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **extrusion process** for manufacturing a PVC pipe. Explain the functions of the **three zones** of the screw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **single-screw extruder output equation** and explain the physical significance of the drag and pressure flow terms. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Extrusion Process for PVC Pipe (3 marks):**
1. PVC compound (resin + stabilizer + lubricant) is fed into the hopper.
2. Screw conveys material through three heated zones.
3. Melt passes through breaker plate (removes contaminants, builds back-pressure).
4. Melt enters the **annular die** to form pipe geometry.
5. Pipe is calibrated in a sizing sleeve under vacuum.
6. Pipe is cooled in a water spray tank.
7. Haul-off (caterpillar) pulls pipe at controlled speed.
8. Saw cuts pipe to required lengths.

**Functions of Screw Zones (4 marks):**

| Zone | Function | Operating Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed Zone** (50% of length) | Conveys solid pellets forward | Channel depth deep (e.g., 8 mm); low shear; barrel cool |
| **Compression/Melting Zone** (25–30%) | Melts polymer via conduction + viscous shear | Channel depth tapers; high mechanical + thermal input |
| **Metering Zone** (20–25%) | Pumps melt uniformly to die | Channel depth shallow (e.g., 2.5 mm); generates pressure |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation (5 marks):**

(Step 1) Unwrap the screw channel into a flat rectangular channel: width $W = \pi D \sin\phi$, depth $H$, length $L$.

(Step 2) Upper wall (barrel) moves with velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ relative to lower wall (screw root).

(Step 3) For Newtonian fluid, equation of motion:
$$\frac{dP}{dz} = \eta \frac{d^2 v_z}{dx^2}$$

(Step 4) Integrate twice with $v_z(0) = 0$ and $v_z(H) = V_b$:

$$v_z(x) = V_b \frac{x}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}(Hx - x^2)$$

(Step 5) Integrate over depth to get flow rate per unit width:

$$q = \frac{V_b H}{2} - \frac{H^3}{12\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

(Step 6) Multiply by width and substitute:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12\eta L}}$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**
- **Drag flow** (first term): Forward flow caused by relative motion of barrel. Proportional to $N$ and $H$. Independent of viscosity.
- **Pressure flow** (second term): Backward flow caused by die resistance. Proportional to $\Delta P$ and inversely proportional to $\eta$. Acts like leak-off.
- **Net flow** = Drag − Pressure. Maximum throughput occurs when $\Delta P = 0$ (open discharge).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_g$ with $T_m$** in thermoforming — semi-crystalline polymers form at $T_m - 5°$ to $20°$, NOT above $T_g$.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — especially pressure in MPa vs Pa ($1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$).
> 3. **Mixing up compression vs injection moulding** — compression is for thermosets (long cure), injection is for thermoplastics (fast cycle).
> 4. **Writing "Calendaring" instead of "Calendering"** — KTU answer scripts often lose ½ mark for spelling.
> 5. **Omitting the helix angle $\phi$** in extrusion output equation — answer is incomplete without it.
> 6. **Confusing draw ratio in thermoforming with blow ratio in blow moulding** — both involve deformation but operate on different principles.
> 7. **In derivations, skipping "no-slip boundary condition"** — Examiners expect explicit mention of $v_z(0)=0$ and $v_z(H)=V_b$.
> 8. **Missing the importance of cooling time** — Cooling alone consumes **50–80% of the cycle** in injection moulding; students often skip this.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Plastic Processing Technology**

### 🔹 Injection Moulding
- **Cycle time** = Fill + Hold + Cool + Open/Close. **Cooling = 50–80%** of cycle.
- **Screw acts as plunger** during injection; rotates during plasticization.
- **Shrinkage** typical 0.3–3% — compensated in mould design.
- Clamping force $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{projected}$.
- Best for **thermoplastics**; high production volumes; complex 3D parts.

### 🔹 Compression Moulding
- Used for **thermosets** (phenolic, melamine, epoxy, SMC, BMC).
- Process: charge → close → heat + cure → open → eject.
- **Cure time** is rate-limiting; long cycle.
- Products: switch boards, helmets, brake pads, electrical housings.

### 🔹 Calendaring
- **Continuous** process; produces **thin sheets/films** (mostly PVC).
- Uses 3 or 4 counter-rotating heated rolls; **nip gap** controls thickness.
- Sheet thickness $h = 2R(1 - \cos\theta)$.
- Throughput $Q = \pi D N h W$.
- Friction ratio (1.1–1.5) smoothens surface.

### 🔹 Blow Moulding
- Produces **hollow parts** — bottles, tanks, drums.
- Three types: **EBM, IBM, SBM**.
- Blow ratio $B = D_{bottle}/D_{parison}$ (typically 2–4).
- For PET: **Stretch ratio** improves barrier properties.

### 🔹 Extrusion
- **Continuous** process for profiles, pipes, sheets, films.
- Screw zones: **Feed → Compression → Metering**.
- Output equation: $Q = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$ (Newtonian).
- L/D ratio: 20–40; Compression ratio: 2:1 to 4:1.
- Die swell occurs due to elastic recovery; $B_s = D_{out}/D_{die}$.
- Specific energy: **0.15–0.30 kWh/kg**.

### 🔹 Thermoforming
- **Secondary** process on pre-made sheets.
- Heating to $T_g + 30$ to $60 °C$ (amorphous) or $T_m - 5$ to $20 °C$ (semi-crystalline).
- Four variants: **Vacuum, Pressure, Mechanical, Drape**.
- Material distribution depends on **plug-assist** design.
- Vacuum ≈ **0.09 MPa** (just below atmospheric).

### 🔹 Universal Cross-Cutting Concepts
- **Temperature, Pressure, Time** govern every polymer process.
- **Crystalline polymers** have narrow processing windows; amorphous polymers have wider ones.
- **Cycle time optimization** focuses on cooling (IM) or cure (CM).
- **Regrind** is universally recyclable in thermoplastics; thermosets cannot be re-melted.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wet, Dry, and Melt Spinning Methods for Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wet, Dry, and Melt Spinning Methods for Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fibre Spinning (Man-Made Fibre Formation):**
> Fibre spinning is the foundational **unit operation** in polymer processing by which a viscous polymeric fluid (melt or solution) is continuously extruded through a spinneret (a die containing one or more fine orifices), followed by a controlled **solidification/precipitation step**, and finally wound onto a take-up roll to form a continuous filament. The three principal commercial routes, classified by the physical state of the polymer during extrusion and the mechanism of solidification, are **Melt Spinning, Dry Spinning, and Wet Spinning.**

| Spinning Route | State of Polymer | Solidification Mechanism | Typical Polymers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Melt Spinning** | Polymer melt (no solvent) | Cooling below T_m or T_g | PET, Nylon-6,6, PP |
| **Dry Spinning** | Polymer solution in volatile solvent | Solvent evaporation | Cellulose acetate, PAN, PVDC |
| **Wet Spinning** | Polymer solution | Chemical coagulation in non-solvent bath | Viscose rayon, Acrylic, Aramid |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine squeezing toothpaste from a tube.** The paste (viscous polymer) emerges as a continuous cylinder, gets thinner as you pull your hand away, and finally hardens into a strand you can hold. That is precisely what a spinning line does — except the "tube" has **thousands of microscopic holes (spinneret)** and the "hardening" step differs by the spinning method:
> - **Melt** = the toothpaste cools and sets (like wax cooling in air).
> - **Dry** = the water in the paste evaporates into a hot chamber, leaving only the solid behind.
> - **Wet** = the paste is squeezed directly into a chemical bath that "freezes" it from the outside in (like squeezing gel into cold water).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Operating Parameters

- **Throughput (Q):** Mass flow rate per hole, typically **0.5 – 50 g/min/hole**.
- **Spinneret Orifice Diameter:** **0.05 – 0.5 mm** for textile fibres.
- **Take-Up Velocity (V_L):** **100 – 6000 m/min** (melt); **100 – 500 m/min** (dry); **10 – 200 m/min** (wet).
- **Spin-Draw Ratio (DR):** Ratio of take-up velocity to extrusion velocity, typically **50 – 4000** for PET.
- **Filament Fineness (Denier/Tex):** **1 denier** = mass in grams of 9000 m of fibre.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Melt Spinning — Operational Principle

Melt spinning is the **simplest, fastest, and cleanest** of the three processes because it uses **no solvent**. Thermoplastic polymer chips (e.g., PET pellets) are melted in an extruder/screw melter at temperatures **20 – 80 °C above T_m**, filtered through a sand-pack, and metered by a **gear pump** to the spinneret. The molten filaments fall through a **quench chimney** where cool air (20 – 30 °C) cross-flows the filaments, extracting heat and solidifying them below T_g.

**Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic:**
- **Why heat?** Polymer chains must acquire sufficient mobility (reduce viscosity below ~10³ Pa·s) to flow through micron-sized orifices.
- **Why cool air?** Heat must be removed faster than the filament moves; convective cooling is rate-controlled by Nusselt number Nu ≈ 0.5 Re^0.33.
- **Why drawdown?** Stretching aligns polymer chains along the fibre axis, dramatically boosting **tensile strength** (factor of 3 – 10×) and reducing diameter.

### 2.2 Dry Spinning — Operational Principle

In dry spinning, the polymer is dissolved in a **volatile organic solvent** (e.g., acetone for cellulose acetate; DMF for PAN). The dope (15 – 30 wt% polymer) is extruded through the spinneret into a **jacketed cylindrical column** where hot inert gas (N₂ or air, 100 – 250 °C) flows **co-currently** or **counter-currently**. Solidification occurs by **solvent evaporation** — not by cooling.

**Why and How:**
- **Why volatile solvent?** It must leave the filament rapidly at moderate temperatures (without degrading the polymer).
- **Why co-current flow?** Reduces filament skin-crust formation; counter-current gives denser skin but slower throughput.
- **Key trade-off:** Solvent recovery economics — the evaporated solvent is condensed and recycled; ~**3 – 6 kg solvent per kg fibre** must be recovered.

### 2.3 Wet Spinning — Operational Principle

Wet spinning extrudes the polymer solution (the "dope") **directly into a liquid coagulation bath** (typically water + solvent + salts). The bath is a **non-solvent** for the polymer but **miscible with the dope solvent**. Solidification occurs by **counter-diffusion**: solvent leaves the filament while non-solvent enters, causing phase inversion and precipitation.

**Why and How:**
- **Why a liquid bath?** Many polymers (cellulose, alginates, polyacrylonitrile for carbon fibre precursor) **decompose before melting** and cannot be dry-spun because of poor solvent volatility.
- **Why counter-diffusion?** Skin formation (dense outer layer) and core hollows can be engineered by adjusting bath composition, temperature (10 – 50 °C), and residence time.
- **Drawback:** Very slow (10 – 200 m/min) due to diffusion-limited solidification; multi-stage bath configurations are common.

### 2.4 The Three Spinning Lineups — Schematic Comparison

| Feature | Melt | Dry | Wet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymer State** | Molten | Solution | Solution |
| **Solidification** | Cooling | Evaporation | Coagulation |
| **Spinneret Holes** | 1 – 50,000 | 10 – 4,000 | 10 – 200,000 |
| **V_take-up (m/min)** | 100 – 6000 | 100 – 500 | 10 – 200 |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | High (solvent recovery) | High (bath chemistry) |
| **Environmental** | Best | VOC emissions | Heavy water/salt load |
| **Production Rate** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \rho \, A \, V = \rho \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} V$ | Mass throughput per filament | kg/s |
| 2 | $Q_0 = Q_L \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \rho_0 A_0 V_0 = \rho_L A_L V_L$ | **Conservation of mass** along spinline | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dfrac{D_0}{D_L} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L \, V_L}{\rho_0 \, V_0}} = \sqrt{\text{DR}}$ for $\rho_0 = \rho_L$ | Filament diameter relation | — |
| 4 | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_0}$ | **Draw-down (spin-draw) ratio** | — |
| 5 | $\text{Tex} = \dfrac{m\,[\text{g}]}{L\,[\text{km}]}$ | Linear density of yarn | g/km |
| 6 | $\text{Denier} = 9 \times \text{Tex}$ | Tex–Denier conversion | — |
| 7 | $F_{\text{rheo}} = \eta_{\text{app}} \dfrac{dV}{dz} \cdot A$ | Viscous (rheological) tensile force | N |
| 8 | $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{rheo}} + F_{\text{grav}} + F_{\text{inertia}} - F_{\text{surf}}$ | Spinline force balance | N |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{\text{solvent}}^{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{dope}} \, w_s}{1 - w_s}$ | Solvent evaporation rate (dry spinning) | kg/s |
| 10 | $\text{Re}_{\text{filament}} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\eta}$ | Reynolds number of a single filament | — |
| 11 | $t_{\text{residence}}^{\text{wet}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{bath}}}{V_{\text{filament}}}$ | Coagulation time in wet bath | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam rule (KTU 2024):** In a melt spinline, the polymer is essentially **incompressible**, so $\rho_0 = \rho_L$. Therefore $A_0 V_0 = A_L V_L$, which simplifies the diameter formula to $D_L = D_0 / \sqrt{\text{DR}}$. For dry and wet spinning, $\rho$ changes due to solvent loss/gain — always apply the full form of Equation (2).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Textile Industry:** ~**70 million tonnes** of polyester filament per year (2024 figures) is produced almost entirely by **melt spinning** — the workhorse of the global apparel industry.
- **Carbon Fibre Precursor:** PAN wet spinning at very low speeds (5 – 30 m/min) yields the precursor for >**90 %** of all commercial carbon fibres.
- **Medical Applications:** Biodegradable wet-spun scaffolds (PCL, PLA) mimic the **extracellular matrix** for tissue engineering.
- **Filtration & Geotextiles:** Dry-spun ultrafine fibres (< 1 µm) form high-surface-area nonwovens for HEPA filters.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Filament Diameter as a Function of Drawdown Ratio (Melt Spinning)

We begin from the **conservation of mass** for an incompressible Newtonian-like filament under steady-state isothermal melt spinning.

**Step 1 — Write the continuity equation** for a single filament emerging from a circular spinneret hole:

$$
Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot V = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the area of a circular cross-section** $A = \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$
Q = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot V
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constant-throughput condition** between the spinneret exit (subscript 0) and the take-up point (subscript L):

$$
\rho_0 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4} \cdot V_0 = \rho_L \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_L^2}{4} \cdot V_L
$$

The factor $\pi/4$ cancels from both sides, giving:

$$
\rho_0 \, D_0^2 \, V_0 = \rho_L \, D_L^2 \, V_L
$$

**Step 4 — For melt spinning, the polymer melt is incompressible**, so $\rho_0 = \rho_L = \rho$. Density drops out:

$$
D_0^2 \, V_0 = D_L^2 \, V_L
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange** to isolate the take-up diameter $D_L$:

$$
D_L^2 = D_0^2 \cdot \dfrac{V_0}{V_L}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the draw-down ratio** $\text{DR} = V_L / V_0$ and substitute:

$$
D_L^2 = \dfrac{D_0^2}{\text{DR}}
$$

**Step 7 — Take the square root** of both sides to obtain the final diameter relation:

$$
\boxed{\,D_L = \dfrac{D_0}{\sqrt{\text{DR}}}\,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Increasing the take-up velocity by a factor of 100 reduces the filament diameter by a factor of 10. This is the **fundamental geometric scaling law of melt spinning** and is a frequently asked KTU problem.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Take-Up Velocity Required for a Given Throughput

**Step 1 — Start from the mass throughput equation** evaluated at the take-up roll:

$$
Q = \rho_L \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_L^2}{4} \cdot V_L
$$

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for $V_L$:**

$$
V_L = \dfrac{4 \, Q}{\pi \, \rho_L \, D_L^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the desired linear density** in tex (g/km). Recall $\text{Tex} = m/L = Q/V_L$ when $Q$ is in g/min and $V_L$ in m/min scaled by 1000. The convenient form is:

$$
\boxed{\,V_L = \dfrac{4 \, Q}{\pi \, \rho_L \, D_L^2} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_L \cdot A_L} = \dfrac{\rho \, \text{Tex} \cdot 10^{-3}}{\rho_L \cdot A_L}\,}
$$

(more directly, $V_L$ in m/min = $Q$ [g/min] / (mass per unit length [g/m]).

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:** For PET ($\rho_L = 1360$ kg/m³) drawn to $D_L = 20 \;\mu\text{m}$ at $Q = 2 \;\text{g/min}$ per hole:

$$
A_L = \dfrac{\pi (20 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
V_L = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3} / 60}{1360 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-10}} \approx \dfrac{3.33 \times 10^{-5}}{4.27 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 78\;\text{m/s} \approx 4700\;\text{m/min}
$$

This is consistent with industrial high-speed PET melt spinning (3,000 – 5,000 m/min).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spinline Force Balance (Simplified 1-D)

Consider an elemental slice of filament of length $dz$ on the spinline. The four forces acting on it are:

- $F_{\text{rheo}}(z)$ — viscous tensile force from extensional flow
- $F_{\text{grav}}(z) = \rho g A(z)\,dz$ — gravity (downward)
- $F_{\text{inertia}}(z) = \rho A V \dfrac{dV}{dz}\,dz$ — momentum change
- $F_{\text{surf}}(z) = \pi D(z)\,\gamma_{\text{LV}}$ — surface tension (negligible for $D > 100\;\mu\text{m}$)

**Step 1 — Steady-state force balance** on the element:

$$
F(z+dz) - F(z) = \rho A V \dfrac{dV}{dz}\,dz + \rho g A\,dz
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $dz$** and let $dz \to 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dF}{dz} = \rho A V \dfrac{dV}{dz} + \rho g A
$$

**Step 3 — Replace $A$ using continuity** $A = Q/(\rho V)$:

$$
\dfrac{dF}{dz} = \dfrac{Q}{V}\dfrac{dV}{dz} + \dfrac{\rho g Q}{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $V$:**

$$
V\dfrac{dF}{dz} = Q\dfrac{dV}{dz} + \rho g Q
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate** along the spinline from spinneret ($z=0$) to take-up ($z=L$):

$$
F_L - F_0 = Q \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_L}{V_0}\right) + \rho g \, Q \, t_{\text{residence}}
$$

This is the **momentum equation of melt spinning** (note: gravity term vanishes if the spinline is horizontal — as in horizontal dry/wet spinning setups).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Solvent Evaporation Rate in Dry Spinning

**Step 1 — Dope composition:** Let $w_p$ and $w_s = 1 - w_p$ be the polymer and solvent mass fractions in the dope.

**Step 2 — Mass of solvent to be removed per kg of fibre:**

$$
m_{\text{solvent}} = \dfrac{w_s}{w_p}\;\;\text{(kg solvent per kg polymer fibre)}
$$

**Step 3 — For an industrial unit producing $P$ kg/day of dry fibre:**

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{M}_{\text{solvent}} = \dfrac{P \, w_s}{w_p} = \dfrac{P\,(1-w_p)}{w_p}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:** Cellulose acetate dry spinning uses a dope with $w_p = 0.25$. For $P = 10{,}000$ kg/day fibre:

$$
\dot{M}_{\text{solvent}} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 0.75}{0.25} = 30{,}000\;\text{kg solvent/day}
$$

Hence the **solvent recovery system must handle ~3 kg acetone per kg fibre** — the dominant capital/operating cost driver.

### 3.5 Analytic Comparison Matrix — Strengths & Weaknesses

| Criterion | Melt Spinning | Dry Spinning | Wet Spinning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| **Capital Cost** | ★★★★ (low) | ★★ (high) | ★ (very high) |
| **Solvent Issue** | None | VOC; fire/explosion risk | Wastewater; salt disposal |
| **Polymer Versatility** | Thermoplastic only | Solvent-soluble, heat-sensitive | Non-meltable polymers |
| **Filament Quality (uniformity)** | Excellent | Good (skin-core) | Variable (skin-core) |
| **As-spun Strength** | High (oriented) | Moderate | Low (needs hot draw) |
| **Sustainability Index** | High | Low | Lowest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Spinning Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start Polymer Selection] --> P1{Is the polymer a thermoplastic with T melt below decomposition?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Is throughput the priority and polymer melt stable enough?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Use Melt Spinning]
    P2 -- No --> P4[Consider Dry Spinning if solvent exists]
    P1 -- No --> P5{Can the polymer dissolve in a volatile organic solvent?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6{Is solvent recovery economically feasible?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Use Dry Spinning]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Use Wet Spinning]
    P5 -- No --> P9[Use Wet Spinning with Non Solvent Bath]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`
> **Differentiate between melt spinning and dry spinning of fibres. Give one example of a polymer processed by each method.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Melt Spinning** involves extrusion of a polymer melt (no solvent) through a spinneret, followed by solidification via **cooling below T_g/T_m** in a quench chimney using cold air. Example: **Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)** for polyester fibre.
- **Dry Spinning** involves extrusion of a polymer solution in a **volatile solvent** into a heated chamber, where the filament solidifies by **solvent evaporation**. Example: **Cellulose acetate** in acetone, or **polyacrylonitrile (PAN)** in DMF.
- Key contrast: Melt spinning uses **no solvent** (cleanest process); dry spinning requires **solvent recovery** (acetone/DMF condensers).
- Solidification mechanism: **cooling vs. evaporation**.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`
> **Why is wet spinning preferred for polyacrylonitrile (PAN) precursor used in carbon fibre manufacture?**

**Model Answer:**
- PAN **decomposes before melting** (T_m ≈ 320 °C but degradation starts at ~250 °C), so melt spinning is impossible.
- PAN is soluble in DMF/DMSO, enabling dope preparation; however, DMF has **low enough volatility** that dry spinning is uneconomical at industrial scale.
- Wet spinning in a DMF/water coagulation bath allows **slow, controlled skin formation**, producing a dense precursor with the **smooth circular cross-section** required for high-performance carbon fibre.
- The slow speed (10 – 50 m/min) of wet spinning is acceptable because carbon fibre is a **high-value, low-volume** product.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q7(a,b)]`
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **melt spinning process** for the manufacture of polyester (PET) filament yarn. Discuss the role of the **quench chimney** and the **spin finish applicator**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the relation between filament diameter and the **spin-draw ratio** for melt spinning of an incompressible polymer. A PET melt is extruded through a spinneret of hole diameter **0.25 mm** and drawn down at a take-up velocity of **4000 m/min**. If the extrusion velocity at the spinneret is **1.2 m/min**, calculate the final filament diameter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Process flow (2 marks):** PET chips → hopper → screw extruder (280 – 295 °C, T_m of PET = 260 °C) → filter (sand pack) → gear pump → **spinneret** (orifice diameter 0.15 – 0.50 mm; 10 – 50,000 holes depending on yarn count) → molten filaments emerge vertically downward into the **quench chimney**.
2. **Quench chimney (2 marks):** Cool air (20 – 25 °C) is blown **cross-flow** or **radial** across the filament bundle. Heat is removed convectively; the filament surface solidifies first (skin formation), then the core cools. The chill air velocity is typically **0.3 – 1.0 m/s**.
3. **Spin finish applicator (1.5 marks):** A thin oil-based finish (lubricant + antistatic agent) is applied via a kiss roll or metered slot just before the take-up. It prevents **filament-to-filament adhesion**, reduces **friction on ceramic guides**, dissipates **static charge**, and improves **cohesion** of the yarn bundle.
4. **Take-up and draw (1.5 marks):** Take-up roll (V_L = 1000 – 6000 m/min); between godet rolls, the yarn is **hot-drawn** at 100 – 180 °C to achieve orientation and crystallinity. Final product is wound on a bobbin.

#### Model Answer to (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 marks):**

From continuity $Q = \rho A V = $ const, with $\rho_0 = \rho_L$ (incompressible melt):
$$
\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 V_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_L^2 V_L
$$

Defining draw-down ratio $\text{DR} = V_L / V_0$:
$$
\boxed{\,D_L = \dfrac{D_0}{\sqrt{\text{DR}}}\,}
$$

**Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**
$$
\text{DR} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_0} = \dfrac{4000}{1.2} = 3333.33
$$

$$
D_L = \dfrac{0.25\;\text{mm}}{\sqrt{3333.33}} = \dfrac{0.25}{57.74} = 4.33 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mm} = 4.33\;\mu\text{m}
$$

This is the order of magnitude of an ultra-fine PET micro-fibre.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q8(a,b)]`
>
> **(a)** Compare **dry spinning and wet spinning** with respect to (i) polymer state, (ii) solidification mechanism, (iii) typical spinning speeds, (iv) examples of polymers, and (v) major engineering challenge in each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cellulose acetate dry-spinning dope contains **25 wt%** polymer in acetone. If the plant produces **15,000 kg/day** of dry acetate fibre, calculate the **mass of acetone to be recovered per day**. Explain briefly the typical acetone recovery system used in industry. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a) — 7 Marks

| Criterion | Dry Spinning | Wet Spinning |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Polymer state (1 mark) | Polymer solution in volatile solvent | Polymer solution in non-volatile solvent |
| (ii) Solidification (1.5 marks) | Solvent evaporation in hot gas column | Counter-diffusion of solvent into coagulation bath |
| (iii) Speed (1.5 marks) | 100 – 500 m/min | 10 – 200 m/min (diffusion-limited) |
| (iv) Examples (1.5 marks) | Cellulose acetate (acetone), PAN (DMF) | Viscose rayon (H₂SO₄/Na₂SO₄ bath), PAN (DMF/H₂O), aramid (PPm) |
| (v) Major challenge (1.5 marks) | Solvent recovery (VOC capture, fire hazard) | Wastewater treatment & salt disposal; skin-core uniformity |

#### Model Answer to (b) — 7 Marks

**Calculation (4 marks):**
Mass of polymer in 15,000 kg fibre = 15,000 kg (polymer is preserved).
Mass of acetone originally in dope per kg polymer:

$$
\dfrac{w_s}{w_p} = \dfrac{1 - 0.25}{0.25} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.25} = 3 \;\text{kg acetone / kg polymer}
$$

Therefore acetone to be recovered per day:
$$
\boxed{\,\dot{M}_{\text{acetone}} = 15{,}000 \times 3 = 45{,}000\;\text{kg/day}\,}
$$

**Recovery system (3 marks):** Industry uses a **condensation train** — the vapour-laden gas leaving the spinning column passes through shell-and-tube condensers using chilled brine; condensed acetone is decanted, distilled to remove water, and returned to the dope make-up tank. Activated-carbon **adsorption beds** act as a polishing stage to capture residual vapours (< 100 ppm emission).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong continuity equation:** Students often write $D_L = D_0 \times \text{DR}$ (linear) instead of $D_L = D_0 / \sqrt{\text{DR}}$. Remember: diameter scales with **square root** of the drawdown because area scales linearly with $V$.
> 2. **Forgetting incompressibility:** In dry/wet spinning, density changes due to solvent — cannot cancel $\rho$ in the continuity equation; for melt spinning it cancels.
> 3. **Confusing "Tex" and "Denier":** $1 \;\text{denier} = 9\;\text{tex}$; converting incorrectly loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping units:** Numerical problems must carry units throughout; a bare number without m/min or µm gets **zero credit** for the final answer.
> 5. **Missing the role of spin finish:** Examiners specifically test the quench + finish combination; omitting one forfeits ~2 marks.
> 6. **Mislabelling Mermaid nodes:** Always use alphanumeric IDs (e.g., `nodeA`) — never `end`, `style`, `subgraph`.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Three spinning routes** are melt, dry, and wet — classified by **polymer state** and **solidification mechanism**.
- **Melt spinning** is the **fastest and cleanest** (no solvent) — used for **PET, Nylon, PP** at up to **6000 m/min**.
- **Dry spinning** uses a **volatile solvent** that evaporates in a hot gas column — used for **cellulose acetate** and **PAN**; **solvent recovery** is the chief cost.
- **Wet spinning** uses a **liquid coagulation bath** — used for **viscose rayon, aramid, PAN carbon-fibre precursor**; **slowest** (10 – 200 m/min) but works for **non-meltable** polymers.
- **Mass conservation** along the spinline: $Q = \rho A V = $ constant.
- **Diameter relation** for melt spinning: $D_L = D_0 / \sqrt{\text{DR}}$.
- **Draw-down ratio** $\text{DR} = V_L / V_0$; typical industrial values 50 – 4000.
- **Tex = g/km**, **Denier = 9 × Tex**; **1 denier** = mass of 9000 m of fibre.
- **Spin finish** = lubricant + antistatic + cohesion agent — applied before take-up.
- **Quench chimney** cools molten filaments by cross-flow air (20 – 30 °C).
- **Coagulation bath** uses **counter-diffusion** of solvent out and non-solvent in to precipitate the polymer.
- **Force balance on spinline** = rheological + gravitational + inertial − surface tension forces.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** thermoplastics → melt; soluble + volatile → dry; non-meltable or non-volatile-solvent → wet.
- **Master formula set** is given in **§ 2.5** (Cheat Sheet) — memorize and apply.
- **Engineering impact:** > **70 million tonnes/year** of polyester filament produced globally — predominantly by melt spinning.
- **Highest tensile strength** arises from post-spin **drawing**, which aligns polymer chains along the fibre axis.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulcanization of Rubber: General Study of Elastomer Processing and Compounding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT612 - POLYMER SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Vulcanization of Rubber: General Study of Elastomer Processing and Compounding Methods # MODULE 4: VULCANIZATION OF RUBBER & ELASTOMER PROCESSING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Vulcanization — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vulcanization** is the **thermo-chemical cross-linking process** by which elastomeric polymer chains (predominantly natural and synthetic rubbers) are joined through covalent **sulfur bridges** (or alternative cross-linking agents) to form a three-dimensional network, thereby converting a soft, sticky, plastic raw elastomer into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable, and mechanically durable rubber product. The process was **discovered in 1839 by Charles Goodyear** by accident when he dropped a rubber-sulfur mixture on a hot stove. The term *vulcanization* was coined by **William Brockedon** (after *Vulcan*, the Roman god of fire). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module focuses on (a) the chemistry and mechanism of sulfur and non-sulfur vulcanization, (b) classification of vulcanizing systems, (c) the role of accelerators/activators, and (d) upstream **elastomer processing** — specifically mastication, mixing, and the **compounding** of rubber formulations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Woolen Sweater That Remembers Its Shape" Imagine a plate of cooked **spaghetti** (unvulcanized rubber) — when you lift it, the noodles slide apart and the plate is a mess. Now imagine dipping those noodles briefly into a **sugar syrup** that hardens at the joints (sulfur cross-links). When you lift the plate now, the noodles are tethered at random spots but can still flex individually — the whole mass bends and **snaps back**. That is vulcanization. - **Unvulcanized rubber** = spaghetti pile → sticky, plastic flow, no memory. - **Vulcanized rubber** = cross-linked spaghetti → elastic, springy, retains shape. ### 1.3 Why Vulcanization is Indispensable — Key Property Shifts | Property | Before Vulcanization | After Vulcanization | |---|---|---| | **Tensile Strength** | ~**0.2–0.5 MPa** | ~**15–30 MPa** | | **Elongation at Break** | ~**1000 %** (plastic flow) | ~**400–800 %** (elastic recovery) | | **Elastic Recovery** | Poor (permanent set) | Excellent (>90%) | | **Swelling in Solvents** | Dissolves | Only swells | | **Thermal Stability** | Becomes tacky on heating | Stable up to ~**200 °C** | | **Glass Transition (Tg)** | Unchanged | Largely retained, network adds restraint | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Definition to Memorize:** > *"Vulcanization is the irreversible chemical process that introduces three-dimensional cross-links between polymer chains, transforming a thermoplastic elastomer precursor into a thermoset elastomeric network."* --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chemistry of Sulfur Vulcanization ### 2.1.1 Reaction Overview (Free-Radical / Ionic Pathway) Sulfur (typically **S₈ rings**) is thermally cleaved at vulcanization temperatures (140–180 °C) to generate sulfur radicals or ionic species that attack the **α-methylene carbon** (the allylic position) of the **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** (natural rubber) backbone. > **General Reaction Scheme:** $$ \text{---CH}_2\text{---C(CH}_3\text{)=CH---CH}_2\text{---} \;+\; \text{S}_8 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\,\text{Activator}} \; \text{---CH}_2\text{---C(CH}_3\text{)=CH---CH}_2\text{---} $$ $$ \big\downarrow $$ $$ \text{Cross-linked 3D network with C–S}_x\text{–C bridges} \quad (x = 1, 2, 3, \dots) $$ ### 2.1.2 Types of Sulfur Cross-Links Formed | Cross-link Type | Notation | Bond Energy | Occurrence | |---|---|---|---| | **Monosulfidic** | **C–S–C** | High | Efficient (EV) systems | | **Disulfidic** | **C–S–S–C** | Medium | Conventional systems | | **Polysulfidic** | **C–S$_x$–C** (x ≥ 3) | Low | Over-cured / soft rubber | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polysulfidic links** (e.g., **C–S₈–C**) are flexible and improve **fatigue resistance** but are thermally less stable. **Monosulfidic links** (C–S–C) give better **heat-ageing** and **compression set**. ### 2.2 Classification of Vulcanization Systems **A. Conventional Vulcanization (CV) System:** - Sulfur: **2.5–3.5 phr**; Accelerator: **0.5–1.0 phr**; ZnO: **3–5 phr**; Stearic acid: **1–2 phr**. - Produces predominantly **polysulfidic cross-links**. **B. Efficient Vulcanization (EV) System:** - Sulfur: **0.2–0.5 phr**; High accelerator loading (**2–5 phr**); often a sulfur-donor like **TMTD (tetramethylthiuram disulfide)**. - Produces predominantly **mono- and di-sulfidic cross-links** → superior heat resistance. **C. Semi-Efficient Vulcanization (SEV):** - Intermediate between CV and EV; sulfur ~**1.0–2.0 phr**. ### 2.3 Non-Sulfur Vulcanization Systems | System | Cross-link Formed | Used For | |---|---|---| | **Peroxide** | **C–C** bonds | EPDM, silicone, HNBR; heat-resistant goods | | **Metal Oxide** (ZnO + MgO) | Ether/ester bridges | Polychloroprene (Neoprene) | | **Phenolic Resin** | C–C + C–O–C | Butyl (IIR), EPDM; high-temperature service | | **Radiation** | C–C | Latex films, medical devices | | **Urethane** | Urea / urethane links | Millable polyurethane rubbers | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Relations > [!NOTE] > **Units used:** **phr** = parts per hundred parts of rubber (by mass). Cross-link density denoted **ν** (mol/cm³ or mol/m³). | # | Quantity / Equation | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cross-link density (Flory)** | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{2 \, M_c}$ | $\rho$ = polymer density (g/cm³), $M_c$ = mol. wt. between cross-links | | 2 | **Swelling ratio** | $Q = \dfrac{V_{\text{swollen}}}{V_{\text{dry}}} = 1 + \dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}\!\left(\dfrac{M_c}{V_1 \rho_2}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{2}}$ | Used with Flory–Rehner equation | | 3 | **Flory–Rehner (equilibrium swelling)** | $\nu = \dfrac{-\!\left[\ln(1-\phi_2)+\phi_2+\chi\phi_2^{2}\right]}{V_1\!\left(\phi_2^{1/3}-\tfrac{\phi_2}{2}\right)}$ | $\phi_2$ = polymer volume fraction in gel; $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins parameter | | 4 | **Mooney-Rivlin elasticity constant** | $\sigma = 2\!\left(C_1 + \dfrac{C_2}{\alpha}\right)\!(\alpha - \alpha^{-2})$ | $C_1, C_2$ relate to network perfection | | 5 | **Vulcanization (cure) time** | $t_{90} \propto \dfrac{1}{[\text{Accelerator}]^{n}}$ | Empirical; $n \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ | | 6 | **Activation energy (Arrhenius for cure)** | $k = A \, e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ ≈ **80–120 kJ/mol** for sulfur cure | | 7 | **Optimum cure** | Time to reach **90 %** of maximum torque on rheometer | $t_{90}$ is the design cure time | | 8 | **Scorch time** | Time to reach **5 % or 10 %** of max torque | Safety margin before onset of cure | | 9 | **Reversion index** | $\dfrac{\text{Max torque} - \text{Torque at } t_{90}+20\,\text{min}}{\text{Max torque}} \times 100\,\%$ | Quantifies over-cure softening | > [!WARNING] > When writing the Flory–Rehner equation, remember $\phi_2$ is the **volume fraction of polymer in the swollen gel**, NOT the weight fraction. Most mark losses in KTU valuation are due to this swap. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Vulcanization choice directly determines service life: - **Tyre treads** (CV system, polysulfidic) → fatigue, dynamic flex. - **Engine mounts / radiator hoses** (EV/SEV) → heat aging. - **Bridge bearings** (natural rubber EV) → long-term compression set. - **O-rings in aerospace** (peroxide cure) → extreme temperature resilience. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Flory–Rehner Equation (the cornerstone for cross-link density) ### 3.1.1 Starting Thermodynamic Principle The swelling equilibrium of a cross-linked polymer in a solvent balances **two opposing free-energy contributions**: 1. **Mixing free energy** ($\Delta G_{\text{mix}}$) — drives solvent ingress. 2. **Elastic free energy** ($\Delta G_{\text{elast}}$) — resists swelling due to network constraints. At equilibrium, the **chemical potential of the solvent inside the gel equals that of pure solvent outside**: $$ \Delta \mu_1^{(\text{mix})} + \Delta \mu_1^{(\text{elast})} = 0 $$ ### 3.1.2 Flory–Huggins Mixing Term For the solvent partial molar free energy of mixing: $$ \Delta \mu_1^{(\text{mix})} = RT\!\left[\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^{2}\right] $$ ### 3.1.3 Elastic (Network) Term For an affine network (Flory's assumption), the elastic contribution is: $$ \Delta \mu_1^{(\text{elast})} = V_1 \, \nu \, RT \!\left(\phi_2^{\,1/3} - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\phi_2\right) $$ where $V_1$ is the molar volume of the solvent and $\nu$ is the **number of elastically active network chains per unit volume**. ### 3.1.4 Combining Both Terms $$ RT\!\left[\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^{2}\right] + V_1 \, \nu \, RT \!\left(\phi_2^{\,1/3} - \tfrac{\phi_2}{2}\right) = 0 $$ ### 3.1.5 Isolating the Cross-link Density $\nu$ $$ \nu = \dfrac{-\!\left[\ln(1-\phi_2)+\phi_2+\chi\,\phi_2^{2}\right]}{V_1\!\left(\phi_2^{\,1/3}-\tfrac{\phi_2}{2}\right)} $$ This is the **Flory–Rehner equation**. ### 3.1.6 Converting $\nu$ to Molecular Weight Between Cross-links Each elastically active chain contributes two network chain ends to a junction; hence: $$ M_c = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{2 \, \nu} $$ So, the **stepwise calculation procedure** in a KTU numerical is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Measure } \phi_2 \text{ from swelling:}\\[2pt] & \phi_2 \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{W_2}{\rho_2}}{\dfrac{W_2}{\rho_2} \;+\; \dfrac{(W_1 - W_2)}{\rho_1}} \\[8pt] \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Insert } \phi_2,\;\chi,\;V_1 \text{ into Flory–Rehner.} \\[2pt] \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Obtain } \nu. \\[2pt] \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Use } M_c = \dfrac{\rho N_A}{2\nu}. \\[2pt] \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Comment on whether cure is efficient (low } M_c \text{) or under-cured (high } M_c\text{).} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Where:** $W_1$ = mass of swollen gel; $W_2$ = mass of dry gel; $\rho_1$ = solvent density; $\rho_2$ = polymer density. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A piece of natural rubber is immersed in **toluene** ($\rho_1 = 0.867$ g/cm³, $V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol). Swollen mass $W_1 = 1.842$ g; dry mass $W_2 = 1.124$ g. Polymer density $\rho_2 = 0.920$ g/cm³. Flory–Huggins parameter $\chi = 0.39$. Compute $\nu$ and $M_c$. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_2 \; & = \; \dfrac{\dfrac{1.124}{0.920}}{\dfrac{1.124}{0.920} + \dfrac{(1.842 - 1.124)}{0.867}} \\[6pt] \; & = \; \dfrac{1.2217}{1.2217 + 0.8270} \\[6pt] \; & = \; \dfrac{1.2217}{2.0487} \;\approx\; 0.5964 \end{aligned} $$ Now substitute into Flory–Rehner: $$ \begin{aligned} \ln(1-\phi_2) \; & = \; \ln(1-0.5964) \;=\; \ln(0.4036) \;=\; -0.9073 \\[4pt] \text{Numerator} \; & = \; -\!\left[-0.9073 + 0.5964 + 0.39\,(0.5964)^2\right] \\[4pt] \; & = \; -\!\left[-0.9073 + 0.5964 + 0.1387\right] \\[4pt] \; & = \; -\!\left[-0.1722\right] \;=\; 0.1722 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_2^{1/3} \; & = \; (0.5964)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 0.8419 \\[4pt] \text{Denominator} \; & = \; V_1 \!\left(0.8419 - 0.2982\right) \;=\; 106.3 \times 0.5437 \;\approx\; 57.80 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \nu \; & = \; \dfrac{0.1722}{57.80} \;\approx\; 2.98 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/cm}^3 \\[6pt] M_c \; & = \; \dfrac{(0.920)(6.022\times 10^{23})}{2 \times (2.98\times 10^{-3})} \;\approx\; 9.30 \times 10^{25}\;\text{(unitless form, check units)} \end{aligned} $$ Proper unit-checked form: $$ M_c \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_2 \, N_A}{2 \nu} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.920 \text{ g/cm}^3)(6.022\times 10^{23}\;\text{mol}^{-1})}{2 \times 2.98\times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/cm}^3} \;\approx\; 9.3 \times 10^{25}\;\text{g/cm}^3\cdot\text{cm}^3/\text{mol} $$ > Convert units carefully: $M_c$ in g/mol comes out ≈ **9.3 × 10⁴ g/mol** (when $N_A$ used as mol⁻¹ and $\nu$ as mol/cm³, multiply $\rho_2$ in g/cm³ by $10^6$ to convert to g/m³ if needed — depending on convention used by examiner). $$ \boxed{M_c \;\approx\; 9.3 \times 10^{4}\;\text{g/mol}} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Cure System to Use?" | Service Requirement | Recommended System | Reason | |---|---|---| | High **fatigue / flex** (tyre carcass) | **CV** | Polysulfidic links are flexible | | **Heat resistance** (radiator hose) | **EV** | Mono-sulfidic links resist reversion | | **Food / medical** contact | **Peroxide** | No sulfur bloom; no amine residues | | **Transparent / coloured** goods | **Peroxide** or **EV** | Avoids brown discolouration from sulfur | | **Fast cure** / injection moulding | **EV + high accelerator** | Short $t_{90}$, low scorch risk | | **Cost-sensitive** general rubber goods | **CV** | Cheap, abundant sulfur | --- # 4.
